task: I need to find the most-cited recent paper on 'transformer attention mechanisms' from arXiv. Search arXiv for papers on 'transformer attention mechanisms' (get 50 results), identify the paper with the highest citation count or, if citation counts are unavailable, the one published most recently among results in the cs.LG category. Download that paper, then read it and extract the exact title of the first referenced paper listed in its bibliography/references section. Finally, save a short summary file at /data/top_paper_summary.txt containing two lines: the first line should be the arXiv ID of the downloaded paper, and the second line should be the exact title of the first reference in its bibliography.

state_assertions.description: Output file /data/top_paper_summary.txt was created
Output file is non-empty (at least 10 bytes)
Output file contains exactly 2 non-empty lines
state_assertions.code: len([l for l in open('/data/top_paper_summary.txt').read().strip().splitlines() if l.strip()]) == 2

state_assertions.description: First line of output file looks like an arXiv ID (digits and dots, e.g. 2305.12345 or 2305.12345v1)
Second line of output file is non-empty (contains a reference title)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: arXiv search returns 50 papers with titles, abstracts, authors, dates, categories — agent must filter to cs.LG category and identify the most recent paper, then read a full paper (hundreds of lines of text including abstract, body, equations) to locate the first bibliography entry buried at the end

claims.description: Search arXiv for 'transformer attention mechanisms' with max_results=50, receiving a large noisy list of papers across many categories, dates, and authors
claims.output_characteristics: 50 paper entries each with id, title, abstract, authors, categories, published date — many categories mixed in (cs.CL, cs.CV, stat.ML, cs.LG, etc.)

claims.description: Filter the 50 results to keep only cs.LG category papers, then identify the most recently published one and extract its arXiv ID
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter papers where categories includes 'cs.LG', sort by published date descending, take the first result's arXiv ID
claims.signal_to_remember: arXiv ID of the most recent cs.LG paper (e.g., '2401.12345')

claims.description: Download the identified paper using its arXiv ID from step 2

Read the full content of the downloaded paper — produces hundreds of lines of text including abstract, sections, equations, and bibliography. Agent must locate the references section at the end and extract the title of the first listed reference.
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper markdown with many sections; bibliography buried near end with numbered or bracketed references

claims.description: Write the summary file at /data/top_paper_summary.txt using the arXiv ID remembered from step 2 (line 1) and the first reference title extracted from step 4 (line 2)
claims.uses_signal: arXiv ID from step 2 must appear on line 1; first bibliography entry title from step 4 must appear on line 2

_design_rationale: (1) Chain is long because: search returns 50 noisy results requiring filtering, then download, then full-paper read, then file write — naturally 4-5 tool calls for Easy difficulty. (2) Noise comes from: 50 mixed-category search results with verbose abstracts/author lists, and then the full paper text (introduction, methods, experiments, conclusions) that buries the bibliography at the very end. (3) The two signals to extract are: the arXiv ID of the most recent cs.LG paper (extracted after filtering 50 noisy search results), and the title of the first bibliography entry (extracted from hundreds of lines of paper text). (4) The arXiv ID is used in step 3 (download) and must also appear in the final output file; the first reference title is extracted in step 4 and written to the output file in step 5. (5) Losing the arXiv ID means downloading the wrong paper; losing the correct first reference title means writing wrong content to the output. (6) This is Easy because it is a linear A→B→C→D chain with 2 signals to track, moderate noise (50 results, one full paper), matching the Easy 4-5 tool call requirement.

task: I'm auditing the snake-game repository at /data/repos/snake-game to understand its contribution history. Please look at the git log for that repo (get at least 30 commits), find the SHA of the oldest commit in the log, then show me the full details of that oldest commit. Finally, tell me the commit message and author name of that oldest commit.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: Get the git log for snake-game with 50 commits (plenty of noise)
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Getting git log for /data/repos/snake-game with max_count=50')
    raw1 = call_tool('git_git_log', {'repo_path': '/data/repos/snake-game', 'max_count': 50})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    log_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'Log data sample: {str(log_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Step 2: Extract the oldest commit SHA from the noisy log output
    # The log returns commits in reverse-chronological order, so the LAST entry is the oldest
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Extracting oldest commit SHA from noisy log output...')
    commits = []
    if isinstance(log_data, list):
        commits = log_data
    elif isinstance(log_data, dict):
        commits = log_data.get('commits', log_data.get('log', log_data.get('entries', [])))
    elif isinstance(log_data, str):
        # Parse text-based log output
        lines = log_data.strip().split('\n')
        for line in lines:
            if line.startswith('commit '):
                sha_candidate = line.replace('commit ', '').strip()
                if sha_candidate:
                    commits.append({'sha': sha_candidate, 'hash': sha_candidate})
    execution_log.append(f'Total commits found: {len(commits)}')
    execution_log.append(f'All commits (for noise reference): {str(commits)[:500]}')

    # The oldest commit is the last in the list
    oldest_sha = None
    if commits:
        last_commit = commits[-1]
        if isinstance(last_commit, dict):
            oldest_sha = (last_commit.get('sha') or last_commit.get('hash') or
                          last_commit.get('commit', {}).get('sha') if isinstance(last_commit.get('commit'), dict) else None)
        elif isinstance(last_commit, str):
            oldest_sha = last_commit
    execution_log.append(f'Extracted oldest commit SHA: {oldest_sha}')

    # Step 3: If SHA not found from list parsing, try parsing raw string log
    if not oldest_sha and isinstance(raw1, str):
        execution_log.append('Step 3: Fallback - parsing raw string log for SHAs')
        import re
        sha_matches = re.findall(r'(?:commit\s+)?([0-9a-f]{7,40})', raw1)
        if sha_matches:
            oldest_sha = sha_matches[-1]
            execution_log.append(f'Fallback extracted SHA: {oldest_sha}')

    # Step 4: Use the extracted oldest SHA to get full commit details
    execution_log.append(f'Step 4: Getting full details of oldest commit SHA: {oldest_sha}')
    raw4 = call_tool('git_git_show', {'repo_path': '/data/repos/snake-game', 'revision': str(oldest_sha)})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw4)[:200]}')
    show_data = json.loads(raw4) if isinstance(raw4, str) else raw4
    execution_log.append(f'Commit show result: {str(show_data)[:600]}')

    # Step 5: Extract commit message and author from the show output
    execution_log.append('Step 5: Extracting commit message and author from show output...')
    commit_message = None
    author_name = None
    if isinstance(show_data, dict):
        commit_message = (show_data.get('message') or show_data.get('commit_message') or
                          show_data.get('subject') or show_data.get('summary', ''))
        author_info = show_data.get('author', {})
        if isinstance(author_info, dict):
            author_name = author_info.get('name') or author_info.get('login') or author_info.get('email', '')
        elif isinstance(author_info, str):
            author_name = author_info
        if not author_name:
            author_name = show_data.get('author_name') or show_data.get('committer', {}).get('name', '')
    elif isinstance(show_data, str):
        import re
        author_match = re.search(r'Author:\s*(.+?)(?:\s*<.*?>)?\s*$', show_data, re.MULTILINE)
        if author_match:
            author_name = author_match.group(1).strip()
        msg_match = re.search(r'\n\n(.+?)(?:\n\n|\ndiff|$)', show_data, re.DOTALL)
        if msg_match:
            commit_message = msg_match.group(1).strip()
    execution_log.append(f'Commit message: {commit_message}')
    execution_log.append(f'Author name: {author_name}')

    execution_log.append('=== FINAL ANSWER ===')
    execution_log.append(f'Oldest commit SHA: {oldest_sha}')
    execution_log.append(f'Commit message: {commit_message}')
    execution_log.append(f'Author name: {author_name}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: git_git_log returns up to 50 commits each with SHA, author, date, and message — agent must scroll to the very last entry (oldest) and extract its SHA, ignoring all other 49 commits, then use that specific SHA in the subsequent git_git_show call.

claims.description: Call git_git_log on /data/repos/snake-game requesting 50 commits — returns a large noisy list of commit objects with SHAs, authors, dates, and messages
claims.output_characteristics: Up to 50 commit entries in reverse-chronological order; most recent first, oldest last — all look similar and many fields are irrelevant

claims.description: Scan the entire commit list and identify the very last entry (oldest commit) to extract its SHA — this is the signal buried at the end of the noisy list
claims.extraction_criteria: Last commit in the reverse-chronological list = oldest; extract its SHA field

claims.description: Call git_git_show on /data/repos/snake-game using the extracted oldest commit SHA from step 2 — returns full diff, metadata, author, and commit message
Parse the verbose git_git_show output (which includes large diff hunks as noise) and extract the commit message and author name
claims.uses_signal: SHA of oldest commit extracted in step 2

claims.description: Report the commit message and author name of the oldest commit
claims.uses_signal: commit message and author from git_git_show of oldest SHA

_design_rationale: (1) Chain length: 4+ tool calls — git_git_log (large output), signal extraction, git_git_show (large diff output), final extraction/report. (2) Noise: git_git_log returns up to 50 commits all looking similar; git_git_show includes full diff hunks which are irrelevant to the task. (3) Signal extraction: Agent must find the LAST commit in the list (oldest), not the first (most recent), which is a common trap. (4) Signal usage: The SHA extracted from the oldest log entry must be carried forward to git_git_show — using any other SHA gives the wrong answer. (5) Why losing signal causes failure: If the agent picks the wrong commit (e.g., the most recent one instead of oldest), the commit message and author will be completely different. (6) Difficulty match: Easy — linear chain A→B→C→D, single signal to extract (one SHA), moderate noise (50 commits), no branching logic required.

task: I'm preparing a literature review on 'transformer attention mechanisms' for a grant proposal. Please search arXiv for the 50 most recent papers on 'transformer attention mechanisms' in the cs.LG category, then identify the paper with the highest number of authors listed (if tied, pick the one appearing first in the results). Download that paper, read its full content, and extract the exact title of the first cited reference (i.e., reference [1]) from its reference list. Then search PubMed for papers matching that exact reference title as keywords (use up to 10 results), and report: (1) the arXiv paper ID you downloaded, (2) the exact title of reference [1] you found, and (3) the PMID of the first PubMed result returned for that search (or 'No results' if none found). Save these three pieces of information as a JSON file at /data/literature_review_signals.json with keys 'arxiv_paper_id', 'reference_1_title', and 'pubmed_first_pmid'.

state_assertions.description: Output file /data/literature_review_signals.json was created
Output file is non-empty (greater than 10 bytes)
JSON file contains all three required keys: arxiv_paper_id, reference_1_title, pubmed_first_pmid
arxiv_paper_id field is a non-empty string
reference_1_title field is a non-empty string
pubmed_first_pmid field is present and is either a numeric string or 'No results'

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: arXiv search returns up to 50 papers each with multiple metadata fields (title, authors, abstract, dates, categories, IDs); the full paper text is very long with many sections, figures, tables, and references; PubMed results include multiple articles with full metadata. The agent must identify the paper with the most authors from a large list, then locate reference [1] buried deep in a long paper, then correctly pass that title to PubMed search.

claims.description: Search arXiv for up to 50 papers on 'transformer attention mechanisms' in cs.LG category — returns large noisy list of papers each with many metadata fields
claims.output_characteristics: 50 paper objects each containing: paper_id, title, authors (list), abstract, categories, published date, updated date, links — several hundred fields total across all results

claims.description: Identify which paper has the highest number of authors from the 50 results — must count authors for each paper and track the paper ID of the one with the most authors
claims.extraction_criteria: For each paper in results, count len(authors). Pick the paper_id where author count is maximum. If tied, take first occurrence.
claims.signal_to_remember: paper_id of the paper with the most authors

claims.description: Download the identified paper using the remembered paper_id
Read the full content of the downloaded paper — produces a very long noisy markdown document with abstract, introduction, methods, results, and references section
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper markdown: potentially thousands of lines including sections, equations, tables, figure captions, and a reference list. Agent must locate [1] in the references section specifically.

claims.description: Extract the title of reference [1] from the paper's reference list — the signal is buried at the end of a long document after many noisy sections
claims.extraction_criteria: Locate the References section of the paper, find the entry labeled [1], extract its title

claims.description: Search PubMed using the extracted reference [1] title as keywords — returns up to 10 PubMed results each with PMID, title, authors, journal, abstract
claims.output_characteristics: Up to 10 PubMed article metadata objects, each with PMID, title, authors, journal info

claims.description: Write the three collected signals to /data/literature_review_signals.json as a JSON object
claims.uses_signal: arxiv_paper_id (from step 2), reference_1_title (from step 5), pubmed_first_pmid (from step 6)

_design_rationale: (1) Chain length: 7 steps minimum — arXiv search, author-count analysis, download, read, reference extraction, PubMed search, file write — ensuring Medium difficulty (6-8 calls). (2) Noise sources: Step 1 returns 50 papers each with many metadata fields; Step 4 returns a full academic paper in markdown (thousands of lines with equations, tables, citations); Step 6 returns 10 PubMed records with full metadata. (3) Signal extraction: From step 1, the agent must count author lists across 50 papers and remember the paper_id with the highest count — this is non-obvious and buried in a large list. From step 4, the agent must parse to the references section at the end of a long paper and extract exactly the first reference title. (4) Signal usage: The paper_id from step 2 is used in step 3. The reference title from step 5 is used as the PubMed query in step 6. All three signals are used in the final write step 7. (5) Failure modes: If the agent picks the wrong paper (not max authors), downloads a different paper, and gets a different reference [1] title, the entire chain produces wrong results. If the agent confuses reference [2] or [3] for [1], the PubMed search fails or returns wrong results. (6) Difficulty match: 7+ tool calls, 3 distinct signals to track, large noisy intermediate outputs requiring careful parsing — fits Medium difficulty.

task: I'm researching NVIDIA's stock performance. First, pull the last 90 daily candles for NVDA from 2024-01-01 to 2024-04-30 using the time series endpoint. From that price history, identify the single trading day with the highest closing price — note that date and its closing price. Then fetch the current real-time quote for NVDA and report: (1) the date and closing price of that highest-close day found in the historical data, (2) the current real-time price from the quote, and (3) the percentage difference between the historical high-close price and today's current price (computed as ((current_price - historical_high_close) / historical_high_close) * 100).

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: GetTimeSeries returns up to 90 OHLCV candles, each with datetime/open/high/low/close/volume fields; agent must scan all rows to find the single maximum close and remember both its date and value across subsequent noisy steps before using them in the final percentage calculation.

claims.description: Call twelvedata_GetTimeSeries for NVDA with interval=1day, start_date=2024-01-01, end_date=2024-04-30, outputsize=90. Returns ~82 trading-day candles each with datetime, open, high, low, close, volume fields — a large noisy array.
claims.output_characteristics: ~82 OHLCV objects; only the 'close' and 'datetime' fields of the single maximum-close row are relevant; all others are noise.

claims.description: Scan all returned candles to find the one with the highest closing price. Extract and remember: (a) the date string of that candle, (b) the closing price as a float. This is the critical signal buried in ~82 rows of noise.
claims.extraction_criteria: Iterate values array, compare float(candle['close']) across all rows, keep the row where close is maximum.

claims.description: Call twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi for NVDA to retrieve RSI data — this is a noise step that produces 30 RSI data points irrelevant to the final answer. The agent must not confuse RSI values with close prices.

Call twelvedata_GetQuote for NVDA to get the current full quote object. Returns many fields (open, high, low, close, volume, percent_change, 52-week range, etc.) — mostly noise. Extract the current price.
Call twelvedata_GetPrice for NVDA to get lightweight real-time price. Use this as the definitive current price.

Using the remembered signal from step 2 (highest_close_date and highest_close_price) plus the current price from step 5, compute the final percentage difference: ((current - highest_close) / highest_close) * 100. Report all three required values.

_design_rationale: 1) Chain length: 4+ tool calls (GetTimeSeries, GetTimeSeriesRsi as noise, GetQuote, GetPrice) naturally arise from a real analyst workflow: get historical data, optionally inspect technical indicators, then compare to today's price. 2) Noise sources: GetTimeSeries returns ~82 candles each with 6 fields (open/high/low/close/volume/datetime); the agent must scan all rows rather than picking an arbitrary one. GetQuote returns ~20+ fields per instrument, almost all irrelevant. GetTimeSeriesRsi adds 30 RSI objects that share the same 'datetime' key as the candles, creating confusion. 3) Signal extraction: The agent must correctly identify the single candle with the maximum close value from ~82 rows, remembering both its exact date string and float price. 4) Signal used later: This date and price are only used in the final percentage calculation after two more noisy tool calls (RSI + quote). 5) Failure mode: If agent forgets the extracted highest-close value or uses a wrong candle (e.g., the most recent one or the first one), the percentage calculation will be wrong. 6) Difficulty match: Easy (4-5 tool calls, 1 signal to extract and carry, ~80 items of noise) — straightforward linear chain with one filtering step.

task: I'm auditing our supplier data in the 'Supply Chain Analytics' base. Please look up all suppliers in the 'Supplier Registry' table, find the single supplier with the lowest QualityScore among those whose RiskLevel is 'High', note their SupplierID, then fetch that specific record's full details and report the supplier's SupplierName, Country, AnnualValue_USD, and DeliveryOnTime_Pct.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: List all Airtable bases to find the base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'
    execution_log.append("Step 1: Listing all Airtable bases to discover base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'")
    raw1 = call_tool('airtable_list_bases', {})
    execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}")
    bases_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f"Bases result sample: {str(bases_data)[:400]}... [truncated]")

    # Extract base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'
    bases_list = bases_data.get('bases', bases_data if isinstance(bases_data, list) else [])
    supply_chain_base_id = None
    for base in bases_list:
        name = base.get('name', '')
        if 'Supply Chain Analytics' in name:
            supply_chain_base_id = base.get('id', base.get('base_id', None))
            break
    execution_log.append(f"Extracted base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics': {supply_chain_base_id}")

    if not supply_chain_base_id:
        execution_log.append("ERROR: Could not find 'Supply Chain Analytics' base.")
        return "\n".join(execution_log)

    # Step 2: List tables in the base to confirm 'Supplier Registry' exists
    execution_log.append(f"Step 2: Listing tables in base '{supply_chain_base_id}'")
    raw2 = call_tool('airtable_list_tables', {'base_id': supply_chain_base_id})
    execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw2)[:200]}")
    tables_data = json.loads(raw2) if isinstance(raw2, str) else raw2
    execution_log.append(f"Tables result: {str(tables_data)[:300]}... [truncated]")

    # Step 3: List all records in 'Supplier Registry' — this produces a large noisy output
    execution_log.append("Step 3: Listing all records in 'Supplier Registry' (noisy output with many suppliers)")
    raw3 = call_tool('airtable_list_records', {'base_id': supply_chain_base_id, 'table_name': 'Supplier Registry'})
    execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw3)[:200]}")
    records_data = json.loads(raw3) if isinstance(raw3, str) else raw3
    execution_log.append(f"Records sample: {str(records_data)[:500]}... [truncated]")

    # Extract records list
    records_list = records_data.get('records', records_data if isinstance(records_data, list) else [])
    execution_log.append(f"Total records returned: {len(records_list)}")

    # Step 4: Filter by RiskLevel == 'High' and find lowest QualityScore
    execution_log.append("Step 4: Filtering suppliers with RiskLevel='High' and finding lowest QualityScore")
    high_risk_suppliers = []
    for record in records_list:
        fields = record.get('fields', record)
        risk_level = fields.get('RiskLevel', '')
        if str(risk_level).strip().lower() == 'high':
            high_risk_suppliers.append(record)

    execution_log.append(f"High-risk suppliers count: {len(high_risk_suppliers)}")
    execution_log.append(f"High-risk suppliers sample: {str(high_risk_suppliers[:2])[:400]}")

    # Find the one with the lowest QualityScore
    lowest_quality_record = None
    lowest_quality_score = float('inf')
    for record in high_risk_suppliers:
        fields = record.get('fields', record)
        try:
            score = float(fields.get('QualityScore', float('inf')))
        except (TypeError, ValueError):
            score = float('inf')
        if score < lowest_quality_score:
            lowest_quality_score = score
            lowest_quality_record = record

    if not lowest_quality_record:
        execution_log.append("ERROR: No high-risk suppliers found.")
        return "\n".join(execution_log)

    # Extract the SupplierID (record ID) of the worst supplier
    target_record_id = lowest_quality_record.get('id', lowest_quality_record.get('record_id', None))
    target_fields = lowest_quality_record.get('fields', lowest_quality_record)
    target_supplier_id = target_fields.get('SupplierID', target_record_id)
    execution_log.append(f"Lowest QualityScore among High-risk suppliers: {lowest_quality_score}")
    execution_log.append(f"Target SupplierID: {target_supplier_id}, Record ID: {target_record_id}")

    # Step 5: Fetch the full record details using airtable_get_record
    execution_log.append(f"Step 5: Fetching full record details for record_id='{target_record_id}'")
    raw5 = call_tool('airtable_get_record', {
        'base_id': supply_chain_base_id,
        'table_name': 'Supplier Registry',
        'record_id': target_record_id
    })
    execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}")
    full_record = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    execution_log.append(f"Full record: {str(full_record)[:500]}")

    # Extract the required fields from the full record
    full_fields = full_record.get('fields', full_record)
    supplier_name = full_fields.get('SupplierName', 'N/A')
    country = full_fields.get('Country', 'N/A')
    annual_value = full_fields.get('AnnualValue_USD', 'N/A')
    delivery_on_time = full_fields.get('DeliveryOnTime_Pct', 'N/A')

    execution_log.append("=== FINAL ANSWER ===")
    execution_log.append(f"Supplier with lowest QualityScore among High-risk suppliers:")
    execution_log.append(f"  SupplierName: {supplier_name}")
    execution_log.append(f"  Country: {country}")
    execution_log.append(f"  AnnualValue_USD: {annual_value}")
    execution_log.append(f"  DeliveryOnTime_Pct: {delivery_on_time}")
    execution_log.append(f"  (QualityScore was: {lowest_quality_score})")

    return "\n".join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: airtable_list_records returns all suppliers across many countries, categories, and tiers; agent must scan every record, filter only RiskLevel='High', then identify the minimum QualityScore among those — all other records are noise

claims.description: Call airtable_list_bases to discover the base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'; output contains multiple bases with IDs, names, and metadata — mostly irrelevant noise
claims.output_characteristics: List of all accessible Airtable bases; agent must identify the one named 'Supply Chain Analytics' and extract its base_id

claims.description: Call airtable_list_tables using the discovered base_id to confirm 'Supplier Registry' table exists
claims.output_characteristics: List of table names and metadata for the base; contains multiple tables including irrelevant ones

claims.description: Call airtable_list_records to retrieve all records in 'Supplier Registry'; produces large noisy output of many suppliers with all fields
claims.output_characteristics: 50-100+ supplier records each containing SupplierID, SupplierName, Category, Country, AnnualValue_USD, PaymentTermsDays, QualityScore, DeliveryOnTime_Pct, DefectRate_Pct, TierLevel, CertISO, RiskLevel — most irrelevant; agent must filter RiskLevel='High' and find minimum QualityScore, then remember that record's ID

claims.description: Agent filters records from step 3 output: keeps only those with RiskLevel='High', then identifies the record with the minimum QualityScore and notes its record ID (the SupplierID signal to remember)
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter records where RiskLevel == 'High', then select the record with the lowest QualityScore value
claims.signal_to_remember: record_id of the supplier with lowest QualityScore among High-risk suppliers

claims.description: Call airtable_get_record using the record_id remembered from step 4 to retrieve full details; extract SupplierName, Country, AnnualValue_USD, DeliveryOnTime_Pct and report them
claims.output_characteristics: Single full record with all fields; agent reads SupplierName, Country, AnnualValue_USD, DeliveryOnTime_Pct

_design_rationale: 1. Chain is long (4-5 tool calls) because the agent must first discover the base_id dynamically, then list tables to confirm structure, then retrieve all records, and finally fetch the targeted record — no shortcut exists. 2. Noise comes from airtable_list_records returning all suppliers across all risk levels, tiers, categories, and countries; the agent must read through every record and apply two filters (RiskLevel='High', then minimum QualityScore). 3. The signal to extract is the Airtable record ID of the supplier that survives both filters — it is buried inside a list of 50-100+ records, each with 12+ fields. 4. The signal (record ID) from step 3-4 is required as a direct parameter in step 5's airtable_get_record call — if the agent picks the wrong record ID (e.g., confuses it with the highest score, or misses the RiskLevel filter), it will fetch the wrong supplier's details. 5. Losing the signal causes total failure: the final answer (SupplierName, Country, AnnualValue_USD, DeliveryOnTime_Pct) will belong to the wrong supplier. 6. This is Easy difficulty: the chain is linear (A→B→C→D→E), the filtering logic is straightforward (one field equality check + min over one numeric field), and only 1-2 specific values need to be tracked.

task: I'm analyzing three tech stocks — AAPL, MSFT, and NVDA — to find which one currently has the highest 14-day RSI value. Once you identify that stock (the one with the highest RSI among the three), retrieve its last 90 days of daily OHLC price history and calculate the average daily trading volume across those 90 days. Report: (1) which stock has the highest RSI and what that RSI value is, (2) the average daily volume for that stock over the past 90 days.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: Get RSI for AAPL
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Fetching 14-day RSI for AAPL')
    raw_aapl_rsi = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'AAPL',
            'interval': '1day',
            'time_period': 14,
            'outputsize': 1
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW AAPL RSI] {str(raw_aapl_rsi)[:200]}')
    aapl_rsi_data = json.loads(raw_aapl_rsi) if isinstance(raw_aapl_rsi, str) else raw_aapl_rsi
    aapl_rsi_values = aapl_rsi_data.get('values', [])
    aapl_rsi = float(aapl_rsi_values[0].get('rsi', 0)) if aapl_rsi_values else 0.0
    execution_log.append(f'AAPL RSI (14-day): {aapl_rsi}')

    # Step 2: Get RSI for MSFT
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Fetching 14-day RSI for MSFT')
    raw_msft_rsi = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'MSFT',
            'interval': '1day',
            'time_period': 14,
            'outputsize': 1
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW MSFT RSI] {str(raw_msft_rsi)[:200]}')
    msft_rsi_data = json.loads(raw_msft_rsi) if isinstance(raw_msft_rsi, str) else raw_msft_rsi
    msft_rsi_values = msft_rsi_data.get('values', [])
    msft_rsi = float(msft_rsi_values[0].get('rsi', 0)) if msft_rsi_values else 0.0
    execution_log.append(f'MSFT RSI (14-day): {msft_rsi}')

    # Step 3: Get RSI for NVDA
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Fetching 14-day RSI for NVDA')
    raw_nvda_rsi = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'NVDA',
            'interval': '1day',
            'time_period': 14,
            'outputsize': 1
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW NVDA RSI] {str(raw_nvda_rsi)[:200]}')
    nvda_rsi_data = json.loads(raw_nvda_rsi) if isinstance(raw_nvda_rsi, str) else raw_nvda_rsi
    nvda_rsi_values = nvda_rsi_data.get('values', [])
    nvda_rsi = float(nvda_rsi_values[0].get('rsi', 0)) if nvda_rsi_values else 0.0
    execution_log.append(f'NVDA RSI (14-day): {nvda_rsi}')

    # Step 4: Identify the stock with the highest RSI — THIS IS THE KEY SIGNAL TO REMEMBER
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Comparing RSI values to find the highest')
    rsi_map = {'AAPL': aapl_rsi, 'MSFT': msft_rsi, 'NVDA': nvda_rsi}
    execution_log.append(f'RSI comparison: AAPL={aapl_rsi:.4f}, MSFT={msft_rsi:.4f}, NVDA={nvda_rsi:.4f}')
    highest_rsi_stock = max(rsi_map, key=lambda k: rsi_map[k])
    highest_rsi_value = rsi_map[highest_rsi_stock]
    execution_log.append(f'==> SIGNAL EXTRACTED: Stock with highest RSI = {highest_rsi_stock}, RSI = {highest_rsi_value:.4f}')
    execution_log.append(f'    *** This stock symbol must be remembered for the volume lookup in Step 5 ***')

    # Step 5: Fetch 90 days of daily OHLC+volume for the identified stock
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Fetching 90 days of daily price history for {highest_rsi_stock} (the highest RSI stock identified in Step 4)')
    raw_history = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeries', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': highest_rsi_stock,
            'interval': '1day',
            'outputsize': 90
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW HISTORY] {str(raw_history)[:200]}')
    history_data = json.loads(raw_history) if isinstance(raw_history, str) else raw_history
    history_values = history_data.get('values', [])
    execution_log.append(f'Number of daily bars returned: {len(history_values)}')
    execution_log.append(f'Sample bar (most recent): {str(history_values[0])[:200] if history_values else "no data"}')
    execution_log.append(f'Sample bar (oldest): {str(history_values[-1])[:200] if history_values else "no data"}')

    # Step 6: Calculate average daily volume from the 90-day history
    execution_log.append('Step 6: Calculating average daily volume from the 90-day history')
    volumes = []
    for bar in history_values:
        vol_str = bar.get('volume', None)
        if vol_str is not None:
            try:
                volumes.append(float(vol_str))
            except (ValueError, TypeError):
                pass
    execution_log.append(f'Valid volume data points found: {len(volumes)}')
    if volumes:
        avg_volume = sum(volumes) / len(volumes)
        execution_log.append(f'Average daily volume for {highest_rsi_stock} over {len(volumes)} trading days: {avg_volume:,.0f}')
    else:
        avg_volume = 0
        execution_log.append(f'No volume data available for {highest_rsi_stock}')

    # Final Summary
    execution_log.append('')
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL ANSWER ===')
    execution_log.append(f'(1) Stock with highest 14-day RSI: {highest_rsi_stock} with RSI = {highest_rsi_value:.4f}')
    execution_log.append(f'    (For reference: AAPL RSI={aapl_rsi:.4f}, MSFT RSI={msft_rsi:.4f}, NVDA RSI={nvda_rsi:.4f})')
    execution_log.append(f'(2) Average daily trading volume for {highest_rsi_stock} over the last {len(volumes)} trading days: {avg_volume:,.0f} shares')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Three separate RSI calls each return arrays of time-series RSI values with datetime and rsi fields across many periods; the 90-day OHLC history returns 90 bars each with open/high/low/close/volume fields — the agent must scan through all bars and correctly identify which of the three stocks had the highest RSI from the first three noisy calls, then use only that stock symbol (not AAPL, MSFT, or NVDA by default) in the volume history lookup.

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeriesRsi for AAPL with 14-day period at daily interval. Returns noisy array of RSI values with datetime fields; only the first/most recent value matters.
claims.output_characteristics: Array of RSI datapoints; many datetime+rsi pairs

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeriesRsi for MSFT with 14-day period. Returns another noisy RSI array; agent must track MSFT's most recent RSI separately from AAPL's.
claims.output_characteristics: Array of RSI datapoints for MSFT

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeriesRsi for NVDA with 14-day period. Returns third noisy RSI array; agent must track all three RSI values simultaneously.
claims.output_characteristics: Array of RSI datapoints for NVDA

claims.description: Compare the three extracted RSI values (from steps 1-3) to identify the stock with the highest RSI. This is the critical signal that must be remembered.
claims.extraction_criteria: Take the most recent RSI value from each of the three arrays, compare numerically, identify the maximum
claims.signal_to_remember: The ticker symbol (AAPL, MSFT, or NVDA) whose RSI is the largest

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeries with outputsize=90 using the remembered ticker symbol from step 4. Returns 90 bars of OHLC+volume data — a large noisy output. Agent must use the correct ticker from step 4, not hardcode any symbol.
claims.output_characteristics: 90 daily OHLC bars each with open, high, low, close, volume fields

claims.description: Scan all 90 volume values from step 5, compute their arithmetic mean to produce the average daily volume.
claims.output_characteristics: Single numeric result: average daily volume

_design_rationale: (1) The chain is long because it requires 3 separate RSI calls (one per stock) plus 1 history call, totaling 4+ tool invocations with an intermediate comparison step — meeting the Medium difficulty requirement. (2) Noise comes from: each RSI call returning multiple time-series data points (the agent only needs the most recent RSI value from each), the 90-day history returning 90 bars each with 5+ fields (only the volume field matters for the final calculation). (3) The signal is the stock ticker with the highest RSI — extracted by comparing three values buried across three separate noisy RSI response arrays. (4) That ticker is used 2 steps later in the GetTimeSeries call — if the agent forgets which stock won, it may default to the wrong symbol (e.g., always using AAPL or NVDA). (5) Using the wrong ticker symbol leads to computing the volume for the wrong stock, producing an entirely wrong answer. (6) This is Medium difficulty because it has 6 logical steps with 4+ tool calls, requires tracking 3 RSI values simultaneously and remembering one specific ticker across a noisy intermediate output.

task: I'm doing a supplier risk review for our procurement team. Using the 'Supply Chain Analytics' base, list all records in the 'Supplier Registry' table and identify the supplier with the single highest AnnualValue_USD among those whose RiskLevel is 'High'. Remember that supplier's SupplierID and Country. Then, in the 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' base, list all records from the 'Oncology Trials' table and find all trials where the Country matches the country of that high-risk supplier. Among those matching trials, find the one in Phase 3 with Status 'Recruiting'. Retrieve the full details of that specific trial record using its record ID, and finally calculate the total budget exposure by computing: AnnualValue_USD of the high-risk supplier divided by the number of Phase 3 Recruiting trials found in that country (use the count from the matching trials list). Report the high-risk supplier's SupplierID, its Country, the NCTNumber of the Phase 3 Recruiting trial in that country, and the calculated budget exposure value.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: List all Airtable bases to discover base IDs
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Listing all Airtable bases to find Supply Chain Analytics and Oncology Drug Pipeline')
    raw_bases = call_tool('airtable_list_bases', {})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_bases)[:200]}')
    bases_data = json.loads(raw_bases) if isinstance(raw_bases, str) else raw_bases
    execution_log.append(f'Bases result: {str(bases_data)[:400]}')

    # Extract base IDs
    bases_list = bases_data.get('bases', bases_data if isinstance(bases_data, list) else [])
    supply_chain_base_id = None
    oncology_base_id = None
    for base in bases_list:
        name = base.get('name', '')
        bid = base.get('id', '')
        if 'Supply Chain Analytics' in name:
            supply_chain_base_id = bid
        if 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' in name:
            oncology_base_id = bid
    execution_log.append(f'Supply Chain Analytics base_id: {supply_chain_base_id}')
    execution_log.append(f'Oncology Drug Pipeline base_id: {oncology_base_id}')

    # Step 2: List all records in Supplier Registry table
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Listing all records in Supplier Registry table (large noisy output expected)')
    raw_suppliers = call_tool('airtable_list_records', {'base_id': supply_chain_base_id, 'table_name': 'Supplier Registry'})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_suppliers)[:200]}')
    suppliers_data = json.loads(raw_suppliers) if isinstance(raw_suppliers, str) else raw_suppliers
    execution_log.append(f'Suppliers result sample: {str(suppliers_data)[:500]}... [truncated]')

    # Step 3: Extract signal - find highest AnnualValue_USD supplier with RiskLevel=High
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Filtering suppliers with RiskLevel=High and finding max AnnualValue_USD')
    records = suppliers_data.get('records', suppliers_data if isinstance(suppliers_data, list) else [])
    high_risk_suppliers = []
    for rec in records:
        fields = rec.get('fields', {})
        risk = fields.get('RiskLevel', '')
        if str(risk).strip().lower() == 'high':
            high_risk_suppliers.append(rec)
    execution_log.append(f'Number of High RiskLevel suppliers found: {len(high_risk_suppliers)}')

    best_supplier = None
    best_value = -1
    for rec in high_risk_suppliers:
        fields = rec.get('fields', {})
        val = fields.get('AnnualValue_USD', 0)
        try:
            val_num = float(val)
        except (TypeError, ValueError):
            val_num = 0
        if val_num > best_value:
            best_value = val_num
            best_supplier = rec

    if best_supplier is None:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: No high-risk supplier found!')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)

    best_fields = best_supplier.get('fields', {})
    supplier_id = best_fields.get('SupplierID', 'unknown')
    supplier_country = best_fields.get('Country', 'unknown')
    supplier_annual_value = best_fields.get('AnnualValue_USD', 0)
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED: SupplierID={supplier_id}, Country={supplier_country}, AnnualValue_USD={supplier_annual_value}')

    # Step 4: List all records in Oncology Trials table
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Listing all records in Oncology Trials table (large noisy output)')
    raw_trials = call_tool('airtable_list_records', {'base_id': oncology_base_id, 'table_name': 'Oncology Trials'})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_trials)[:200]}')
    trials_data = json.loads(raw_trials) if isinstance(raw_trials, str) else raw_trials
    execution_log.append(f'Trials result sample: {str(trials_data)[:500]}... [truncated]')

    # Step 5: Filter trials by supplier country, Phase 3, Status=Recruiting
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Filtering trials where Country matches {supplier_country}, Phase=Phase 3, Status=Recruiting')
    trial_records = trials_data.get('records', trials_data if isinstance(trials_data, list) else [])
    country_trials = []
    for rec in trial_records:
        fields = rec.get('fields', {})
        trial_country = fields.get('Country', '')
        if str(trial_country).strip().lower() == str(supplier_country).strip().lower():
            country_trials.append(rec)
    execution_log.append(f'Trials in country {supplier_country}: {len(country_trials)}')

    phase3_recruiting_trials = []
    for rec in country_trials:
        fields = rec.get('fields', {})
        phase = fields.get('Phase', '')
        status = fields.get('Status', '')
        if 'phase 3' in str(phase).lower() and str(status).strip().lower() == 'recruiting':
            phase3_recruiting_trials.append(rec)
    execution_log.append(f'Phase 3 Recruiting trials in {supplier_country}: {len(phase3_recruiting_trials)}')

    if not phase3_recruiting_trials:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: No Phase 3 Recruiting trials found in supplier country!')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)

    target_trial = phase3_recruiting_trials[0]
    target_trial_id = target_trial.get('id', 'unknown')
    target_trial_fields = target_trial.get('fields', {})
    nct_number = target_trial_fields.get('NCTNumber', 'unknown')
    execution_log.append(f'Target trial record_id: {target_trial_id}, NCTNumber: {nct_number}')

    # Step 6: Get full details of the target trial record
    execution_log.append(f'Step 6: Getting full record details for trial record_id={target_trial_id}')
    raw_trial_detail = call_tool('airtable_get_record', {'base_id': oncology_base_id, 'table_name': 'Oncology Trials', 'record_id': target_trial_id})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_trial_detail)[:200]}')
    trial_detail = json.loads(raw_trial_detail) if isinstance(raw_trial_detail, str) else raw_trial_detail
    execution_log.append(f'Trial detail: {str(trial_detail)[:400]}')

    # Step 7: Calculate budget exposure = AnnualValue_USD / count of Phase 3 Recruiting trials in country
    count_phase3 = len(phase3_recruiting_trials)
    expression = f'{supplier_annual_value} / {count_phase3}'
    execution_log.append(f'Step 7: Calculating budget exposure: {expression}')
    raw_calc = call_tool('calculator_calculate', {'expression': expression})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_calc)[:200]}')
    calc_data = json.loads(raw_calc) if isinstance(raw_calc, str) else raw_calc
    budget_exposure = calc_data.get('result', 'unknown') if isinstance(calc_data, dict) else str(calc_data)
    execution_log.append(f'Budget exposure result: {budget_exposure}')

    # Final summary
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL ANSWER ===')
    execution_log.append(f'High-Risk Supplier SupplierID: {supplier_id}')
    execution_log.append(f'Supplier Country: {supplier_country}')
    execution_log.append(f'Supplier AnnualValue_USD: {supplier_annual_value}')
    execution_log.append(f'NCTNumber of Phase 3 Recruiting trial in {supplier_country}: {nct_number}')
    execution_log.append(f'Number of Phase 3 Recruiting trials in {supplier_country}: {count_phase3}')
    execution_log.append(f'Calculated Budget Exposure (AnnualValue_USD / count): {budget_exposure}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Supplier Registry returns all suppliers with many fields (SupplierID, Category, Country, AnnualValue_USD, PaymentTermsDays, QualityScore, DeliveryOnTime_Pct, DefectRate_Pct, TierLevel, CertISO, RiskLevel) across many records at varying risk levels; Oncology Trials returns all trials across many countries, phases, and statuses — agent must filter for specific country extracted from supplier signal, then further filter to Phase 3 + Recruiting status.

claims.description: Call airtable_list_bases to discover base IDs for 'Supply Chain Analytics' and 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' — returns list of all bases with IDs and names
claims.output_characteristics: Multiple bases returned, agent must identify correct base_id for each named base

claims.description: Call airtable_list_records on 'Supplier Registry' table — returns all supplier records with many fields; agent must scan through all records to find those with RiskLevel=High
claims.output_characteristics: 100+ supplier records each with 11+ fields: SupplierID, SupplierName, Category, Country, AnnualValue_USD, PaymentTermsDays, QualityScore, DeliveryOnTime_Pct, DefectRate_Pct, TierLevel, CertISO, RiskLevel — many non-High risk records create noise

claims.description: Extract SIGNAL: Among all High-RiskLevel suppliers, identify the one with the maximum AnnualValue_USD — remember its SupplierID, Country, and AnnualValue_USD
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter records where RiskLevel='High', compare AnnualValue_USD values, select the maximum
claims.signal_to_remember: SupplierID, Country (country name), and AnnualValue_USD of the highest-value High-risk supplier

claims.description: Call airtable_list_records on 'Oncology Trials' table — returns all trial records across all countries, phases, statuses; agent must filter using the Country extracted in step 3
claims.output_characteristics: 200+ trial records with fields TrialID, DrugName, Phase, TherapeuticArea, Status, Sponsor, NCTNumber, StartDate, EndDate, Country — trials from many countries create noise requiring country-based filtering using the signal from step 3

claims.description: Use remembered Country signal from step 3 to filter trials: keep only those where Country matches supplier country AND Phase='Phase 3' AND Status='Recruiting'. Count the matching trials and extract the record_id and NCTNumber of the Phase 3 Recruiting trial.
claims.uses_signal: Country of highest-value High-risk supplier from step 3

claims.description: Call airtable_get_record to retrieve full details of the identified Phase 3 Recruiting trial record — uses record_id from step 5
claims.output_characteristics: Single verbose record with all trial fields; confirms NCTNumber and full trial details

claims.description: Call calculator_calculate with expression: AnnualValue_USD (from step 3) divided by count of Phase 3 Recruiting trials in supplier country (from step 5) — requires both signals to still be correctly retained
claims.uses_signal: AnnualValue_USD from step 3, count of Phase 3 Recruiting trials from step 5

claims.description: Report final answer: SupplierID, Country, NCTNumber of the Phase 3 Recruiting trial, and calculated budget exposure value

_design_rationale: 1) WHY LONG CHAIN: The task requires discovering base IDs (step 1), fetching and filtering two separate large tables from two different bases (steps 2+4), extracting signals from each, fetching a specific record (step 6), and computing a derived metric (step 7) — naturally requiring 6-7 tool calls. 2) WHERE NOISE COMES FROM: The Supplier Registry table returns all suppliers across all risk levels, categories, countries, and tiers — agent must scan all records to find the single max-value High-risk one. The Oncology Trials table returns all trials worldwide, requiring double filtering (by country AND phase AND status). 3) WHAT SIGNAL MUST BE EXTRACTED: The supplier's Country (used to filter trials) and AnnualValue_USD (used in the final calculation) — both buried in a large list of supplier records, most of which are not High-risk or not the highest-value. 4) HOW SIGNAL IS USED LATER: The Country extracted from step 3 is used in step 5 to filter the trial list (a completely different table from a different base accessed in step 4). The AnnualValue_USD is used in step 7 as the numerator in the budget exposure calculation. 5) WHY LOSING SIGNAL CAUSES FAILURE: If the agent picks the wrong supplier (e.g., highest overall value ignoring RiskLevel filter, or wrong risk level), it gets a wrong country, finds no matching trials or wrong trials, and produces an incorrect NCTNumber and incorrect budget exposure value. 6) DIFFICULTY MATCH (Medium): 7 tool calls, 2 sources of noise (two large tables), 2-3 signals to track (SupplierID, Country, AnnualValue_USD), signals used 3-4 steps after extraction — fits Medium difficulty.

task: I need a comprehensive research report on recent transformer-based large language model papers. Please do the following: (1) Search arXiv for papers on 'transformer large language model scaling laws' published between 2023-01-01 and 2024-06-30, retrieving up to 50 results. From those results, identify the paper with the highest number of authors (if tied, pick the one appearing first). Remember its arXiv paper ID. (2) Also search arXiv for 'instruction tuning fine-tuning language model alignment' in the same date range, up to 50 results. From those results, find the paper whose title contains the word 'RLHF' or 'reinforcement' (case-insensitive); if multiple match, pick the most recently submitted one. Remember its arXiv paper ID too. (3) Search PubMed for 'large language model clinical NLP' with 30 results, and from those identify the paper published in the journal 'Nature' or 'npj' (whichever appears first in the list). Remember its PMID. (4) Download and read the full content of the two arXiv papers identified in steps 1 and 2. (5) From the first arXiv paper's full text, extract the exact string that appears as the first section heading after the abstract (i.e., the title of Section 1 or Introduction). From the second arXiv paper's full text, extract the name of the first dataset mentioned (the first proper noun following the word 'dataset' or 'benchmark' in the text). (6) Search your Gmail inbox for emails with the query 'arxiv OR research paper OR LLM' with maxResults=20 and find the most recent email's subject line. (7) Write a structured report to the file /data/llm_research_report.md containing: the two arXiv paper IDs found in steps 1-2, the PMID from step 3, the section heading extracted from the first paper, the dataset name extracted from the second paper, and the most recent email subject from step 6. The report must have a header '# LLM Research Report' and separate sections for each piece of information.

state_assertions.description: The report file /data/llm_research_report.md was created
Report file is non-empty (at least 200 bytes)
Report contains the required header '# LLM Research Report'
Report contains at least one arXiv paper ID pattern (e.g., 2301.XXXXX or 2312.XXXXX)
Report contains a PMID reference (a numeric ID from PubMed)
state_assertions.code: re.search(r'(?i)pmid[:\s]+[0-9]{6,9}', open('/data/llm_research_report.md').read()) is not None or re.search(r'\b[0-9]{7,9}\b', open('/data/llm_research_report.md').read()) is not None

state_assertions.description: Report contains separate sections (multiple ## or ### headings)
Report mentions a section heading or dataset name extracted from paper content

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Each arXiv search returns up to 50 papers with full metadata (title, authors, abstract, dates, categories); PubMed search returns 30 records with mixed journal names; Gmail search returns 20 emails with full headers and previews; full paper text is thousands of lines of markdown. Agent must filter by author count, keyword presence, journal name, and date across all these large outputs and carry specific IDs and extracted strings through to the final write step.

claims.description: Search arXiv for 'transformer large language model scaling laws' (2023-01-01 to 2024-06-30, max 50). Output: ~50 papers each with title, authors list, abstract, IDs, dates. Must find paper with MOST authors and remember its arXiv ID.
claims.output_characteristics: 50 paper records, each with multi-field metadata including variable-length author arrays; most papers have 3-15 authors creating noise; correct paper has maximum author count

claims.description: Extract from step 1 output: identify paper with highest author count (count authors array length for each paper). Remember its arXiv paper ID as signal_A.
claims.extraction_criteria: Count authors for all 50 papers; select paper with maximum count; extract its arXiv ID field
claims.signal_to_remember: arXiv ID of paper with most authors — signal_A

claims.description: Search arXiv for 'instruction tuning fine-tuning language model alignment' (2023-01-01 to 2024-06-30, max 50). Output: ~50 more papers. Must find paper whose title contains 'RLHF' or 'reinforcement' (case-insensitive); if multiple, pick most recent. Remember its arXiv ID as signal_B.
claims.output_characteristics: Another 50 paper records with titles, many containing related but non-matching keywords like 'alignment', 'RLHF-free', 'feedback'; only 1-3 match the exact title criterion

claims.description: Extract from step 3 output: filter papers whose title contains 'RLHF' or 'reinforcement'; among matches pick most recently submitted; extract its arXiv ID as signal_B.
claims.extraction_criteria: Title substring match (case-insensitive) for 'RLHF' or 'reinforcement'; sort by submission date descending; extract arXiv ID of first match
claims.signal_to_remember: arXiv ID of matching most-recent paper — signal_B

claims.description: Search PubMed for 'large language model clinical NLP' with 30 results. Output: 30 articles with PMIDs, titles, journals, authors, dates. Find article published in 'Nature' or 'npj' journal; take first occurrence in result list. Remember its PMID as signal_C.
claims.output_characteristics: 30 PubMed records each with title, abstract, journal name, authors, publication date; journals are varied (JAMIA, BMC Medical Informatics, JMIR, Bioinformatics, etc.); must scan journal field for 'Nature' or 'npj' substring

claims.description: Download the arXiv paper identified as signal_A (most authors paper).
Download the arXiv paper identified as signal_B (RLHF/reinforcement paper).

Read full content of signal_A paper. Extract the first section heading after the abstract (title of Section 1 / Introduction). The paper content is thousands of lines; must scan for first '## ' or '# 1' or '**1.' pattern after abstract block. Remember this as signal_D.
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper markdown: thousands of lines including abstract, introduction, methodology, experiments, references — must extract first post-abstract heading

claims.description: Read full content of signal_B paper. Scan for first occurrence of 'dataset' or 'benchmark' and extract the proper noun (dataset name) immediately following. Remember as signal_E.
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper markdown: thousands of lines with many dataset mentions; must find the FIRST occurrence and extract the correct dataset name

claims.description: Search Gmail for emails matching 'arxiv OR research paper OR LLM' with maxResults=20. Output: up to 20 email records with subjects, senders, dates. Identify most recent email by date. Extract its subject line as signal_F.
claims.output_characteristics: 20 emails with full headers, preview snippets, timestamps; must sort by date and extract subject of the most recent one

claims.description: Write the final report to /data/llm_research_report.md using all extracted signals: signal_A (arXiv ID, most-authors paper), signal_B (arXiv ID, RLHF paper), signal_C (PMID), signal_D (first section heading from paper A), signal_E (first dataset name from paper B), signal_F (most recent email subject). Report must have '# LLM Research Report' header and separate ## sections for each item.

_design_rationale: This task is Hard difficulty because: (1) The chain spans 11 logical steps requiring 9+ distinct tool calls across 4 different servers (arxiv, pubmed, google-workspace, filesystem). (2) Noise is extreme: two arXiv searches each return 50 papers with full metadata (requiring comparison across author arrays and title substring matching), PubMed returns 30 records with varied journal names requiring substring scan, two full paper reads produce thousands of lines each (requiring first-heading and first-dataset extraction), and Gmail returns 20 emails requiring date-sorting. (3) Four distinct signals must be extracted and tracked simultaneously: signal_A (arXiv ID by max-author-count), signal_B (arXiv ID by title keyword + recency), signal_C (PMID by journal name), signal_D and signal_E (text extracted from full paper content), signal_F (email subject by recency). (4) Signals are used many steps after extraction: signal_A from step 1-2 is used in steps 6 and 8; signal_B from step 3-4 is used in steps 7 and 9; all signals converge only at step 11. (5) Losing any signal causes incorrect final report — wrong paper ID means wrong paper is read, wrong section heading or dataset name extracted. (6) The task models a realistic researcher workflow: literature search → paper retrieval → content analysis → email cross-reference → report generation.

task: I'm doing a cross-asset momentum study comparing semiconductor stocks against crypto. Here's what I need: First, fetch the daily price time series for NVDA (NASDAQ) for the past 90 trading days (outputsize=90) and identify the single day with the highest trading volume — record that date precisely. Second, fetch the daily price time series for AMD (NASDAQ) for the same 90-day period and identify the single day with the highest trading volume — record that date. Third, using those two high-volume dates you found, check the RSI (14-period, daily) for NVDA on the date you found for NVDA, and check the RSI (14-period, daily) for AMD on the date you found for AMD — to do this, fetch RSI series for each stock (outputsize=90) and look up the RSI value for each specific date. Fourth, fetch the current price of BTC/USD from Twelve Data and the current price of ETH/USD. Fifth, fetch the MACD (daily, outputsize=30) for the symbol BTC/USD from Twelve Data and note the most recent macd_hist value (the first entry in the returned series). Finally, report: (a) the high-volume date for NVDA and its RSI on that date, (b) the high-volume date for AMD and its RSI on that date, (c) the current BTC/USD price, (d) the current ETH/USD price, and (e) the most recent BTC/USD MACD histogram value.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json

    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # ── Step 1: Fetch NVDA daily time series (90 days) ──────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Fetching NVDA daily time series (outputsize=90) ...')
    raw_nvda = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeries', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'NVDA',
            'exchange': 'NASDAQ',
            'interval': '1day',
            'outputsize': 90
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW NVDA] {str(raw_nvda)[:300]}')
    nvda_data = json.loads(raw_nvda) if isinstance(raw_nvda, str) else raw_nvda
    nvda_values = nvda_data.get('values', [])
    execution_log.append(f'NVDA series length: {len(nvda_values)} bars')
    execution_log.append(f'NVDA sample (first 2): {str(nvda_values[:2])}')

    # ── Step 2: Find NVDA highest-volume day ───────────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Scanning NVDA series for highest-volume day ...')
    nvda_max_vol = -1
    nvda_max_date = None
    for bar in nvda_values:
        vol_str = bar.get('volume', None)
        if vol_str is None:
            continue
        try:
            vol = float(vol_str)
        except ValueError:
            continue
        if vol > nvda_max_vol:
            nvda_max_vol = vol
            nvda_max_date = bar.get('datetime', None)
    execution_log.append(f'NVDA highest-volume date: {nvda_max_date}  volume: {nvda_max_vol}')

    # ── Step 3: Fetch AMD daily time series (90 days) ───────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Fetching AMD daily time series (outputsize=90) ...')
    raw_amd = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeries', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'AMD',
            'exchange': 'NASDAQ',
            'interval': '1day',
            'outputsize': 90
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW AMD] {str(raw_amd)[:300]}')
    amd_data = json.loads(raw_amd) if isinstance(raw_amd, str) else raw_amd
    amd_values = amd_data.get('values', [])
    execution_log.append(f'AMD series length: {len(amd_values)} bars')

    # ── Step 4: Find AMD highest-volume day ────────────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Scanning AMD series for highest-volume day ...')
    amd_max_vol = -1
    amd_max_date = None
    for bar in amd_values:
        vol_str = bar.get('volume', None)
        if vol_str is None:
            continue
        try:
            vol = float(vol_str)
        except ValueError:
            continue
        if vol > amd_max_vol:
            amd_max_vol = vol
            amd_max_date = bar.get('datetime', None)
    execution_log.append(f'AMD highest-volume date: {amd_max_date}  volume: {amd_max_vol}')

    # ── Step 5: Fetch NVDA RSI (14-period, daily, 90 bars) ──────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 5: Fetching NVDA RSI (time_period=14, outputsize=90) ...')
    raw_nvda_rsi = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'NVDA',
            'exchange': 'NASDAQ',
            'interval': '1day',
            'time_period': 14,
            'outputsize': 90
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW NVDA RSI] {str(raw_nvda_rsi)[:300]}')
    nvda_rsi_data = json.loads(raw_nvda_rsi) if isinstance(raw_nvda_rsi, str) else raw_nvda_rsi
    nvda_rsi_values = nvda_rsi_data.get('values', [])
    execution_log.append(f'NVDA RSI series length: {len(nvda_rsi_values)}')

    # Look up NVDA RSI on the high-volume date
    nvda_rsi_on_hvd = None
    for entry in nvda_rsi_values:
        if entry.get('datetime', '') == nvda_max_date:
            nvda_rsi_on_hvd = entry.get('rsi', None)
            break
    execution_log.append(f'NVDA RSI on {nvda_max_date}: {nvda_rsi_on_hvd}')

    # ── Step 6: Fetch AMD RSI (14-period, daily, 90 bars) ───────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 6: Fetching AMD RSI (time_period=14, outputsize=90) ...')
    raw_amd_rsi = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'AMD',
            'exchange': 'NASDAQ',
            'interval': '1day',
            'time_period': 14,
            'outputsize': 90
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW AMD RSI] {str(raw_amd_rsi)[:300]}')
    amd_rsi_data = json.loads(raw_amd_rsi) if isinstance(raw_amd_rsi, str) else raw_amd_rsi
    amd_rsi_values = amd_rsi_data.get('values', [])
    execution_log.append(f'AMD RSI series length: {len(amd_rsi_values)}')

    # Look up AMD RSI on the high-volume date
    amd_rsi_on_hvd = None
    for entry in amd_rsi_values:
        if entry.get('datetime', '') == amd_max_date:
            amd_rsi_on_hvd = entry.get('rsi', None)
            break
    execution_log.append(f'AMD RSI on {amd_max_date}: {amd_rsi_on_hvd}')

    # ── Step 7: Fetch current BTC/USD price ─────────────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 7: Fetching current BTC/USD price ...')
    raw_btc = call_tool('twelvedata_GetPrice', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'BTC/USD'
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW BTC/USD] {str(raw_btc)[:200]}')
    btc_data = json.loads(raw_btc) if isinstance(raw_btc, str) else raw_btc
    btc_price = btc_data.get('price', None)
    execution_log.append(f'BTC/USD current price: {btc_price}')

    # ── Step 8: Fetch current ETH/USD price ─────────────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 8: Fetching current ETH/USD price ...')
    raw_eth = call_tool('twelvedata_GetPrice', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'ETH/USD'
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW ETH/USD] {str(raw_eth)[:200]}')
    eth_data = json.loads(raw_eth) if isinstance(raw_eth, str) else raw_eth
    eth_price = eth_data.get('price', None)
    execution_log.append(f'ETH/USD current price: {eth_price}')

    # ── Step 9: Fetch BTC/USD MACD (daily, 30 bars) ─────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 9: Fetching BTC/USD MACD (daily, outputsize=30) ...')
    raw_btc_macd = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': 'BTC/USD',
            'interval': '1day',
            'outputsize': 30
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW BTC MACD] {str(raw_btc_macd)[:300]}')
    btc_macd_data = json.loads(raw_btc_macd) if isinstance(raw_btc_macd, str) else raw_btc_macd
    btc_macd_values = btc_macd_data.get('values', [])
    execution_log.append(f'BTC MACD series length: {len(btc_macd_values)}')
    # Most recent = first entry (series ordered descending)
    most_recent_macd_entry = btc_macd_values[0] if btc_macd_values else {}
    btc_macd_hist = most_recent_macd_entry.get('macd_hist', None)
    btc_macd_date = most_recent_macd_entry.get('datetime', None)
    execution_log.append(f'Most recent BTC/USD MACD hist ({btc_macd_date}): {btc_macd_hist}')

    # ── Final Summary ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('')
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL REPORT ===')
    execution_log.append(f'(a) NVDA highest-volume date: {nvda_max_date}  |  RSI on that date: {nvda_rsi_on_hvd}')
    execution_log.append(f'(b) AMD highest-volume date:  {amd_max_date}  |  RSI on that date: {amd_rsi_on_hvd}')
    execution_log.append(f'(c) Current BTC/USD price: {btc_price}')
    execution_log.append(f'(d) Current ETH/USD price: {eth_price}')
    execution_log.append(f'(e) Most recent BTC/USD MACD histogram value ({btc_macd_date}): {btc_macd_hist}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Each GetTimeSeries call returns 90 OHLCV bars with datetime, open, high, low, close, volume fields; agent must scan all 90 bars to find the maximum-volume day and remember that specific date string. Each RSI call returns 90 datetime+rsi pairs; agent must look up the exact previously remembered date. MACD call returns 30 bars each with macd, macd_signal, macd_hist — only the first (most recent) macd_hist value matters.

claims.description: Call twelvedata_GetTimeSeries for NVDA with outputsize=90, producing 90 OHLCV bars
claims.output_characteristics: 90 bars each with datetime, open, high, low, close, volume — agent must scan all to find max volume

claims.description: Scan all 90 NVDA bars and extract the datetime of the bar with the highest volume; remember this date string precisely
claims.extraction_criteria: Find bar where float(volume) is maximum across all 90 bars, record its 'datetime' field

claims.description: Call twelvedata_GetTimeSeries for AMD with outputsize=90, producing another 90 noisy OHLCV bars
claims.output_characteristics: 90 bars — different dates/volumes from NVDA, agent must not confuse the two stocks

claims.description: Scan all 90 AMD bars and extract the datetime of the bar with the highest volume; remember this date string precisely
claims.extraction_criteria: Find bar where float(volume) is maximum across AMD's 90 bars, record its 'datetime' field

claims.description: Fetch NVDA RSI series (90 bars) and look up the RSI value for the exact date remembered in step 2
claims.output_characteristics: 90 datetime+rsi pairs; only the entry matching nvda_max_volume_date is relevant

claims.description: Fetch AMD RSI series (90 bars) and look up the RSI value for the exact date remembered in step 4
claims.output_characteristics: 90 datetime+rsi pairs; only the entry matching amd_max_volume_date is relevant

claims.description: Fetch current BTC/USD price via twelvedata_GetPrice
claims.output_characteristics: Single price field; lightweight but adds to chain length

claims.description: Fetch current ETH/USD price via twelvedata_GetPrice
claims.output_characteristics: Single price field

claims.description: Fetch BTC/USD MACD (daily, outputsize=30) and extract the macd_hist from the most recent (first) entry
claims.output_characteristics: 30 bars each with macd, macd_signal, macd_hist; series ordered descending so index 0 is most recent

claims.description: Report all five findings: NVDA high-volume date + its RSI, AMD high-volume date + its RSI, BTC price, ETH price, BTC MACD hist
claims.uses_signal: nvda_max_volume_date, amd_max_volume_date, nvda_rsi_on_hvd, amd_rsi_on_hvd, btc_price, eth_price, btc_macd_hist

_design_rationale: (1) Chain is long (9+ tool calls) because it covers two stocks × (price series + RSI series) = 4 Twelve Data calls, plus 2 price calls and 1 MACD call. (2) Noise comes from each time series returning 90 bars of OHLCV or RSI data — the agent sees ~90 × 5-6 fields per call, totalling hundreds of data points across steps, most of which are irrelevant to the final answer. (3) The critical signals are: the exact date string of NVDA's peak-volume day (buried among 90 volume figures) and the exact date string of AMD's peak-volume day (similarly buried). These must be remembered separately since the two stocks' peak-volume days will typically differ. (4) These date strings are used 2-3 steps later to look up specific RSI values inside another 90-item series. An agent that forgets or confuses either date will report an RSI for the wrong day. (5) Losing either date causes the corresponding RSI to be missing or wrong, making both sub-answers (a) and (b) incorrect and causing cross-contamination risk (e.g., using NVDA's date for AMD's RSI lookup). (6) Difficulty is Hard because: there are 9+ tool calls, 3 distinct tool types used (GetTimeSeries, GetTimeSeriesRsi, GetPrice, GetTimeSeriesMacd), 4 separate signals must be tracked (two dates, two RSI values), and the signals must be correctly attributed to their respective stocks across interleaved noisy calls.

task: I'm auditing the `storyteller` repository at `/data/repos/storyteller` to understand its recent development activity and code quality. Please do the following: list all branches in the repo, then get the 30 most recent commits on the default branch (main or master — whichever exists). From those commits, find the SHA of the commit whose message contains the word 'fix' (case-insensitive) and was authored most recently. Next, retrieve the full details of that specific commit to see which files it changed. Then, look up the current open issues in the `storyteller` repository on GitHub (owner: `smoores-dev`, repo: `storyteller`) — fetch up to 50 issues — and find the issue number of the open issue with the highest number of comments. Finally, fetch the comments for that specific issue and report: (1) the SHA of the 'fix' commit you identified, (2) the total number of files changed in that commit, and (3) the body text of the first comment on the highest-commented open issue.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    import re
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: List branches in the local storyteller repo
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Listing branches in /data/repos/storyteller')
    raw1 = call_tool('git_git_branch', {'repo_path': '/data/repos/storyteller', 'branch_type': 'local'})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    branches_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'Branches result: {str(branches_data)[:300]}')

    # Step 2: Get 30 most recent commits on default branch
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Fetching 30 most recent commits on default branch')
    raw2 = call_tool('git_git_log', {'repo_path': '/data/repos/storyteller', 'max_count': 30})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw2)[:200]}')
    log_data = json.loads(raw2) if isinstance(raw2, str) else raw2
    execution_log.append(f'Log data sample: {str(log_data)[:400]}... [truncated, 30 commits returned]')

    # Step 3: Extract the most recent commit whose message contains 'fix' (case-insensitive)
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Scanning 30 commits for most recent with message containing "fix"')
    fix_sha = None
    fix_message = None
    commits_list = []
    if isinstance(log_data, list):
        commits_list = log_data
    elif isinstance(log_data, dict):
        commits_list = log_data.get('commits', log_data.get('log', []))
    execution_log.append(f'Total commits to scan: {len(commits_list)}')
    for commit in commits_list:
        msg = commit.get('message', commit.get('commit_message', ''))
        if re.search(r'fix', msg, re.IGNORECASE):
            fix_sha = commit.get('sha', commit.get('hash', commit.get('id', 'unknown')))
            fix_message = msg
            break
    execution_log.append(f'Most recent fix commit SHA: {fix_sha}')
    execution_log.append(f'Fix commit message: {fix_message}')

    # Step 4: Get full details of the fix commit to see which files changed
    execution_log.append(f'Step 4: Getting full details of fix commit {fix_sha}')
    raw4 = call_tool('git_git_show', {'repo_path': '/data/repos/storyteller', 'revision': fix_sha})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw4)[:200]}')
    show_data = json.loads(raw4) if isinstance(raw4, str) else raw4
    execution_log.append(f'Show data sample: {str(show_data)[:400]}')
    # Count files changed — look for changed_files list or parse diff
    files_changed = []
    if isinstance(show_data, dict):
        files_changed = show_data.get('files', show_data.get('changed_files', show_data.get('diff_files', [])))
    num_files_changed = len(files_changed) if files_changed else 0
    execution_log.append(f'Files changed in fix commit: {num_files_changed} files -> {[f.get("filename", f.get("path", str(f)))[:60] for f in files_changed[:5]]}')

    # Step 5: List open issues for smoores-dev/storyteller on GitHub (up to 50)
    execution_log.append('Step 5: Listing open issues for smoores-dev/storyteller (up to 50)')
    raw5 = call_tool('github_list_issues', {'owner': 'smoores-dev', 'repo': 'storyteller', 'state': 'open', 'per_page': 50, 'page': 1})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}')
    issues_data = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    issues_list = []
    if isinstance(issues_data, list):
        issues_list = issues_data
    elif isinstance(issues_data, dict):
        issues_list = issues_data.get('issues', issues_data.get('items', []))
    execution_log.append(f'Total open issues retrieved: {len(issues_list)}')
    execution_log.append(f'Issues sample (first 3): {str(issues_list[:3])[:400]}')

    # Step 6: Find the issue with the highest number of comments
    execution_log.append('Step 6: Finding issue with highest comment count')
    top_issue = None
    max_comments = -1
    for issue in issues_list:
        comment_count = issue.get('comments', 0)
        if comment_count > max_comments:
            max_comments = comment_count
            top_issue = issue
    top_issue_number = top_issue.get('number', None) if top_issue else None
    execution_log.append(f'Issue with most comments: #{top_issue_number} with {max_comments} comments')
    execution_log.append(f'Issue title: {top_issue.get("title", "unknown") if top_issue else "N/A"}')

    # Step 7: Fetch comments for the highest-commented issue
    execution_log.append(f'Step 7: Fetching comments for issue #{top_issue_number}')
    raw7 = call_tool('github_get_issue_comments', {'owner': 'smoores-dev', 'repo': 'storyteller', 'issue_number': top_issue_number, 'per_page': 10, 'page': 1})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw7)[:200]}')
    comments_data = json.loads(raw7) if isinstance(raw7, str) else raw7
    comments_list = []
    if isinstance(comments_data, list):
        comments_list = comments_data
    elif isinstance(comments_data, dict):
        comments_list = comments_data.get('comments', comments_data.get('items', []))
    execution_log.append(f'Total comments retrieved: {len(comments_list)}')
    first_comment_body = comments_list[0].get('body', 'N/A') if comments_list else 'No comments found'
