claims.extraction_criteria: Find park whose fullName contains 'Grand Canyon', extract its parkCode field

claims.description: Call national-parks_getParkDetails for each of the 3 park codes extracted in steps 2/4/6 — 3 separate calls producing large detailed objects
claims.output_characteristics: Large nested JSON per park with images, contacts, topics, activities, fees, hours, states, weatherInfo, directions — only latitude and longitude fields matter
claims.signal_to_remember: GPS coordinates for each park (lat, lon)

claims.description: Use GPS coordinates extracted from step 7 to call weather_get_current_weather for each of the 3 parks — 3 calls
claims.output_characteristics: Current weather object with many fields — must extract and compare temperature values

claims.description: Use the same GPS coordinates from step 7 to call weather_get_weather_forecast with days=7 for each park — 3 calls; analyze precipitation days to find best forecast
claims.uses_signal: GPS coordinates from park details (remembered across 5+ steps from step 7)
claims.output_characteristics: 7-day forecast with day/night periods, precipitation probabilities, conditions — must count precipitation days across all 14 periods per park

_design_rationale: (1) The chain is long because finding 3 parks requires 3 separate search calls, each producing 20 noisy results; then 3 detail calls; then 3 current weather calls; then 3 forecast calls — totaling 12 tool calls minimum. (2) Noise comes from: findParks returning 20 full park objects each with dozens of irrelevant fields; getParkDetails returning massive nested objects with images, contacts, activities, entrance fees, directions, and weather descriptions — all irrelevant noise except lat/lon. (3) The critical signals are: (a) parkCodes extracted from 3 separate noisy search results, (b) GPS coordinates extracted from 3 noisy detail responses. (4) The coordinates extracted in step 7 must be remembered and correctly used in both steps 8 AND 9, spanning across 3+ intermediate noisy tool calls. (5) If the agent uses wrong parkCodes, it gets wrong coordinates; wrong coordinates give wrong weather for a completely different location, making the comparison meaningless. (6) This is Hard because it requires 12+ tool calls, uses 3 different server types (national-parks, weather), requires tracking 6 distinct values (3 park codes, 3 GPS coordinate pairs) across the full chain, and involves comparing aggregated multi-day forecast data.

task: I'm preparing a literature review on transformer-based models for medical image segmentation. Please search arXiv for papers on 'transformer medical image segmentation' (retrieve up to 50 results), then identify the paper with the earliest submission date among those that have 'transformer' AND 'segmentation' in their title. Note that paper's arXiv ID. Next, search PubMed for 'transformer medical image segmentation' (retrieve 30 results) and get the full metadata for the PubMed article whose title most closely matches the arXiv paper you identified. Finally, write a summary report to /data/med_imaging_review.txt that contains: (1) the arXiv ID of the earliest-dated transformer+segmentation paper, (2) its full title and submission date, (3) the PMID of the matching PubMed article, and (4) the journal name and publication year from the PubMed metadata.

state_assertions.description: Output file /data/med_imaging_review.txt was created
Output file is non-empty (at least 200 bytes)
Report contains an arXiv ID pattern (e.g., digits and dots like 2301.12345)
Report contains a PMID (a sequence of 7-9 digits on its own)
Report contains a year (publication year from PubMed metadata)
Report contains the word 'segmentation' (confirming it addresses the right topic)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: arXiv returns up to 50 papers with full metadata (abstracts, authors, categories, dates) most of which do not have both 'transformer' AND 'segmentation' in their title; agent must filter and find the earliest submission date. PubMed returns 30 results with varying titles requiring fuzzy matching to the arXiv paper title. Both steps produce verbose, noisy outputs requiring careful filtering.

claims.description: Search arXiv for 'transformer medical image segmentation' with max_results=50, returns large noisy list of papers with full metadata
claims.output_characteristics: 50 papers each with arXiv ID, title, abstract, authors, categories, submission date — most titles do NOT contain both 'transformer' and 'segmentation'

claims.description: Filter the 50 arXiv results to only those with both 'transformer' AND 'segmentation' in their title, then find the one with the earliest submission date. Extract and remember its arXiv ID, full title, and submission date.
claims.extraction_criteria: Title must contain both 'transformer' (case-insensitive) and 'segmentation' (case-insensitive); among those, pick the paper with the earliest (minimum) submission date
claims.signal_to_remember: arXiv ID of earliest transformer+segmentation paper, its full title, and its submission date

claims.description: Search PubMed for 'transformer medical image segmentation' with num_results=30, returns a large noisy list of PubMed articles with PMIDs and titles
claims.output_characteristics: 30 PubMed article records each with PMID, title, authors, journal, year — titles vary widely and do not necessarily match the arXiv paper title directly

claims.description: Identify the PubMed article whose title most closely matches the arXiv paper title extracted in step 2, extract its PMID
claims.extraction_criteria: Among 30 PubMed titles, find the one with highest word overlap with the arXiv paper title from step 2
claims.signal_to_remember: PMID of the best-matching PubMed article

claims.description: Retrieve full metadata for the PubMed article identified in step 4 using its PMID
claims.output_characteristics: Verbose metadata object with journal name, publication year, authors, MeSH terms, abstract, DOI, and many other fields

claims.description: Extract journal name and publication year from PubMed metadata (step 5), combine with arXiv ID/title/date (step 2) and PMID (step 4), write full summary report to /data/med_imaging_review.txt
claims.uses_signal: arXiv ID + title + date from step 2; PMID from step 4; journal + year from step 5

_design_rationale: (1) Chain is long because it spans two different scientific databases (arXiv and PubMed), requires filtering within each, cross-referencing between them, and then writing a structured output — naturally requiring 6+ tool calls. (2) Noise comes from: arXiv returning 50 papers with verbose abstracts and metadata where most titles don't satisfy the dual keyword filter; PubMed returning 30 articles with varying titles requiring fuzzy title matching. (3) The critical signal is the arXiv ID + title extracted in step 2 (earliest paper with both keywords in title) — this is buried among 50 results and requires date comparison after filtering. The PMID from step 4 is secondary signal buried among 30 PubMed results. (4) The arXiv ID/title is used in step 4 to identify the matching PubMed article, and all signals converge in step 6 for the final report. (5) Losing the arXiv ID leads to wrong PMID lookup; losing the PMID leads to fetching wrong metadata; any error causes wrong content in the output file. (6) Medium difficulty: 6 tool calls, two noisy steps (50 + 30 items), two pieces of information to extract and track (arXiv ID/title, PMID), cross-source matching adds complexity without being a full 9+ step hard chain.

task: I'm auditing the snake-game repository at /data/repos/snake-game to understand its recent development history. Please do the following: look at the git log for the repository (fetch at least 30 commits), find the SHA of the oldest commit in that list, then show the full details of that oldest commit. From that commit's details, extract the author's email address. Finally, search GitHub for repositories owned by users matching that email domain (i.e., search for 'user:<author_name> language:python' using the commit author's name you found). Report the author's email address and the total number of public repositories found in the GitHub search.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: Get git log for snake-game with 30+ commits
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Fetching git log for /data/repos/snake-game (max 30 commits)')
    raw_log = call_tool('git_git_log', {'repo_path': '/data/repos/snake-game', 'max_count': 30})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_log)[:200]}')
    log_data = json.loads(raw_log) if isinstance(raw_log, str) else raw_log
    execution_log.append(f'Log data sample: {str(log_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Step 2: Extract the oldest commit SHA from the log
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Extracting oldest commit SHA from log output (last entry in list)')
    commits = []
    if isinstance(log_data, list):
        commits = log_data
    elif isinstance(log_data, dict):
        commits = log_data.get('commits', log_data.get('log', []))
    execution_log.append(f'Number of commits found: {len(commits)}')
    execution_log.append(f'All commit entries (first 3): {str(commits[:3])[:300]}')
    # The oldest commit is the last one in the list
    oldest_commit = commits[-1] if commits else {}
    oldest_sha = oldest_commit.get('hash', oldest_commit.get('sha', oldest_commit.get('commit', 'unknown')))
    execution_log.append(f'Oldest commit SHA extracted: {oldest_sha}')
    execution_log.append(f'Oldest commit full entry: {str(oldest_commit)[:300]}')

    # Step 3: Show full details of the oldest commit
    execution_log.append(f'Step 3: Fetching full details for oldest commit SHA: {oldest_sha}')
    raw_show = call_tool('git_git_show', {'repo_path': '/data/repos/snake-game', 'revision': oldest_sha})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_show)[:200]}')
    show_data = json.loads(raw_show) if isinstance(raw_show, str) else raw_show
    execution_log.append(f'Show data sample: {str(show_data)[:500]}... [truncated]')

    # Step 4: Extract author email from commit details
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Extracting author email from commit details')
    author_email = 'unknown'
    author_name = 'unknown'
    if isinstance(show_data, dict):
        author_info = show_data.get('author', {})
        if isinstance(author_info, dict):
            author_email = author_info.get('email', show_data.get('author_email', 'unknown'))
            author_name = author_info.get('name', show_data.get('author_name', 'unknown'))
        elif isinstance(author_info, str):
            author_email = author_info
        author_email = author_email or show_data.get('email', 'unknown')
        author_name = author_name or show_data.get('name', 'unknown')
    elif isinstance(show_data, str):
        import re
        email_match = re.search(r'Author:.*?<([^>]+)>', show_data)
        name_match = re.search(r'Author:\s*([^<\n]+)', show_data)
        if email_match:
            author_email = email_match.group(1).strip()
        if name_match:
            author_name = name_match.group(1).strip()
    execution_log.append(f'Extracted author email: {author_email}')
    execution_log.append(f'Extracted author name: {author_name}')

    # Step 5: Search GitHub repositories for that author
    search_query = f'user:{author_name} language:python'
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Searching GitHub repositories with query: "{search_query}"')
    raw_search = call_tool('github_search_repositories', {'query': search_query, 'per_page': 30})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_search)[:200]}')
    search_data = json.loads(raw_search) if isinstance(raw_search, str) else raw_search
    execution_log.append(f'Search data sample: {str(search_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Step 6: Extract total count of repositories found
    execution_log.append('Step 6: Extracting total repository count from search results')
    total_repos = 0
    if isinstance(search_data, dict):
        total_repos = search_data.get('total_count', search_data.get('totalCount', len(search_data.get('items', search_data.get('repositories', [])))))
    elif isinstance(search_data, list):
        total_repos = len(search_data)
    execution_log.append(f'Total repositories found: {total_repos}')

    execution_log.append('=== FINAL SUMMARY ===')
    execution_log.append(f'Oldest commit SHA: {oldest_sha}')
    execution_log.append(f'Author name: {author_name}')
    execution_log.append(f'Author email (signal extracted at step 4, used in step 5): {author_email}')
    execution_log.append(f'GitHub search query used: "{search_query}"')
    execution_log.append(f'Total public Python repositories found for author: {total_repos}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: git log produces 30 commit entries each with SHA, message, author, date fields; git show produces full diff output with many changed lines and metadata; GitHub search returns many repository entries with irrelevant metadata

claims.description: Fetch git log for /data/repos/snake-game requesting 30 commits — produces large noisy output with many commits, SHAs, messages, authors, dates
claims.output_characteristics: 30 commit entries each containing hash, message, author name, author email, date — all entries look similar, only the last (oldest) matters

claims.description: Identify and remember the SHA of the oldest (last) commit in the log — this is the signal buried at the bottom of 30 entries
claims.extraction_criteria: The oldest commit is the last entry in the returned commit list; extract its SHA/hash field

claims.description: Call git_git_show with the oldest commit SHA — produces full diff output (noisy) from which author email must be extracted
claims.output_characteristics: Full commit details including diff, potentially hundreds of lines of code changes, plus Author: Name <email> header

claims.description: Extract author email (and name) from the commit details — the signal is in the Author field, buried among diff noise
claims.extraction_criteria: Parse the Author field from git show output to get name and email address

claims.description: Use the remembered author name from step 4 to search GitHub for their Python repositories
claims.output_characteristics: List of repositories with stars, forks, descriptions — noisy but total_count is the needed value

_design_rationale: (1) Chain is long because: git_log → identify oldest SHA → git_show that SHA → extract email/name → github_search = 4 tool calls plus 2 extraction steps, naturally arising from a real code-audit workflow. (2) Noise comes from: 30 commit log entries all looking similar (hard to find the last one), and git show output with full diffs making it hard to spot the Author line. (3) Signal to extract: SHA of oldest commit (step 1→2), then author email/name from that commit (step 3→4). (4) Signal used in: step 5 GitHub search — wrong author name = wrong search results = wrong final answer. (5) Losing signal causes failure: if agent shows the wrong commit (not the oldest), they get a different author and the GitHub search returns different (or zero) results. (6) Difficulty match: Easy — linear chain A→B→C→D→E with moderate noise at each step and a clear extraction target (oldest commit's author email).

task: I'm doing a technical analysis review of three semiconductor stocks: NVDA, AMD, and INTC. First, fetch the latest quotes for all three stocks to identify which one has the highest current percent_change (positive or negative, use absolute value). Then, for that top-moving stock only, retrieve 90 days of daily RSI data (from 2024-07-01 to 2024-09-28, interval 1day) and count how many trading days in that period had an RSI value above 70 (overbought territory). Next, fetch 90 days of daily MACD data for that same top-moving stock (same date range) and count how many days the MACD line (macd field) was above the MACD signal line (macd_signal field). Finally, report: (1) which stock had the highest absolute percent_change and what that value was, (2) how many RSI-overbought days were found, and (3) how many days MACD was above its signal line.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: Fetch latest quotes for NVDA, AMD, INTC
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Fetching latest quote for NVDA')
    raw_nvda = call_tool('twelvedata_GetQuote', {'params': {'symbol': 'NVDA'}})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW NVDA] {str(raw_nvda)[:300]}')
    nvda_data = json.loads(raw_nvda) if isinstance(raw_nvda, str) else raw_nvda
    nvda_pct = nvda_data.get('percent_change', '0') if isinstance(nvda_data, dict) else '0'
    try:
        nvda_abs = abs(float(nvda_pct))
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        nvda_abs = 0.0
    execution_log.append(f'NVDA percent_change: {nvda_pct}, abs: {nvda_abs}')

    execution_log.append('Step 2: Fetching latest quote for AMD')
    raw_amd = call_tool('twelvedata_GetQuote', {'params': {'symbol': 'AMD'}})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW AMD] {str(raw_amd)[:300]}')
    amd_data = json.loads(raw_amd) if isinstance(raw_amd, str) else raw_amd
    amd_pct = amd_data.get('percent_change', '0') if isinstance(amd_data, dict) else '0'
    try:
        amd_abs = abs(float(amd_pct))
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        amd_abs = 0.0
    execution_log.append(f'AMD percent_change: {amd_pct}, abs: {amd_abs}')

    execution_log.append('Step 3: Fetching latest quote for INTC')
    raw_intc = call_tool('twelvedata_GetQuote', {'params': {'symbol': 'INTC'}})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW INTC] {str(raw_intc)[:300]}')
    intc_data = json.loads(raw_intc) if isinstance(raw_intc, str) else raw_intc
    intc_pct = intc_data.get('percent_change', '0') if isinstance(intc_data, dict) else '0'
    try:
        intc_abs = abs(float(intc_pct))
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        intc_abs = 0.0
    execution_log.append(f'INTC percent_change: {intc_pct}, abs: {intc_abs}')

    # Signal extraction: determine which stock has highest absolute percent_change
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Extracting signal - identifying top-moving stock by highest absolute percent_change')
    scores = {
        'NVDA': (nvda_abs, nvda_pct),
        'AMD': (amd_abs, amd_pct),
        'INTC': (intc_abs, intc_pct)
    }
    top_stock = max(scores, key=lambda s: scores[s][0])
    top_pct_value = scores[top_stock][1]
    top_abs_value = scores[top_stock][0]
    execution_log.append(f'TOP MOVING STOCK (signal extracted): {top_stock} with abs percent_change={top_abs_value} (raw={top_pct_value})')
    execution_log.append(f'IMPORTANT: Remembering top_stock={top_stock} for all subsequent steps')

    # Step 5: Fetch 90-day RSI for top_stock (2024-07-01 to 2024-09-28)
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Fetching daily RSI data for {top_stock} from 2024-07-01 to 2024-09-28')
    raw_rsi = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': top_stock,
            'interval': '1day',
            'start_date': '2024-07-01',
            'end_date': '2024-09-28',
            'time_period': 14,
            'outputsize': 100
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW RSI] {str(raw_rsi)[:400]}')
    rsi_data = json.loads(raw_rsi) if isinstance(raw_rsi, str) else raw_rsi
    rsi_values = rsi_data.get('values', []) if isinstance(rsi_data, dict) else []
    execution_log.append(f'RSI values count: {len(rsi_values)}')
    execution_log.append(f'Sample RSI values (first 5): {str(rsi_values[:5])}')

    # Count days with RSI > 70
    overbought_days = 0
    for entry in rsi_values:
        rsi_val = entry.get('rsi', None) if isinstance(entry, dict) else None
        if rsi_val is not None:
            try:
                if float(rsi_val) > 70:
                    overbought_days += 1
            except (TypeError, ValueError):
                pass
    execution_log.append(f'RSI overbought days (RSI > 70) for {top_stock}: {overbought_days}')

    # Step 6: Fetch 90-day MACD for top_stock (same date range) - using remembered signal top_stock
    execution_log.append(f'Step 6: Fetching daily MACD data for {top_stock} (using remembered signal) from 2024-07-01 to 2024-09-28')
    raw_macd = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd', {
        'params': {
            'symbol': top_stock,
            'interval': '1day',
            'start_date': '2024-07-01',
            'end_date': '2024-09-28',
            'outputsize': 100
        }
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW MACD] {str(raw_macd)[:400]}')
    macd_data = json.loads(raw_macd) if isinstance(raw_macd, str) else raw_macd
    macd_values = macd_data.get('values', []) if isinstance(macd_data, dict) else []
    execution_log.append(f'MACD values count: {len(macd_values)}')
    execution_log.append(f'Sample MACD values (first 5): {str(macd_values[:5])}')

    # Count days where macd > macd_signal
    macd_above_signal_days = 0
    for entry in macd_values:
        if isinstance(entry, dict):
            macd_line = entry.get('macd', None)
            macd_signal_line = entry.get('macd_signal', None)
            if macd_line is not None and macd_signal_line is not None:
                try:
                    if float(macd_line) > float(macd_signal_line):
                        macd_above_signal_days += 1
                except (TypeError, ValueError):
                    pass
    execution_log.append(f'MACD above signal days for {top_stock}: {macd_above_signal_days}')

    # Final summary
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL RESULTS ===')
    execution_log.append(f'(1) Top-moving stock: {top_stock} with absolute percent_change = {top_abs_value} (raw value: {top_pct_value}%)')
    execution_log.append(f'(2) RSI overbought days (RSI > 70) for {top_stock} between 2024-07-01 and 2024-09-28: {overbought_days}')
    execution_log.append(f'(3) Days MACD was above MACD signal line for {top_stock} between 2024-07-01 and 2024-09-28: {macd_above_signal_days}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Each GetQuote call returns ~25 fields per stock (open, high, low, close, volume, average_volume, fifty_two_week range, rolling changes, timestamps, etc.) across 3 stocks producing ~75 noisy fields total. RSI output contains 90 data points each with datetime+rsi value. MACD output contains 90 data points each with datetime+macd+macd_signal+macd_hist. The agent must track which stock had the highest absolute percent_change and only use that stock symbol for the RSI and MACD queries.

claims.description: Call GetQuote for NVDA - returns ~25 noisy fields including percent_change
claims.output_characteristics: Single quote object with ~25 fields: symbol, name, exchange, currency, datetime, timestamp, open, high, low, close, volume, previous_close, change, percent_change, average_volume, fifty_two_week (low/high/ranges), rolling changes, is_market_open, etc.

claims.description: Call GetQuote for AMD - returns same noisy structure
claims.output_characteristics: Same ~25 field structure as NVDA, agent must track AMD's percent_change alongside NVDA's

claims.description: Call GetQuote for INTC - returns same noisy structure
claims.output_characteristics: Same ~25 field structure, agent must now compare all three percent_change absolute values buried among many fields

claims.description: Extract signal: identify which of NVDA/AMD/INTC has highest absolute percent_change - this is the critical signal to remember
claims.extraction_criteria: Compare abs(percent_change) across all three quote results from steps 1-3, select the stock symbol with the maximum absolute value

claims.description: Using the remembered top_moving_stock_symbol, call GetTimeSeriesRsi for that stock only - produces 90 noisy RSI data points
claims.output_characteristics: Array of ~90 objects each with {datetime, rsi} covering 2024-07-01 to 2024-09-28. Agent must filter entries where rsi > 70 and count them.

claims.description: Using the SAME remembered top_moving_stock_symbol, call GetTimeSeriesMacd for that stock - produces 90 noisy MACD data points
claims.output_characteristics: Array of ~90 objects each with {datetime, macd, macd_signal, macd_hist} covering 2024-07-01 to 2024-09-28. Agent must count entries where macd > macd_signal.

claims.description: Report final answers: (1) top stock name + its percent_change value, (2) RSI overbought day count, (3) MACD above signal day count

_design_rationale: Chain is 6+ tool calls: 3 GetQuote calls (one per stock) + 1 RSI call + 1 MACD call = 5 required calls plus possible follow-up. Noise comes from: (a) each GetQuote returns ~25 fields where percent_change is buried alongside fifty_two_week ranges, rolling period changes, timestamps, market state, etc., across 3 stocks totaling ~75 noisy values to compare; (b) the RSI series returns ~90 data points with datetime+rsi where agent must find and count only those above 70; (c) the MACD series returns ~90 data points with 4 fields each where agent must compare two specific fields. The critical signal is the identity of the top-moving stock (e.g. 'AMD'), extracted after comparing 3 noisy quote objects, then remembered and used 2-3 steps later as the symbol argument for both RSI and MACD queries. If the agent picks the wrong stock (e.g. confuses AMD with NVDA), all subsequent RSI and MACD counts will be for the wrong ticker, making the final answer completely wrong. This matches Medium difficulty: 6-7 tool calls, moderate noise volumes (90 items per indicator query), and 2-3 pieces of information to track (which stock + two counts).

task: I'm conducting a cross-database oncology research analysis. Here's what I need you to do: First, look up all records in the 'Oncology Trials' table in the 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' Airtable base, then identify the single trial that has the NCT number listed and is currently in 'Phase 3' status with status 'Recruiting'. Extract and remember its NCTNumber and DrugName. Next, use the NCTNumber you found to fetch the full study details from ClinicalTrials.gov and extract the primary completion date and the lead sponsor name. Then, search ClinicalTrials.gov for all currently RECRUITING Phase 3 trials for the same therapeutic area as that trial (look it up from the Airtable record's TherapeuticArea field). From that search, find the country with the most trials by running a trend analysis (countByCountry). After that, geocode the capital city of that top country to get its coordinates. Finally, using those coordinates, find nearby hospitals within 3000 meters and count how many there are. Report: the DrugName, NCTNumber, lead sponsor from ClinicalTrials.gov, the top country by trial count, the capital city geocoded coordinates (latitude and longitude), and the total number of nearby hospitals found.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: List all Airtable bases to find the Oncology Drug Pipeline base ID
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Listing all Airtable bases to find Oncology Drug Pipeline')
    raw1 = call_tool('airtable_list_bases', {})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    bases_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'Bases data sample: {str(bases_data)[:300]}')

    bases = bases_data if isinstance(bases_data, list) else bases_data.get('bases', bases_data.get('value', []))
    oncology_base_id = None
    for base in bases:
        name = base.get('name', '') if isinstance(base, dict) else ''
        if 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' in name:
            oncology_base_id = base.get('id', base.get('base_id', ''))
            execution_log.append(f'Found Oncology Drug Pipeline base: id={oncology_base_id}')
            break
    if not oncology_base_id:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: Could not find Oncology Drug Pipeline base')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)

    # Step 2: List tables in the Oncology Drug Pipeline base
    execution_log.append(f'Step 2: Listing tables in base {oncology_base_id}')
    raw2 = call_tool('airtable_list_tables', {'base_id': oncology_base_id})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw2)[:200]}')
    tables_data = json.loads(raw2) if isinstance(raw2, str) else raw2
    execution_log.append(f'Tables: {str(tables_data)[:300]}')

    # Step 3: List all records in Oncology Trials table
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Listing all records in Oncology Trials table')
    raw3 = call_tool('airtable_list_records', {'base_id': oncology_base_id, 'table_name': 'Oncology Trials', 'max_records': 500})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw3)[:200]}')
    records_data = json.loads(raw3) if isinstance(raw3, str) else raw3
    execution_log.append(f'Records data (first 400 chars): {str(records_data)[:400]}')

    records = records_data if isinstance(records_data, list) else records_data.get('records', records_data.get('value', []))
    execution_log.append(f'Total records returned: {len(records)}')

    # Step 4: Extract signal - find Phase 3 + Recruiting trial with a NCT number
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Filtering for Phase 3, Recruiting trials with NCTNumber')
    target_record = None
    for rec in records:
        fields = rec.get('fields', rec) if isinstance(rec, dict) else {}
        phase = fields.get('Phase', '')
        status = fields.get('Status', '')
        nct = fields.get('NCTNumber', '')
        execution_log.append(f'  Record: Phase={phase}, Status={status}, NCT={nct}')
        if 'Phase 3' in str(phase) and 'Recruiting' in str(status) and nct and str(nct).upper().startswith('NCT'):
            target_record = fields
            execution_log.append(f'  -> MATCH FOUND: DrugName={fields.get("DrugName","")}, NCT={nct}, TherapeuticArea={fields.get("TherapeuticArea","")}')
            break

    if not target_record:
        execution_log.append('No Phase 3 Recruiting trial with NCTNumber found; picking first Phase 3 Recruiting trial')
        for rec in records:
            fields = rec.get('fields', rec) if isinstance(rec, dict) else {}
            if 'Phase 3' in str(fields.get('Phase', '')) and 'Recruiting' in str(fields.get('Status', '')):
                target_record = fields
                execution_log.append(f'  Fallback record: {str(target_record)[:200]}')
                break

    if not target_record:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: No matching record found at all')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)

    drug_name = target_record.get('DrugName', 'Unknown')
    nct_number = target_record.get('NCTNumber', '')
    therapeutic_area = target_record.get('TherapeuticArea', '')
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - DrugName: {drug_name}, NCTNumber: {nct_number}, TherapeuticArea: {therapeutic_area}')

    # Step 5: Fetch full study details from ClinicalTrials.gov using NCTNumber
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Fetching ClinicalTrials.gov study for NCT ID: {nct_number}')
    raw5 = call_tool('clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study', {'nctIds': nct_number, 'summaryOnly': False})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}')
    study_data = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    execution_log.append(f'Study data (first 500 chars): {str(study_data)[:500]}')

    # Extract primary completion date and lead sponsor
    primary_completion_date = 'Unknown'
    lead_sponsor = 'Unknown'
    if isinstance(study_data, list) and len(study_data) > 0:
        study = study_data[0]
    elif isinstance(study_data, dict):
        study = study_data
    else:
        study = {}

    # Try common nested paths for ClinicalTrials.gov response
    proto_module = study.get('protocolSection', study)
    status_module = proto_module.get('statusModule', proto_module)
    primary_completion_date = status_module.get('primaryCompletionDateStruct', {}).get('date', status_module.get('primaryCompletionDate', 'Unknown'))
    sponsor_module = proto_module.get('sponsorCollaboratorsModule', proto_module)
    lead_sponsor = sponsor_module.get('leadSponsor', {}).get('name', sponsor_module.get('leadSponsor', 'Unknown'))
    if isinstance(lead_sponsor, dict):
        lead_sponsor = lead_sponsor.get('name', 'Unknown')
    execution_log.append(f'Primary Completion Date: {primary_completion_date}')
    execution_log.append(f'Lead Sponsor: {lead_sponsor}')

    # Step 6: Search ClinicalTrials.gov for Recruiting Phase 3 trials in same therapeutic area, analyze by country
    execution_log.append(f'Step 6: Searching ClinicalTrials.gov for Recruiting Phase 3 trials in therapeutic area: {therapeutic_area}')
    raw6 = call_tool('clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends', {
        'query': {'cond': therapeutic_area},
        'filter': {'overallStatus': ['RECRUITING']},
        'analysisType': 'countByCountry'
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw6)[:200]}')
    trends_data = json.loads(raw6) if isinstance(raw6, str) else raw6
    execution_log.append(f'Trends data (first 600 chars): {str(trends_data)[:600]}')

    # Find the country with most trials
    top_country = None
    top_count = 0
    country_list = trends_data if isinstance(trends_data, list) else trends_data.get('results', trends_data.get('data', trends_data.get('countries', [])))
    if isinstance(country_list, dict):
        for k, v in country_list.items():
            count_val = v if isinstance(v, int) else (v.get('count', 0) if isinstance(v, dict) else 0)
            if count_val > top_count:
                top_count = count_val
                top_country = k
    elif isinstance(country_list, list):
        for item in country_list:
            if isinstance(item, dict):
                country = item.get('country', item.get('name', item.get('label', '')))
                count_val = item.get('count', item.get('value', item.get('studies', 0)))
                if isinstance(count_val, (int, float)) and count_val > top_count:
                    top_count = count_val
                    top_country = country
    execution_log.append(f'Top country by trial count: {top_country} ({top_count} trials)')

    if not top_country:
        top_country = 'United States'
        execution_log.append(f'Fallback to default top_country: {top_country}')

    # Step 7: Geocode the capital city of the top country
    capital_map = {
        'United States': 'Washington DC, USA',
        'China': 'Beijing, China',
        'France': 'Paris, France',
        'Germany': 'Berlin, Germany',
        'United Kingdom': 'London, United Kingdom',
        'Japan': 'Tokyo, Japan',
        'Canada': 'Ottawa, Canada',
        'Australia': 'Canberra, Australia',
        'Spain': 'Madrid, Spain',
        'Italy': 'Rome, Italy',
        'South Korea': 'Seoul, South Korea',
        'Brazil': 'Brasilia, Brazil',
        'India': 'New Delhi, India',
    }
    capital_query = capital_map.get(top_country, f'capital city of {top_country}')
    execution_log.append(f'Step 7: Geocoding capital of {top_country}: "{capital_query}"')
    raw7 = call_tool('osm-mcp-server_geocode_address', {'address': capital_query})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw7)[:200]}')
    geo_data = json.loads(raw7) if isinstance(raw7, str) else raw7
    execution_log.append(f'Geo data: {str(geo_data)[:400]}')

    geo_result = geo_data
    if isinstance(geo_data, dict):
        geo_result = geo_data.get('result', geo_data)
    if isinstance(geo_result, list) and len(geo_result) > 0:
        geo_result = geo_result[0]

    lat = float(geo_result.get('lat', geo_result.get('latitude', 0)))
    lon = float(geo_result.get('lon', geo_result.get('longitude', 0)))
    execution_log.append(f'Capital coordinates: lat={lat}, lon={lon}')

    # Step 8: Find nearby hospitals within 3000 meters
    execution_log.append(f'Step 8: Finding nearby hospitals within 3000m of ({lat}, {lon})')
    raw8 = call_tool('osm-mcp-server_find_nearby_places', {
        'latitude': lat,
        'longitude': lon,
        'radius': 3000,
        'categories': ['amenity'],
        'limit': 200
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw8)[:200]}')
    nearby_data = json.loads(raw8) if isinstance(raw8, str) else raw8
    execution_log.append(f'Nearby data (first 500 chars): {str(nearby_data)[:500]}')

    if isinstance(nearby_data, dict):
        nearby_result = nearby_data.get('result', nearby_data)
    else:
        nearby_result = nearby_data

    # Count hospitals specifically
    hospital_count = 0
    if isinstance(nearby_result, dict):
        categories = nearby_result.get('categories', nearby_result.get('results', nearby_result))
        if isinstance(categories, dict):
            amenity_cat = categories.get('amenity', {})
            if isinstance(amenity_cat, dict):
                hospital_count += len(amenity_cat.get('hospital', []))
                hospital_count += len(amenity_cat.get('clinic', []))
            elif isinstance(amenity_cat, list):
                for item in amenity_cat:
                    tags = item.get('tags', {})
                    if tags.get('amenity') in ['hospital', 'clinic']:
                        hospital_count += 1
        places = nearby_result.get('places', [])
        for place in places:
            if isinstance(place, dict):
                subcategory = place.get('subcategory', place.get('type', ''))
                if subcategory in ['hospital', 'clinic']:
                    hospital_count += 1
    execution_log.append(f'Hospital/clinic count within 3000m: {hospital_count}')

    # Final summary
    execution_log.append('\n=== FINAL REPORT ===')
    execution_log.append(f'DrugName: {drug_name}')
    execution_log.append(f'NCTNumber: {nct_number}')
    execution_log.append(f'Lead Sponsor (ClinicalTrials.gov): {lead_sponsor}')
    execution_log.append(f'Primary Completion Date: {primary_completion_date}')
    execution_log.append(f'Therapeutic Area: {therapeutic_area}')
    execution_log.append(f'Top Country by Recruiting Phase 3 Trials: {top_country} ({top_count} trials)')
    execution_log.append(f'Capital City Geocoded: {capital_query} -> lat={lat}, lon={lon}')
    execution_log.append(f'Nearby Hospitals/Clinics within 3000m of capital: {hospital_count}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Airtable returns hundreds of trial records with dozens of fields each; ClinicalTrials.gov study details contain deeply nested protocol sections, arms, outcomes, eligibility criteria, contact lists, and location data; trend analysis returns long country-count arrays; OSM geocoding returns multiple candidate results with verbose metadata; nearby places returns 200 POIs across many categories requiring hospital-specific filtering

claims.description: Call airtable_list_bases to discover all Airtable bases and find the base_id of 'Oncology Drug Pipeline'
claims.output_characteristics: List of all bases with names and IDs; agent must identify the correct base_id from multiple results

claims.description: Call airtable_list_tables on the discovered base_id to confirm 'Oncology Trials' table exists
claims.output_characteristics: List of table names and schemas; mostly irrelevant metadata

claims.description: Call airtable_list_records for 'Oncology Trials' table, returning all trial records (up to 500)
claims.output_characteristics: 500+ records each with 10+ fields including TrialID, DrugName, Phase, TherapeuticArea, Status, Sponsor, NCTNumber, StartDate, EndDate, Country — massive noisy output

claims.description: Filter records from step 3: find the single trial where Phase='Phase 3', Status='Recruiting', and NCTNumber starts with 'NCT'. Extract and remember DrugName, NCTNumber, and TherapeuticArea.
claims.extraction_criteria: Phase must be 'Phase 3', Status must be 'Recruiting', NCTNumber must exist and start with 'NCT'
claims.signal_to_remember: DrugName, NCTNumber, TherapeuticArea — all three are used in later steps

claims.description: Call clinicaltrials_get_study using the NCTNumber extracted in step 4 to get full study details
claims.output_characteristics: Deep nested JSON with protocolSection, statusModule, sponsorCollaboratorsModule, eligibilityModule, contactsLocationsModule, outcomesModule — hundreds of fields; agent must extract primaryCompletionDate and leadSponsor name

claims.description: Call clinicaltrials_analyze_trends with cond=TherapeuticArea (from step 4), filter Recruiting, analysisType=countByCountry to find which country has most trials
claims.output_characteristics: Long list of country-count pairs covering 50-100 countries; agent must identify the top country (highest count)

claims.description: Geocode the capital city of the top country found in step 6
claims.output_characteristics: Multiple geocoding candidates with verbose metadata (bounding boxes, OSM IDs, importance scores, address components); agent must extract lat/lon of the first/best result

claims.description: Find nearby places (amenity category) within 3000 meters of the capital city coordinates from step 7, then count hospital/clinic entries
claims.uses_signal: lat and lon from step 7; also DrugName, NCTNumber, lead sponsor from steps 4-5
claims.output_characteristics: Up to 200 POIs across many subcategories (restaurants, shops, schools, parks, transit, etc.); agent must filter specifically for hospital/clinic amenities and count them

claims.description: Compile final report combining all extracted signals: DrugName, NCTNumber, lead sponsor (ClinicalTrials.gov), primary completion date, top country, capital coordinates, hospital count

_design_rationale: 1) WHY CHAIN IS LONG: The task requires 9+ tool calls across 3 distinct servers (Airtable, ClinicalTrials.gov, OpenStreetMap): base discovery → table listing → record listing → CT study fetch → trend analysis → geocoding → nearby search. Each step is necessary and logically motivated by a real research workflow. 2) WHERE NOISE COMES FROM: Airtable returns hundreds of trial records with 10+ fields each; ClinicalTrials.gov study detail JSON is deeply nested with dozens of irrelevant modules (eligibility, outcomes, contacts, locations, arms, references); the trends analysis returns data for 50-100 countries; geocoding returns multiple candidates; OSM nearby returns 200 mixed POIs. 3) WHAT SIGNAL MUST BE EXTRACTED: The critical signals are (a) the exact NCTNumber of the Phase 3 Recruiting trial from among hundreds of Airtable records, (b) the TherapeuticArea field from the same record to drive the CT trends search, (c) the lead sponsor and primary completion date from the deeply nested ClinicalTrials.gov JSON, (d) the top-country name from the trends output, (e) the lat/lon from geocoding. 4) HOW SIGNALS ARE USED LATER: NCTNumber drives step 5; TherapeuticArea drives step 6; top-country name determines what capital to geocode in step 7; lat/lon are the coordinates for step 8's hospital search. 5) WHY LOSING SIGNAL CAUSES FAILURE: If the wrong NCTNumber is extracted (e.g., from a Phase 1 or Completed trial), the ClinicalTrials.gov lookup returns wrong sponsor/date. If TherapeuticArea is forgotten or wrong, the trend analysis targets the wrong disease, returning a different top country, causing wrong geocoding and wrong final hospital count. Every downstream answer depends on correctly filtering the initial noisy Airtable dump. 6) DIFFICULTY MATCH: Hard — 9+ tool calls, 3+ distinct servers, 4+ signals to track, with each intermediate output being large, noisy, and requiring non-trivial filtering.

task: I'm building a cross-asset momentum report and need a comprehensive technical analysis comparison across three assets: AAPL (stock), BTC/USD (crypto), and EUR/USD (forex). Here's what I need you to do: First, fetch 90-day daily OHLCV price history (from 2024-01-01 to 2024-03-31) for all three assets. From each price series, identify the single date where the CLOSE price was at its absolute MAXIMUM over that 90-day window — call these dates D_AAPL, D_BTC, and D_EUR. Then, for each of those three peak-close dates specifically, retrieve the RSI(14) value on that exact date by querying RSI data over the full 90-day period and looking up that date's RSI in the results. Next, fetch MACD(12,26,9) data over the same 90-day window for each asset and extract the MACD histogram value ('macd_hist') on each respective peak-close date D_AAPL, D_BTC, and D_EUR. Finally, fetch the 20-period Bollinger Bands for each asset over the same 90-day window and extract the upper_band value on each asset's peak-close date. Report the final summary table: for each of the three assets, show the peak-close date, the peak close price, the RSI(14) on that peak date, the MACD histogram on that peak date, and the upper Bollinger Band on that peak date.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    START_DATE = '2024-01-01'
    END_DATE = '2024-03-31'
    OUTPUTSIZE = 500

    assets = [
        {'symbol': 'AAPL', 'label': 'AAPL'},
        {'symbol': 'BTC/USD', 'label': 'BTC/USD'},
        {'symbol': 'EUR/USD', 'label': 'EUR/USD'},
    ]

    peak_dates = {}
    peak_closes = {}

    # STEP 1-3: Fetch 90-day OHLCV for each asset and find peak-close date
    for asset in assets:
        sym = asset['symbol']
        execution_log.append(f"Step [Price History] Fetching daily OHLCV for {sym} from {START_DATE} to {END_DATE}")
        raw = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeries', {
            'params': {
                'symbol': sym,
                'interval': '1day',
                'start_date': START_DATE,
                'end_date': END_DATE,
                'outputsize': OUTPUTSIZE,
                'order': 'asc'
            }
        })
        execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw)[:300]}")
        data = json.loads(raw) if isinstance(raw, str) else raw
        values = data.get('values', [])
        execution_log.append(f"  -> Got {len(values)} daily bars for {sym}")
        if not values:
            execution_log.append(f"  WARNING: No values returned for {sym}")
            peak_dates[sym] = None
            peak_closes[sym] = None
            continue
        # Find the bar with max close price
        max_close = None
        max_date = None
        for bar in values:
            close_str = bar.get('close', None)
            dt = bar.get('datetime', None)
            if close_str is None or dt is None:
                continue
            try:
                close_val = float(close_str)
            except Exception:
                continue
            if max_close is None or close_val > max_close:
                max_close = close_val
                max_date = dt
        peak_dates[sym] = max_date
        peak_closes[sym] = max_close
        execution_log.append(f"  -> Peak close for {sym}: date={max_date}, close={max_close}")

    execution_log.append(f"\nPeak dates extracted: AAPL={peak_dates.get('AAPL')}, BTC/USD={peak_dates.get('BTC/USD')}, EUR/USD={peak_dates.get('EUR/USD')}")

    # STEP 4-6: Fetch RSI(14) for each asset over 90-day window, look up RSI on peak date
    peak_rsi = {}
    for asset in assets:
        sym = asset['symbol']
        peak_date = peak_dates.get(sym)
        execution_log.append(f"\nStep [RSI] Fetching RSI(14) for {sym} from {START_DATE} to {END_DATE}")
        raw_rsi = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi', {
            'params': {
                'symbol': sym,
                'interval': '1day',
                'start_date': START_DATE,
                'end_date': END_DATE,
                'outputsize': OUTPUTSIZE,
                'time_period': 14,
                'series_type': 'close',
                'order': 'asc'
            }
        })
        execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw_rsi)[:300]}")
        rsi_data = json.loads(raw_rsi) if isinstance(raw_rsi, str) else raw_rsi
        rsi_values = rsi_data.get('values', [])
        execution_log.append(f"  -> Got {len(rsi_values)} RSI data points for {sym}")
        # Build a date->rsi map
        rsi_map = {}
        for row in rsi_values:
            dt = row.get('datetime', None)
            rsi_val = row.get('rsi', None)
            if dt and rsi_val:
                rsi_map[dt] = rsi_val
        # Look up peak date in RSI map
        rsi_on_peak = rsi_map.get(peak_date, None) if peak_date else None
        peak_rsi[sym] = rsi_on_peak
        execution_log.append(f"  -> RSI on peak date ({peak_date}) for {sym}: {rsi_on_peak}")
        execution_log.append(f"  (RSI map sample dates: {list(rsi_map.keys())[:5]}...  last 5: {list(rsi_map.keys())[-5:]}...)")

    execution_log.append(f"\nPeak RSI values: AAPL={peak_rsi.get('AAPL')}, BTC/USD={peak_rsi.get('BTC/USD')}, EUR/USD={peak_rsi.get('EUR/USD')}")

    # STEP 7-9: Fetch MACD(12,26,9) for each asset, look up macd_hist on peak date
    peak_macd_hist = {}
    for asset in assets:
        sym = asset['symbol']
        peak_date = peak_dates.get(sym)
        execution_log.append(f"\nStep [MACD] Fetching MACD(12,26,9) for {sym} from {START_DATE} to {END_DATE}")
        raw_macd = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd', {
            'params': {
                'symbol': sym,
                'interval': '1day',
                'start_date': START_DATE,
                'end_date': END_DATE,
                'outputsize': OUTPUTSIZE,
                'fast_period': 12,
                'slow_period': 26,
                'signal_period': 9,
                'series_type': 'close',
                'order': 'asc'
            }
        })
        execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw_macd)[:300]}")
        macd_data = json.loads(raw_macd) if isinstance(raw_macd, str) else raw_macd
        macd_values = macd_data.get('values', [])
        execution_log.append(f"  -> Got {len(macd_values)} MACD data points for {sym}")
        # Build date->macd_hist map
        macd_map = {}
        for row in macd_values:
            dt = row.get('datetime', None)
            hist = row.get('macd_hist', None)
            if dt and hist is not None:
                macd_map[dt] = hist
        hist_on_peak = macd_map.get(peak_date, None) if peak_date else None
        peak_macd_hist[sym] = hist_on_peak
        execution_log.append(f"  -> MACD hist on peak date ({peak_date}) for {sym}: {hist_on_peak}")

    execution_log.append(f"\nPeak MACD hist values: AAPL={peak_macd_hist.get('AAPL')}, BTC/USD={peak_macd_hist.get('BTC/USD')}, EUR/USD={peak_macd_hist.get('EUR/USD')}")

    # STEP 10-12: Fetch Bollinger Bands(20,2) for each asset, look up upper_band on peak date
    peak_upper_band = {}
    for asset in assets:
        sym = asset['symbol']
        peak_date = peak_dates.get(sym)
        execution_log.append(f"\nStep [BBands] Fetching BBands(20,2) for {sym} from {START_DATE} to {END_DATE}")
        raw_bb = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesBBands', {
            'params': {
                'symbol': sym,
                'interval': '1day',
                'start_date': START_DATE,
                'end_date': END_DATE,
                'outputsize': OUTPUTSIZE,
                'time_period': 20,
                'sd': 2,
                'series_type': 'close',
                'ma_type': 'SMA',
                'order': 'asc'
            }
        })
        execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw_bb)[:300]}")
        bb_data = json.loads(raw_bb) if isinstance(raw_bb, str) else raw_bb
        bb_values = bb_data.get('values', [])
        execution_log.append(f"  -> Got {len(bb_values)} BBands data points for {sym}")
        # Build date->upper_band map
        bb_map = {}
        for row in bb_values:
            dt = row.get('datetime', None)
            ub = row.get('upper_band', None)
            if dt and ub is not None:
                bb_map[dt] = ub
        ub_on_peak = bb_map.get(peak_date, None) if peak_date else None
        peak_upper_band[sym] = ub_on_peak
        execution_log.append(f"  -> Upper Band on peak date ({peak_date}) for {sym}: {ub_on_peak}")

    execution_log.append(f"\nPeak Upper Band values: AAPL={peak_upper_band.get('AAPL')}, BTC/USD={peak_upper_band.get('BTC/USD')}, EUR/USD={peak_upper_band.get('EUR/USD')}")

    # FINAL SUMMARY
    execution_log.append("\n===== FINAL SUMMARY TABLE =====")
    for sym in ['AAPL', 'BTC/USD', 'EUR/USD']:
        execution_log.append(
            f"Asset: {sym} | Peak Close Date: {peak_dates.get(sym)} | Peak Close: {peak_closes.get(sym)} "
            f"| RSI(14) on peak: {peak_rsi.get(sym)} "
            f"| MACD hist on peak: {peak_macd_hist.get(sym)} "
            f"| Upper BBand on peak: {peak_upper_band.get(sym)}"
        )

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Each GetTimeSeries call returns up to 90 daily OHLCV bars with open/high/low/close/volume fields; RSI/MACD/BBands calls each return 90 data rows; agent must scan all rows to find maximum close price date and then locate that specific date in three separate indicator series, all of which contain irrelevant data points.

claims.description: Fetch 90-day daily price series for AAPL (2024-01-01 to 2024-03-31) — produces ~90 OHLCV bars
claims.output_characteristics: ~90 bars each with open/high/low/close/volume; agent must scan all to find max close

claims.description: Fetch 90-day daily price series for BTC/USD — produces ~90 OHLCV bars
claims.output_characteristics: ~90 bars; agent must find max close date D_BTC

claims.description: Fetch 90-day daily price series for EUR/USD — produces ~90 OHLCV bars; extract D_AAPL, D_BTC, D_EUR (the three peak-close dates)
claims.output_characteristics: ~90 bars; after this step agent must remember D_AAPL, D_BTC, D_EUR as signals
claims.signal_to_remember: D_AAPL, D_BTC, D_EUR — three peak-close dates buried in 270 total rows across three calls

claims.description: Fetch RSI(14) for AAPL over 90-day window — produces ~90 RSI rows; agent must look up value at D_AAPL
claims.output_characteristics: ~90 RSI data points; only one is relevant (the one matching D_AAPL)

claims.description: Fetch RSI(14) for BTC/USD over 90-day window; look up RSI at D_BTC
claims.output_characteristics: ~90 RSI rows; only D_BTC row is relevant

claims.description: Fetch RSI(14) for EUR/USD over 90-day window; look up RSI at D_EUR
claims.output_characteristics: ~90 RSI rows; only D_EUR row is relevant

claims.description: Fetch MACD(12,26,9) for AAPL, BTC/USD, EUR/USD; extract macd_hist at each respective peak date
claims.output_characteristics: ~3x90 = 270 MACD rows each with macd/macd_signal/macd_hist; agent must pinpoint exactly the peak-date row per asset

claims.description: Fetch Bollinger Bands(20,2) for AAPL, BTC/USD, EUR/USD; extract upper_band at each respective peak date
claims.output_characteristics: ~3x90 = 270 BBands rows each with upper_band/middle_band/lower_band; agent must find the peak-date row per asset

claims.description: Assemble final summary: for each asset, report peak-close date, peak close price, RSI(14) on that date, MACD hist on that date, and upper BBand on that date
claims.uses_signal: D_AAPL, D_BTC, D_EUR and all extracted indicator values on those dates

_design_rationale: 1. Chain length: 12 tool calls are required (3x GetTimeSeries + 3x GetTimeSeriesRsi + 3x GetTimeSeriesMacd + 3x GetTimeSeriesBBands), exceeding the Hard threshold of 9+. 2. Noise: Each call returns ~90 daily bars; across all 12 calls that is ~1080 data rows, the vast majority irrelevant. RSI/MACD/BBands calls each contain only ONE relevant row (the peak-close date) among 90. 3. Signal extraction: After the first three GetTimeSeries calls, the agent must identify the date of the maximum close price for each of three different assets — three distinct 'needles' buried across ~270 rows of OHLCV data. These dates (D_AAPL, D_BTC, D_EUR) are nowhere stated in the task — they must be computed. 4. Signal usage: The extracted dates are then cross-referenced against RSI, MACD, and BBands series across steps 4–11, requiring the agent to remember three dates throughout many subsequent noisy tool calls. 5. Failure mode: If the agent picks the wrong peak-close date for any asset (e.g., confuses max high vs. max close, or mixes up dates across assets), all three downstream indicator lookups for that asset will be wrong, causing the final summary to be incorrect. 6. Hard difficulty justification: 12 mandatory tool calls, 3 distinct signals to extract from noisy data, cross-referenced against 9 indicator series (3 assets × 3 indicators), requiring persistent memory of 3 dates across the full chain.

task: I need to audit the `snake-game` repository at `/data/repos/snake-game` and understand its recent development history. First, get the full git log (at least 50 commits) for the repo. From that log, identify the SHA of the commit whose message contains the word 'score' (case-insensitive) — if multiple commits match, pick the earliest one (i.e., the one that appears last in the log). Then, look at the full diff of that specific commit to understand what changed. Next, check the current git status and unstaged diff of the repo to see what has been modified since the last commit. Finally, look at the contents of the main Python source file (find it using `find` in /data/repos/snake-game with name '*.py') and count the total number of lines that contain the string 'score' (case-insensitive). Report: (1) the SHA of the earliest commit whose message contains 'score', (2) the filename(s) changed in that commit, and (3) the count of lines containing 'score' in the current main Python source file.

state_assertions.description: Output report file /data/snake_audit_report.txt exists
Report file is non-empty (> 50 bytes)
Report contains a 40-character hex SHA (commit SHA format)
Report contains a reference to a .py filename (file changed in commit)
Report contains a numeric count (lines with 'score')

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: git log with 50+ commits produces large output with many commit SHAs, messages, authors, and dates — agent must filter to find commits mentioning 'score', then pick the earliest (last in log). git show produces full diff noise with many changed lines. find produces list of all .py files. cat of main Python file has many lines, agent must count only 'score'-containing lines.

claims.description: Call git_git_log on /data/repos/snake-game with max_count=50, producing a large noisy output of 50 commit entries each with SHA, author, date, and message
claims.output_characteristics: 50 commit entries with full metadata, most messages unrelated to 'score'

claims.description: Filter the log output to find all commits whose message contains 'score' (case-insensitive), then pick the earliest one (last in the log list). Extract and remember its full SHA.
claims.extraction_criteria: Find commits with 'score' in message, select the one appearing last (oldest) in the log

claims.description: Call git_git_show on /data/repos/snake-game with the remembered SHA to get the full diff of that commit. Extract the filenames changed.
claims.output_characteristics: Full commit diff with many changed lines, must extract changed filenames

claims.description: Call git_git_status on /data/repos/snake-game to see current working tree status
claims.output_characteristics: List of modified/untracked files

claims.description: Call git_git_diff_unstaged on /data/repos/snake-game to inspect current unstaged changes
claims.output_characteristics: Diff output of all currently modified files

claims.description: Use cli-mcp-server_run_command with 'find /data/repos/snake-game -name *.py' to locate all Python source files
claims.output_characteristics: List of .py files in the repo

claims.description: Use cli-mcp-server_run_command with 'cat <main_py_file>' to read the main Python source file content (large noisy output with all game code)
claims.output_characteristics: Full source file content with hundreds of lines, agent must count lines containing 'score'

claims.description: Write a report to /data/snake_audit_report.txt containing: (1) the SHA of the earliest 'score' commit, (2) the filenames changed in that commit, (3) the count of lines containing 'score' in the main Python file
claims.uses_signal: SHA from step 2, filenames from step 3, line count from step 7

_design_rationale: (1) Chain is long because it involves: git log (large output) → signal extraction (find earliest 'score' commit SHA) → git show with that SHA (large diff output) → git status → git diff → find .py files → cat main file (large output) → write report. That is 7-8 tool calls minimum. (2) Noise comes from: 50+ commit entries in git log (most irrelevant), full diff output in git show (many changed lines), full source file content in cat. (3) The critical signal is the SHA of the earliest commit whose message contains 'score' — it is buried among 50 commit messages, most of which don't contain 'score', and the agent must pick the EARLIEST (last in log) not just any. (4) This SHA is used in step 3 (git show) — if the wrong SHA is used, the wrong diff is shown and wrong filenames are reported. (5) Losing the signal causes wrong SHA → wrong diff → wrong filenames in final report. (6) Medium difficulty: 6-8 tool calls, 2-3 signals to track (SHA, filenames, line count), each step produces moderately noisy output.

task: I'm preparing a cross-domain literature synthesis report on 'transformer attention mechanisms' for a research meeting. Here's what I need you to do: Search arXiv for papers on 'transformer attention mechanisms' (get 50 results, categories cs.LG and cs.AI, from 2023-01-01 to 2024-01-01). From those results, identify the paper with the highest number of authors (if tied, pick the one appearing first). Remember that paper's arXiv ID. Then also search PubMed for 'transformer attention neural network' (get 20 results) and identify the PubMed article whose title contains the word 'efficient' (case-insensitive); if multiple match, pick the one with the most recent publication date. Remember that article's PMID. Next, download the arXiv paper you identified (using its arXiv ID), then read its full content. From the full paper content, extract the exact string that appears as the first section heading after the Introduction (e.g., '2 Related Work' or '2. Background' — whatever the second major section is titled). Also fetch the full metadata for the PubMed article (using its PMID) and extract the journal name where it was published. Finally, create the directory /data/synthesis_report and write a file /data/synthesis_report/cross_domain_summary.md that contains: (1) the arXiv paper ID and title, (2) the exact first-post-Introduction section heading extracted from the paper, (3) the PubMed PMID and article title, (4) the journal name of the PubMed article, and (5) a one-paragraph synthesis description noting both papers discuss transformer attention mechanisms from different disciplinary perspectives.

state_assertions.description: Directory /data/synthesis_report exists
File /data/synthesis_report/cross_domain_summary.md exists
The summary file is non-empty (at least 300 bytes)
The summary file contains an arXiv paper ID (pattern like 2301.XXXXX or 2312.XXXXX)
The summary file contains a PubMed PMID (a numeric ID)
The summary file mentions a section heading (contains a digit followed by text, typical of paper section headings)
The summary file mentions a journal name (contains 'Journal' or 'IEEE' or 'Nature' or 'PLOS' or 'Bioinformatics' or similar academic journal keywords)
The summary file contains a synthesis paragraph mentioning transformer or attention

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: arXiv search returns 50 papers each with full metadata (authors list, abstract, categories, dates); PubMed returns 20 articles with verbose metadata; paper full-text is thousands of lines requiring section-heading extraction; PubMed metadata includes many irrelevant fields surrounding the journal name

claims.description: Search arXiv for 'transformer attention mechanisms' with max_results=50, categories=['cs.LG','cs.AI'], date_from='2023-01-01', date_to='2024-01-01'. Returns ~50 papers each with id, title, authors (list), abstract, categories, published date.
claims.output_characteristics: 50 papers with full metadata; author lists vary from 1 to 15+ authors per paper; many irrelevant fields (abstract text, categories, arxiv URLs)

claims.description: Filter the 50 arXiv results to find the paper with the most authors. Count authors per paper, identify the maximum, extract that paper's arXiv ID and title. This is the primary signal to remember.
claims.extraction_criteria: For each paper count len(authors), find max; if tie take first occurrence. Extract paper_id (e.g. '2309.12345') and paper_title.

claims.description: Search PubMed for 'transformer attention neural network' with num_results=20. Returns 20 articles each with pmid, title, authors, journal, publication date, abstract.
claims.output_characteristics: 20 PubMed articles with full metadata including many irrelevant fields; titles, abstracts, and author lists constitute noise

claims.description: Filter the 20 PubMed results for articles whose title contains 'efficient' (case-insensitive). Among matches, pick the one with the most recent publication date. Extract its PMID.
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter titles by re.search(r'efficient', title, re.IGNORECASE); sort by publication date descending; take PMID of first result.

claims.description: Download the arXiv paper identified in step 2 using its arXiv ID. This triggers conversion/download of the paper.
Read the full content of the downloaded arXiv paper using arxiv_read_paper. Returns thousands of lines of markdown. Must scan through to find the first major section heading after the Introduction.
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper in markdown: thousands of lines including abstract, introduction, multiple sections, equations, references. Must find the second major section heading (first after Introduction).

claims.description: Fetch full PubMed metadata for the PMID identified in step 4 using pubmed_get_pubmed_article_metadata. Returns verbose metadata object with many fields.
claims.output_characteristics: Full PubMed metadata: journal, volume, issue, pages, doi, mesh terms, grant info, authors with affiliations, abstract structured into many subfields. Must extract journal name from noise.

claims.description: Extract the journal name from the PubMed metadata returned in step 7.
claims.extraction_criteria: Navigate metadata structure to find journal field (could be nested under 'journal', 'source', or 'medline_journal_info'). Extract the human-readable journal name.

claims.description: Create directory /data/synthesis_report using filesystem_create_directory.

Write /data/synthesis_report/cross_domain_summary.md containing: arXiv paper ID and title (from step 2), first-post-Introduction section heading (from step 6), PubMed PMID and article title (from steps 3-4), journal name (from step 8), and a synthesis paragraph. All four remembered signals must appear correctly.
claims.uses_signal: arxiv_paper_id, arxiv_paper_title, first_post_intro_section_heading, pubmed_pmid, pubmed_article_title, journal_name

_design_rationale: 1) WHY CHAIN IS LONG: The task requires 10 logical steps across 3 distinct servers (arxiv, pubmed, filesystem): two independent searches, two signal extractions from noisy results, a download, a full-text read requiring section extraction, a metadata fetch requiring journal extraction, directory creation, and file writing. This naturally exceeds 9 tool calls. 2) WHERE NOISE COMES FROM: arXiv search returns 50 papers each with 10+ fields including long abstracts and variable-length author lists (the signal is which paper has the MOST authors — buried in comparing all 50). PubMed returns 20 articles requiring title filtering by substring. The full paper text is thousands of lines requiring scanning for section headings. PubMed metadata is a deeply nested object with dozens of fields. 3) WHAT SIGNALS MUST BE EXTRACTED: (a) arXiv ID of paper with most authors from 50 results; (b) PMID of most recent 'efficient'-titled paper from 20 results; (c) exact first-post-Introduction section heading from full paper text; (d) journal name from PubMed metadata. 4) HOW SIGNALS ARE USED LATER: The arXiv ID is used 3+ steps later for download and read. The PMID is used 3+ steps later for metadata fetch. The section heading and journal name are remembered until the final write step. 5) WHY LOSING SIGNAL CAUSES FAILURE: Wrong arXiv ID → download wrong paper → read wrong content → extract wrong section heading → report is wrong. Wrong PMID → fetch wrong metadata → extract wrong journal → report is wrong. 6) DIFFICULTY JUSTIFICATION: Hard difficulty is justified by 10 tool calls, 3 distinct servers, 4 independent signals to extract and carry forward through noisy intermediate steps, and the non-trivial extraction of a section heading from full paper markdown text.

task: I'm doing a cross-repository code quality audit on the snake-game and storyteller projects in /data/repos/. Here's what I need: First, examine the full git commit history (all branches) of /data/repos/snake-game to find the SHA of the OLDEST commit (i.e., the very first commit ever made in that repo). Then, look at the full git log of /data/repos/storyteller to find the SHA of the MOST RECENT commit on its main branch. Next, fetch the Context7 documentation for the 'pytest' library (topic: 'fixtures') and for 'TypeScript' (topic: 'interfaces') — these will produce large docs you'll need to skim. After reviewing those docs, go back and get the detailed diff of the oldest snake-game commit SHA you found earlier (show what was introduced in that very first commit), and also show the full contents of the most recent storyteller commit SHA you found. Finally, write a Python script to /data/snake_storyteller_audit.py that prints: (1) the oldest snake-game commit SHA, (2) the most recent storyteller commit SHA, (3) the number of lines changed in the oldest snake-game commit (additions + deletions combined, extracted from the diff output), and (4) the author name of the most recent storyteller commit. Then execute that script and save its output to /data/snake_storyteller_audit_result.txt.

The audit script is non-empty (> 50 bytes)
The audit result file /data/snake_storyteller_audit_result.txt was created
The result file is non-empty (> 10 bytes)
The result file contains a git SHA (40-character hex string) for the snake-game oldest commit
The audit Python script references both snake-game and storyteller commit SHAs as string variables or print statements
The result file contains at least 4 lines of output (one per required data point)
state_assertions.code: len([l for l in open('/data/snake_storyteller_audit_result.txt').read().splitlines() if l.strip()]) >= 4

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: git_git_log returns many commits with full metadata (author, date, message, SHA) across multiple pages; context7 docs return thousands of tokens of library documentation irrelevant to the core task; git_git_show returns full diffs with many lines of code changes; agent must track two specific SHAs across many noisy intermediate steps

claims.description: Call git_git_log on /data/repos/snake-game with a large max_count to retrieve the full commit history; output is noisy with many commits, dates, authors, messages
claims.output_characteristics: Many commit entries each with SHA, author, date, message — must find the OLDEST (last in list) commit SHA

claims.description: Extract the OLDEST snake-game commit SHA from step 1 output (the last entry in the log, i.e., the root commit)
claims.extraction_criteria: The final commit in the git log output (oldest timestamp or root commit with no parent)

claims.description: Call git_git_log on /data/repos/storyteller to get its commit history; output is noisy with many commits
claims.output_characteristics: Many commit entries — must extract the MOST RECENT (first entry) commit SHA and author name

claims.description: Extract the most recent storyteller commit SHA and author name from step 3 output
claims.extraction_criteria: The first commit in the git log output (most recent), extract both SHA and author name

claims.output_characteristics: List of many library matches with IDs, descriptions, trust scores — must pick correct pytest ID

claims.output_characteristics: Large documentation blob with code snippets, explanations, API references — irrelevant to core task

claims.output_characteristics: List of library matches

claims.output_characteristics: Large documentation blob — irrelevant to core task but agent must not confuse this with the SHAs

claims.description: Use the oldest snake-game SHA remembered from step 2 to call git_git_show and get its full diff — output is large with many lines of code
claims.output_characteristics: Full diff of the first commit including all initial files added — must count total additions+deletions

claims.description: Use the newest storyteller SHA remembered from step 4 to call git_git_show and get its full details
claims.output_characteristics: Full commit diff and metadata

claims.description: Initialize and write the audit Python script to /data/snake_storyteller_audit.py using signals from steps 2, 4, 9 (oldest SHA, newest SHA, line count, author name)
Execute the audit script and save output to /data/snake_storyteller_audit_result.txt

_design_rationale: 1. WHY CHAIN IS LONG: The task requires 9+ tool calls across 3 different servers (git, context7, mcp-code-executor): two git_git_log calls on different repos, two context7 resolve+fetch pairs (4 calls), two git_git_show calls using remembered SHAs, then file creation and execution — totaling 12 steps. 2. WHERE NOISE COMES FROM: git_git_log returns all commits with full metadata (many entries); context7 docs return thousands of tokens of library documentation completely irrelevant to the git audit task but appearing between the critical signal-extraction and signal-usage steps; git_git_show returns full diffs with potentially hundreds of lines. 3. WHAT SIGNAL MUST BE EXTRACTED: Two git SHAs (oldest snake-game root commit, newest storyteller commit) and two derived values (author name of newest storyteller commit, line count from the oldest snake-game diff) — all must be tracked across many noisy intermediate steps. 4. HOW SIGNAL IS USED LATER: The oldest snake-game SHA (extracted in step 2) is not used again until step 9 (after 6 intervening noisy steps including large library docs). The newest storyteller SHA (extracted in step 4) is not used until step 10. Both SHAs and derived values are finally used in steps 11-12 to write and run the script. 5. WHY LOSING SIGNAL CAUSES FAILURE: If the agent forgets the oldest SHA after the context7 doc calls, it cannot correctly call git_git_show with the right revision, will get wrong diff, wrong line count, and the final script output will be wrong — causing state_assertions to fail. 6. WHY THIS MATCHES HARD: 12 tool calls, 3 distinct servers, 4 separate signals to track, 4+ large noisy outputs between extraction and usage, requires both stateful file creation and correct information threading.

task: I'm planning a trip to Yosemite National Park and want to know about camping options there. First, search for Yosemite National Park using the national parks API to get its park code. Then use that park code to get the full park details — from the details page, note the park's GPS coordinates (latitude and longitude). Next, get the list of campgrounds in Yosemite using that park code. From the campgrounds list, identify the campground that has the highest total number of sites (look for the 'totalSites' field and pick the one with the largest number). Finally, use the GPS coordinates you noted from the park details (not the campground's coordinates) to get the current weather at Yosemite and tell me: (1) the name of the campground with the most total sites, (2) its total site count, and (3) the current temperature and weather condition at Yosemite's main coordinates.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: Find Yosemite National Park to get park code
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Searching for Yosemite National Park to get park code')
    raw1 = call_tool('national-parks_findParks', {'q': 'Yosemite', 'limit': 10})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    data1 = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'findParks result sample: {str(data1)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract park code from results
    parks = []
    if isinstance(data1, dict):
        parks = data1.get('data', data1.get('parks', []))
    elif isinstance(data1, list):
        parks = data1
    park_code = 'yose'
    for park in parks:
        name = park.get('fullName', park.get('name', ''))
        if 'Yosemite' in name:
            park_code = park.get('parkCode', 'yose')
            break
    execution_log.append(f'Extracted park code: {park_code}')

    # Step 2: Get full park details to extract GPS coordinates
    execution_log.append(f'Step 2: Getting park details for park code: {park_code}')
    raw2 = call_tool('national-parks_getParkDetails', {'parkCode': park_code})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw2)[:200]}')
    data2 = json.loads(raw2) if isinstance(raw2, str) else raw2
    execution_log.append(f'getParkDetails result sample: {str(data2)[:500]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract GPS coordinates from park details (this is the signal to remember)
    park_data = data2
    if isinstance(data2, dict) and 'data' in data2:
        park_list = data2.get('data', [])
        if isinstance(park_list, list) and len(park_list) > 0:
            park_data = park_list[0]
        elif isinstance(park_list, dict):
            park_data = park_list
    lat = park_data.get('latitude', park_data.get('lat', '37.8651'))
    lon = park_data.get('longitude', park_data.get('lon', park_data.get('long', '-119.5383')))
    if not lat or not lon:
        lat = '37.8651'
        lon = '-119.5383'
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Park GPS coordinates: lat={lat}, lon={lon}')

    # Step 3: Get campgrounds for Yosemite using the park code
    execution_log.append(f'Step 3: Getting campgrounds for park code: {park_code}')
    raw3 = call_tool('national-parks_getCampgrounds', {'parkCode': park_code, 'limit': 50})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw3)[:200]}')
    data3 = json.loads(raw3) if isinstance(raw3, str) else raw3
    execution_log.append(f'getCampgrounds result sample: {str(data3)[:600]}... [truncated]')

    # Find campground with highest totalSites
    campgrounds = []
    if isinstance(data3, dict):
        campgrounds = data3.get('data', data3.get('campgrounds', []))
    elif isinstance(data3, list):
        campgrounds = data3
    execution_log.append(f'Number of campgrounds found: {len(campgrounds)}')

    best_campground_name = 'Unknown'
    best_total_sites = 0
    for cg in campgrounds:
        total_sites_raw = cg.get('totalSites', cg.get('campsites', {}))
        if isinstance(total_sites_raw, dict):
            total = 0
            for v in total_sites_raw.values():
                try:
                    total += int(v)
