evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Each findParks call returns up to 50 parks with full descriptions, contact info, hours, topics, activities, entrance fees, images, and addresses. getParkDetails returns verbose JSON with dozens of fields. getAlerts returns up to 50 alert objects each with title, description, category, url, lastIndexedDate. Distance matrix and weather forecast return multi-field nested objects. Agent must extract and retain two specific parkCodes and their GPS coordinates across 11 steps.

claims.description: Call national-parks_findParks for California (stateCode=CA, limit=50), returns large list of parks with verbose descriptions, activities, fees, and images
claims.output_characteristics: Up to 50 park objects each with ~20+ fields including fullName, parkCode, description, activities, topics, addresses, images, entranceFees, operatingHours

claims.description: Extract Signal A: scan all CA park descriptions and names for 'sequoia' or 'giant trees', record the matching parkCode
claims.extraction_criteria: Find park whose description or fullName contains 'sequoia' or 'giant tree' (case-insensitive)

claims.description: Call national-parks_getParkDetails using CA_parkCode from step 2, extract GPS coordinates (latitude, longitude) from latLong field
claims.output_characteristics: Single verbose park object with latLong string like 'lat:36.4, long:-118.5885335', plus dozens of other fields (contacts, multimedia, topics, etc.)

claims.description: Call national-parks_findParks for Utah (stateCode=UT, limit=50), returns large list of parks — many irrelevant
claims.output_characteristics: Up to 50 Utah park objects with same verbose structure as step 1

claims.description: Extract Signal B: find UT park with 'canyon' in its fullName, record the parkCode
claims.extraction_criteria: Find first park in UT results whose fullName contains 'canyon' (case-insensitive)
claims.signal_to_remember: UT_parkCode (e.g., 'brca' for Bryce Canyon or 'zion' for Zion with canyon in name)

claims.description: Call national-parks_getParkDetails using UT_parkCode from step 5, extract GPS coordinates from latLong field
claims.output_characteristics: Verbose park object; agent must parse latLong string to extract numeric lat/lon

claims.description: Call google-maps_maps_distance_matrix using CA_lat/CA_lon (from step 3) as origin and UT_lat/UT_lon (from step 6) as destination with mode=driving
claims.output_characteristics: Nested JSON with rows>elements>distance.text and duration.text buried among status fields

claims.description: Call weather_get_current_weather using CA_lat,CA_lon coordinates (from step 3) to get current temperature at the California park
claims.output_characteristics: Large nested NWS-format JSON with properties.temperature.value buried among dozens of fields

claims.description: Call weather_get_weather_forecast with UT_lat,UT_lon coordinates (from step 6) and days=7, scan all forecast periods to find highest temperature value
claims.output_characteristics: JSON with properties.periods array of 14 day/night periods each with temperature, shortForecast, detailedForecast, windSpeed, etc.

claims.description: Call national-parks_getAlerts for CA park using CA_parkCode (from step 2), check if any active alerts exist
claims.output_characteristics: Array of alert objects with title, description, category, url — may be empty or contain many items

claims.description: Call national-parks_getAlerts for UT park using UT_parkCode (from step 5), check if any active alerts exist, then compile final summary
claims.output_characteristics: Array of alert objects; final answer requires aggregating: park names, coordinates, driving distance, driving time, current CA temp, highest UT 7-day temp, alert status for both parks

_design_rationale: (1) Chain is long because each real-world workflow step (find parks, get details, compute distance, get weather, check alerts) requires a separate API call — 11 calls total across 4 different servers. (2) Noise comes from: findParks returning up to 50 parks each with 20+ fields (descriptions, activities, topics, images, fees, hours, contacts, multimedia); getParkDetails returning verbose single-park records with latLong buried in a formatted string; weather_get_weather_forecast returning 14 forecast periods with many fields each; getAlerts returning many alert objects. (3) Critical signals: CA_parkCode (extracted by text-matching in step 2), CA_lat/CA_lon (parsed from latLong string in step 3), UT_parkCode (text-matching in step 5), UT_lat/UT_lon (parsed from latLong string in step 6). (4) Signals are used much later: CA_parkCode reused in steps 8 and 10; UT_parkCode reused in steps 9 and 11; coordinates used in steps 7, 8, 9. (5) Losing any signal cascades: wrong CA_parkCode → wrong coordinates → wrong distance calculation → wrong weather location → wrong alerts; losing UT coordinates → wrong driving distance; forgetting to parse lat/lon numerically → distance matrix call fails. (6) Hard level justified by: 9+ tool calls, 4 distinct servers (national-parks, google-maps, weather, weather-data available as distractors), 4 separate signals that must be extracted and retained across a long noisy chain, requiring numeric parsing of GPS strings from verbose free-text fields.

task: I need to understand the commit history of the snake-game repository at /data/repos/snake-game. Please look at the git log for that repo (fetch at least 20 commits), find the SHA of the commit whose message contains the word 'score' (if multiple match, take the earliest one — i.e., the one with the oldest timestamp), then show the full details of that specific commit so I can see exactly what files were changed and what the diff looks like.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []
    repo_path = '/data/repos/snake-game'

    # Step 1: Fetch a large git log (lots of commits = noisy output)
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Fetching git log for /data/repos/snake-game (max_count=50)')
    raw1 = call_tool('git_git_log', {'repo_path': repo_path, 'max_count': 50})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    log_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'Log data sample: {str(log_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Step 2: Extract signal — find commits whose message contains 'score', pick earliest (oldest)
    execution_log.append("Step 2: Filtering commits whose message contains 'score'")
    commits = log_data if isinstance(log_data, list) else log_data.get('commits', [])
    score_commits = []
    for c in commits:
        msg = c.get('message', '') if isinstance(c, dict) else ''
        if 'score' in msg.lower():
            score_commits.append(c)
    execution_log.append(f'Commits mentioning score: {len(score_commits)}')
    for sc in score_commits:
        execution_log.append(f"  SHA: {sc.get('hash', sc.get('sha', 'unknown'))}, Date: {sc.get('date', sc.get('authored_date', 'unknown'))}, Msg: {sc.get('message', '')[:80]}")

    # Pick the earliest (oldest) — last in the list if log is newest-first, or sort by date
    target_sha = 'unknown'
    if score_commits:
        # git log is ordered newest-first, so the last matching commit is the oldest
        oldest_commit = score_commits[-1]
        target_sha = oldest_commit.get('hash', oldest_commit.get('sha', 'unknown'))
    execution_log.append(f'Extracted signal: target_sha = {target_sha}')

    # Step 3: Check repo status (noisy intermediate step)
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Checking git status of repo (noisy/distracting step)')
    raw3 = call_tool('git_git_status', {'repo_path': repo_path})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw3)[:200]}')
    execution_log.append(f'Status result: {str(raw3)[:300]}')

    # Step 4: List branches (more noise)
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Listing branches (distracting noise)')
    raw4 = call_tool('git_git_branch', {'repo_path': repo_path, 'branch_type': 'all'})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw4)[:200]}')
    execution_log.append(f'Branches: {str(raw4)[:300]}')

    # Step 5: Use extracted signal — show the specific commit
    execution_log.append(f"Step 5: Using extracted signal '{target_sha}' from Step 2 — calling git_git_show")
    raw5 = call_tool('git_git_show', {'repo_path': repo_path, 'revision': target_sha})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}')
    execution_log.append(f'Full commit details: {str(raw5)[:1000]}')

    execution_log.append(f'\n=== FINAL ANSWER ===')
    execution_log.append(f'Target commit SHA (earliest commit with "score" in message): {target_sha}')
    execution_log.append(f'Commit details (files changed, diff): See git_git_show output above.')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Large git log with 20-50 commits containing many fields (SHA, author, date, message, stats), most commits do not mention 'score'; intermediate status/branch calls add further noise before the signal is used.

claims.description: Call git_git_log on /data/repos/snake-game with max_count=50, producing a large list of commit objects (SHA, author, date, message, stats) — mostly irrelevant commits.
claims.output_characteristics: 50 commit records, each with multiple fields; the vast majority do not contain 'score' in their message.

claims.description: Filter the commit list to find those whose message contains 'score'. Among all matches, identify the one with the oldest timestamp (earliest commit). Extract and remember its SHA.
claims.extraction_criteria: message contains 'score' (case-insensitive); if multiple, pick the one farthest back in history (last item when log is sorted newest-first).

claims.description: Call git_git_status on the repo — produces a status dump that is noisy/irrelevant but must be navigated past before using the extracted SHA.
Call git_git_branch to list all branches — another noisy intermediate step that does not contribute to the final answer.
Use the remembered SHA from step 2 to call git_git_show, retrieving the full commit details including changed files and diff.

_design_rationale: (1) Chain is long: 5 tool calls total — log fetch, signal extraction, status, branch list, then show — ensuring the agent must carry the SHA across multiple noisy steps. (2) Noise comes from: a 50-entry git log with many irrelevant commits, plus intermediate git_status and git_branch outputs that don't contribute to the answer. (3) Signal to extract: the SHA of the earliest (oldest) commit whose message contains 'score' — buried inside 50 commit records. (4) Signal usage: the SHA is only consumed at the final step (git_git_show), after two distracting intermediate tool calls. (5) If the agent forgets the SHA, picks the wrong commit (e.g., most recent instead of oldest), or misidentifies the filtering criterion, the git_git_show call will display the wrong commit diff — incorrect final answer. (6) Easy difficulty: linear A→B→C→D→E chain, single signal to track (one SHA), moderate noise volume (50 commits), no branching logic.

task: I'm a clinical research analyst investigating the oncology drug pipeline in relation to real-world clinical trial activity. Here's what I need you to do: First, look up the 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' Airtable base and list all records in the 'Oncology Trials' table. From that full list, identify the trial that has Status='Recruiting' AND Phase='Phase 3' AND TherapeuticArea='Lung Cancer' — extract its NCTNumber and Sponsor fields (there may be many records with noise; find the one matching all three conditions). Next, use that NCTNumber to fetch the full study details from ClinicalTrials.gov, and from those details extract: (a) the primary completion date, (b) the total enrollment count, and (c) the lead sponsor name. Then search ClinicalTrials.gov for all RECRUITING Phase 3 lung cancer studies sponsored by that same sponsor name, and get a count of how many such studies exist. After that, run a trend analysis on ClinicalTrials.gov for lung cancer trials grouped by sponsor type (countBySponsorType). Finally, search the 'Oncology Trials' Airtable table for all records where Sponsor matches the sponsor you extracted, count how many Airtable records exist for that sponsor, and use the calculator to compute: (total ClinicalTrials.gov enrollment count from step above) divided by (number of Airtable records for that sponsor). Report: the NCTNumber you found, the sponsor name, the primary completion date, the enrollment count, the number of recruiting Phase 3 lung cancer studies on ClinicalTrials.gov for that sponsor, the sponsor type breakdown from the trend analysis, the Airtable record count for that sponsor, and the final computed ratio.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: List all Airtable bases to find the Oncology Drug Pipeline base ID
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Listing all Airtable bases to find Oncology Drug Pipeline base_id')
    raw_bases = call_tool('airtable_list_bases', {})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_bases)[:200]}')
    bases_data = json.loads(raw_bases) if isinstance(raw_bases, str) else raw_bases
    execution_log.append(f'Bases data sample: {str(bases_data)[:300]}')

    # Extract Oncology Drug Pipeline base_id
    oncology_base_id = None
    bases_list = bases_data if isinstance(bases_data, list) else bases_data.get('bases', bases_data.get('data', []))
    for base in bases_list:
        base_name = base.get('name', '')
        if 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' in base_name:
            oncology_base_id = base.get('id', base.get('base_id', ''))
            execution_log.append(f'Found Oncology Drug Pipeline base_id: {oncology_base_id}')
            break
    if not oncology_base_id:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: Could not find Oncology Drug Pipeline base')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)

    # Step 2: List all records in Oncology Trials table (noisy - many records)
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Listing all records in Oncology Trials table (expecting large noisy output)')
    raw_records = call_tool('airtable_list_records', {'base_id': oncology_base_id, 'table_name': 'Oncology Trials'})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_records)[:200]}')
    records_data = json.loads(raw_records) if isinstance(raw_records, str) else raw_records
    execution_log.append(f'Records data sample: {str(records_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Step 3: Filter for Status=Recruiting, Phase=Phase 3, TherapeuticArea=Lung Cancer
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Filtering records for Status=Recruiting AND Phase=Phase 3 AND TherapeuticArea=Lung Cancer')
    records_list = records_data if isinstance(records_data, list) else records_data.get('records', records_data.get('data', []))
    execution_log.append(f'Total records returned: {len(records_list)}')

    target_nct = None
    target_sponsor = None
    for record in records_list:
        fields = record.get('fields', record)
        status = fields.get('Status', '')
        phase = fields.get('Phase', '')
        area = fields.get('TherapeuticArea', '')
        if status == 'Recruiting' and phase == 'Phase 3' and area == 'Lung Cancer':
            target_nct = fields.get('NCTNumber', '')
            target_sponsor = fields.get('Sponsor', '')
            execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL FOUND - NCTNumber: {target_nct}, Sponsor: {target_sponsor}')
            break

    if not target_nct:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: No matching record found with Status=Recruiting, Phase=Phase 3, TherapeuticArea=Lung Cancer')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)

    execution_log.append(f'Extracted NCTNumber: {target_nct}')
    execution_log.append(f'Extracted Sponsor: {target_sponsor}')

    # Step 4: Fetch full study details from ClinicalTrials.gov using the extracted NCTNumber
    execution_log.append(f'Step 4: Fetching ClinicalTrials.gov study details for {target_nct}')
    raw_study = call_tool('clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study', {'nctIds': target_nct, 'summaryOnly': False})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_study)[:200]}')
    study_data = json.loads(raw_study) if isinstance(raw_study, str) else raw_study
    execution_log.append(f'Study data sample: {str(study_data)[:500]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract primary completion date, enrollment, lead sponsor
    primary_completion_date = None
    enrollment_count = None
    lead_sponsor_name = None

    # Navigate nested structure defensively
    studies_list = study_data if isinstance(study_data, list) else study_data.get('studies', [study_data])
    if studies_list:
        s = studies_list[0] if isinstance(studies_list, list) else studies_list
        proto = s.get('protocolSection', {})
        status_module = proto.get('statusModule', {})
        primary_completion_date = status_module.get('primaryCompletionDateStruct', {}).get('date', status_module.get('primaryCompletionDate', 'unknown'))
        design_module = proto.get('designModule', {})
        enrollment_count = design_module.get('enrollmentInfo', {}).get('count', design_module.get('enrollment', {}).get('count', 'unknown'))
        id_module = proto.get('identificationModule', {})
        sponsor_module = proto.get('sponsorCollaboratorsModule', {})
        lead_sponsor_name = sponsor_module.get('leadSponsor', {}).get('name', 'unknown')
        execution_log.append(f'Primary Completion Date: {primary_completion_date}')
        execution_log.append(f'Enrollment Count: {enrollment_count}')
        execution_log.append(f'Lead Sponsor Name from ClinicalTrials.gov: {lead_sponsor_name}')

    if not lead_sponsor_name or lead_sponsor_name == 'unknown':
        lead_sponsor_name = target_sponsor
        execution_log.append(f'Falling back to Airtable sponsor: {lead_sponsor_name}')

    # Step 5: Search ClinicalTrials.gov for all RECRUITING Phase 3 lung cancer studies by same sponsor
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Searching ClinicalTrials.gov for RECRUITING Phase 3 lung cancer studies sponsored by {lead_sponsor_name}')
    raw_search = call_tool('clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_list_studies', {
        'query': {'cond': 'Lung Cancer', 'spons': lead_sponsor_name},
        'filter': {'overallStatus': ['RECRUITING'], 'advanced': 'AREA[Phase](PHASE3)'},
        'countTotal': True,
        'pageSize': 50
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_search)[:200]}')
    search_data = json.loads(raw_search) if isinstance(raw_search, str) else raw_search
    execution_log.append(f'Search data sample: {str(search_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    ct_recruiting_count = search_data.get('totalCount', search_data.get('total', len(search_data.get('studies', []))))
    execution_log.append(f'Total RECRUITING Phase 3 Lung Cancer studies for {lead_sponsor_name} on ClinicalTrials.gov: {ct_recruiting_count}')

    # Step 6: Run trend analysis on ClinicalTrials.gov for lung cancer by sponsor type
    execution_log.append('Step 6: Running trend analysis on ClinicalTrials.gov - lung cancer by sponsor type (countBySponsorType)')
    raw_trends = call_tool('clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends', {
        'query': {'cond': 'Lung Cancer'},
        'analysisType': 'countBySponsorType'
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_trends)[:200]}')
    trends_data = json.loads(raw_trends) if isinstance(raw_trends, str) else raw_trends
    execution_log.append(f'Trend analysis result: {str(trends_data)[:500]}')

    # Step 7: Search Airtable Oncology Trials for all records where Sponsor matches the extracted sponsor
    execution_log.append(f'Step 7: Searching Airtable Oncology Trials for Sponsor={target_sponsor}')
    raw_airtable_search = call_tool('airtable_search_records', {
        'base_id': oncology_base_id,
        'table_name': 'Oncology Trials',
        'field_name': 'Sponsor',
        'value': target_sponsor
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_airtable_search)[:200]}')
    airtable_search_data = json.loads(raw_airtable_search) if isinstance(raw_airtable_search, str) else raw_airtable_search
    execution_log.append(f'Airtable search result sample: {str(airtable_search_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    airtable_records_for_sponsor = airtable_search_data if isinstance(airtable_search_data, list) else airtable_search_data.get('records', airtable_search_data.get('data', []))
    airtable_sponsor_count = len(airtable_records_for_sponsor)
    execution_log.append(f'Number of Airtable records for sponsor {target_sponsor}: {airtable_sponsor_count}')

    # Step 8: Compute ratio: enrollment_count / airtable_sponsor_count
    execution_log.append(f'Step 8: Computing ratio = enrollment_count / airtable_sponsor_count = {enrollment_count} / {airtable_sponsor_count}')
    if airtable_sponsor_count > 0 and enrollment_count not in (None, 'unknown'):
        expression = f'{enrollment_count} / {airtable_sponsor_count}'
        raw_calc = call_tool('calculator_calculate', {'expression': expression})
        execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw_calc)[:200]}')
        calc_data = json.loads(raw_calc) if isinstance(raw_calc, str) else raw_calc
        ratio_result = calc_data.get('result', 'unknown') if isinstance(calc_data, dict) else str(calc_data)
        execution_log.append(f'Computed ratio: {ratio_result}')
    else:
        ratio_result = 'undefined (division by zero or unknown enrollment)'
        execution_log.append(f'Cannot compute ratio: airtable_sponsor_count={airtable_sponsor_count}, enrollment_count={enrollment_count}')

    # Final Summary
    execution_log.append('\n=== FINAL SUMMARY ===')
    execution_log.append(f'NCTNumber: {target_nct}')
    execution_log.append(f'Sponsor (Airtable): {target_sponsor}')
    execution_log.append(f'Lead Sponsor (ClinicalTrials.gov): {lead_sponsor_name}')
    execution_log.append(f'Primary Completion Date: {primary_completion_date}')
    execution_log.append(f'Enrollment Count: {enrollment_count}')
    execution_log.append(f'Recruiting Phase 3 Lung Cancer studies on ClinicalTrials.gov for this sponsor: {ct_recruiting_count}')
    execution_log.append(f'Sponsor Type Breakdown (Lung Cancer, all studies): {str(trends_data)[:300]}')
    execution_log.append(f'Airtable record count for sponsor {target_sponsor}: {airtable_sponsor_count}')
    execution_log.append(f'Computed ratio (enrollment / airtable_records): {ratio_result}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Airtable list_records returns all trials across all phases/statuses/areas (many records with irrelevant entries); ClinicalTrials.gov get_study returns deeply nested full study JSON with hundreds of fields; list_studies returns many studies with only one sponsor field of interest; analyze_trends returns aggregated stats across many sponsor types; airtable_search_records returns all records for the sponsor across all phases and statuses.

claims.description: Call airtable_list_bases to discover the base_id of 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' — returns list of all accessible bases, agent must identify the correct one by name.
claims.output_characteristics: List of bases with IDs and names; agent must filter to extract oncology base_id

claims.description: Call airtable_list_records on 'Oncology Trials' table — returns all records across all phases, statuses, and therapeutic areas (large noisy output)
claims.output_characteristics: 200+ records with mixed Phase/Status/TherapeuticArea/NCTNumber/Sponsor values; most records are irrelevant noise

claims.description: Filter the full record list to find the single record where Status='Recruiting' AND Phase='Phase 3' AND TherapeuticArea='Lung Cancer'; extract NCTNumber and Sponsor as key signals to carry forward
claims.extraction_criteria: Status == 'Recruiting' AND Phase == 'Phase 3' AND TherapeuticArea == 'Lung Cancer'
claims.signal_to_remember: NCTNumber (e.g., NCT########) and Sponsor name — both must be carried forward through multiple noisy steps

claims.description: Use extracted NCTNumber to call clinicaltrials_get_study — returns massive nested study JSON; extract primary completion date, enrollment count, and lead sponsor name from deeply nested fields
claims.output_characteristics: Full study JSON with hundreds of nested fields; primary completion date buried in statusModule, enrollment in designModule, lead sponsor in sponsorCollaboratorsModule

claims.description: Use extracted sponsor name to search ClinicalTrials.gov for all RECRUITING Phase 3 lung cancer studies by that sponsor; extract total count
claims.uses_signal: lead_sponsor_name from step 4 / Sponsor from step 3
claims.output_characteristics: Search results listing multiple studies; only totalCount is needed

claims.description: Run analyze_trends on ClinicalTrials.gov for lung cancer studies grouped by sponsor type (countBySponsorType) — returns aggregate breakdown across many sponsor type categories
claims.output_characteristics: Aggregated counts by sponsor type (NIH, Industry, Other, etc.) — large output with many categories; agent must record full breakdown

claims.description: Use extracted Sponsor from step 3 to search Airtable Oncology Trials for all records matching that sponsor; count the results
claims.uses_signal: Sponsor from step 3 AND base_id from step 1
claims.output_characteristics: Returns all records across all phases/statuses for that sponsor — agent must count them correctly

claims.description: Compute ratio: enrollment_count (from step 4) divided by airtable_sponsor_record_count (from step 7) using calculator
claims.uses_signal: enrollment_count from step 4 AND airtable_sponsor_count from step 7 — both signals carried across many noisy intermediate steps

_design_rationale: 1. WHY LONG CHAIN: The task requires 9+ tool calls across 3 distinct servers (Airtable, ClinicalTrials.gov, Calculator), with each server producing large, noisy outputs. The chain cannot be shortcut because each step's output feeds into the next. 2. WHERE NOISE COMES FROM: Airtable list_records returns all trials (many phases, statuses, areas) forcing the agent to scan and filter a large list. ClinicalTrials.gov get_study returns hundreds of nested JSON fields where the three needed values are buried. The list_studies and analyze_trends calls return many results where only counts/specific fields matter. The Airtable search_records returns multiple records across all phases for the sponsor. 3. WHAT SIGNAL MUST BE EXTRACTED: Two primary signals extracted early — NCTNumber and Sponsor — from step 3 filtering a large Airtable record set. Then two secondary signals — enrollment count and lead sponsor name — from deeply nested ClinicalTrials.gov JSON in step 4. 4. HOW SIGNAL IS USED LATER: NCTNumber is used in step 4 to fetch study details. Sponsor/lead_sponsor_name is used in step 5 (search) and step 7 (Airtable search). Enrollment count is used in step 8. Airtable sponsor count is used in step 8. 5. WHY LOSING SIGNAL CAUSES FAILURE: If the agent loses the NCTNumber after step 3, it cannot fetch the correct study. If it confuses the enrollment count with another study's count, the ratio is wrong. If it loses the sponsor name, steps 5 and 7 query the wrong entity. 6. WHY HARD: 9+ tool calls, 3 distinct server types, 4+ pieces of information to track simultaneously across very noisy intermediate outputs, signals extracted early must survive through multiple large-output steps before use.

task: I'm doing a multi-asset technical analysis comparing three tech stocks: AAPL, MSFT, and NVDA. Here is what I need: First, fetch the last 90 daily OHLCV bars for each of the three stocks (AAPL, MSFT, NVDA). From these 270 data points combined, identify which single stock had the highest single-day trading volume across all three series — record that stock's ticker symbol and the exact date of that maximum-volume day. Next, get the 14-period RSI for that identified stock (daily, last 30 data points). From the RSI series, find the most recent date on which the RSI value was below 35 (oversold) — record that date. Then fetch the MACD (default periods: fast=12, slow=26, signal=9) for that same stock, daily, for the last 60 data points. From the MACD series, find the most recent date on which the MACD histogram (macd_hist) crossed from negative to positive (i.e., the macd_hist value is positive on that date but was negative on the previous date in the series) — record that date. Finally, fetch the Bollinger Bands (20-period SMA, 2 std dev) for that same stock, daily, last 30 data points, and report: (1) the ticker of the identified stock, (2) the date of the maximum-volume day, (3) the most recent RSI-below-35 date, (4) the most recent MACD histogram positive-crossover date, and (5) the current upper Bollinger Band value (most recent data point's upper_band).

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: Fetch last 90 daily OHLCV bars for AAPL
    execution_log.append('Step 1a: Fetching 90 daily bars for AAPL')
    raw_aapl = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeries', {'params': {'symbol': 'AAPL', 'interval': '1day', 'outputsize': 90}})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW AAPL] {str(raw_aapl)[:200]}')
    data_aapl = json.loads(raw_aapl) if isinstance(raw_aapl, str) else raw_aapl
    aapl_values = data_aapl.get('values', []) if isinstance(data_aapl, dict) else []
    execution_log.append(f'AAPL bars count: {len(aapl_values)}')

    # Step 1b: Fetch last 90 daily OHLCV bars for MSFT
    execution_log.append('Step 1b: Fetching 90 daily bars for MSFT')
    raw_msft = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeries', {'params': {'symbol': 'MSFT', 'interval': '1day', 'outputsize': 90}})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW MSFT] {str(raw_msft)[:200]}')
    data_msft = json.loads(raw_msft) if isinstance(raw_msft, str) else raw_msft
    msft_values = data_msft.get('values', []) if isinstance(data_msft, dict) else []
    execution_log.append(f'MSFT bars count: {len(msft_values)}')

    # Step 1c: Fetch last 90 daily OHLCV bars for NVDA
    execution_log.append('Step 1c: Fetching 90 daily bars for NVDA')
    raw_nvda = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeries', {'params': {'symbol': 'NVDA', 'interval': '1day', 'outputsize': 90}})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW NVDA] {str(raw_nvda)[:200]}')
    data_nvda = json.loads(raw_nvda) if isinstance(raw_nvda, str) else raw_nvda
    nvda_values = data_nvda.get('values', []) if isinstance(data_nvda, dict) else []
    execution_log.append(f'NVDA bars count: {len(nvda_values)}')

    # Step 2: Find the stock+date with maximum single-day volume across all three
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Scanning all 270 bars to find maximum volume day')
    max_volume = -1
    max_ticker = None
    max_date = None

    for bar in aapl_values:
        vol_str = bar.get('volume', None)
        if vol_str is not None:
            try:
                vol = float(vol_str)
                if vol > max_volume:
                    max_volume = vol
                    max_ticker = 'AAPL'
                    max_date = bar.get('datetime', 'unknown')
            except (ValueError, TypeError):
                pass

    for bar in msft_values:
        vol_str = bar.get('volume', None)
        if vol_str is not None:
            try:
                vol = float(vol_str)
                if vol > max_volume:
                    max_volume = vol
                    max_ticker = 'MSFT'
                    max_date = bar.get('datetime', 'unknown')
            except (ValueError, TypeError):
                pass

    for bar in nvda_values:
        vol_str = bar.get('volume', None)
        if vol_str is not None:
            try:
                vol = float(vol_str)
                if vol > max_volume:
                    max_volume = vol
                    max_ticker = 'NVDA'
                    max_date = bar.get('datetime', 'unknown')
            except (ValueError, TypeError):
                pass

    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED: Max volume ticker = {max_ticker}, date = {max_date}, volume = {max_volume}')

    # Step 3: Fetch 14-period RSI for the identified stock (last 30 data points)
    execution_log.append(f'Step 3: Fetching RSI (14-period, daily, 30 bars) for {max_ticker}')
    raw_rsi = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi', {'params': {'symbol': max_ticker, 'interval': '1day', 'outputsize': 30, 'time_period': 14}})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW RSI] {str(raw_rsi)[:200]}')
    data_rsi = json.loads(raw_rsi) if isinstance(raw_rsi, str) else raw_rsi
    rsi_values = data_rsi.get('values', []) if isinstance(data_rsi, dict) else []
    execution_log.append(f'RSI series count: {len(rsi_values)}')
    for rv in rsi_values[:5]:
        execution_log.append(f'  RSI sample: {rv}')

    # Step 4: Find most recent RSI < 35 date
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Scanning RSI series for most recent date with RSI < 35')
    rsi_below_35_date = None
    # values are ordered descending (most recent first)
    for rv in rsi_values:
        rsi_val_str = rv.get('rsi', None)
        if rsi_val_str is not None:
            try:
                rsi_val = float(rsi_val_str)
                if rsi_val < 35:
                    rsi_below_35_date = rv.get('datetime', 'unknown')
                    break
            except (ValueError, TypeError):
                pass
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL: Most recent RSI < 35 date for {max_ticker}: {rsi_below_35_date}')

    # Step 5: Fetch MACD (12,26,9) for the identified stock (last 60 data points)
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Fetching MACD (12,26,9, daily, 60 bars) for {max_ticker}')
    raw_macd = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd', {'params': {'symbol': max_ticker, 'interval': '1day', 'outputsize': 60, 'fast_period': 12, 'slow_period': 26, 'signal_period': 9}})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW MACD] {str(raw_macd)[:200]}')
    data_macd = json.loads(raw_macd) if isinstance(raw_macd, str) else raw_macd
    macd_values = data_macd.get('values', []) if isinstance(data_macd, dict) else []
    execution_log.append(f'MACD series count: {len(macd_values)}')
    for mv in macd_values[:3]:
        execution_log.append(f'  MACD sample: {mv}')

    # Step 6: Find most recent MACD histogram positive crossover
    # Values are ordered descending (most recent first), so index 0 is latest
    execution_log.append('Step 6: Scanning MACD histogram for most recent positive crossover')
    macd_crossover_date = None
    # Need at least 2 consecutive points: index i (current) positive, index i+1 (previous in time) negative
    for i in range(len(macd_values) - 1):
        curr = macd_values[i]
        prev = macd_values[i + 1]
        curr_hist_str = curr.get('macd_hist', None)
        prev_hist_str = prev.get('macd_hist', None)
        if curr_hist_str is not None and prev_hist_str is not None:
            try:
                curr_hist = float(curr_hist_str)
                prev_hist = float(prev_hist_str)
                if curr_hist > 0 and prev_hist < 0:
                    macd_crossover_date = curr.get('datetime', 'unknown')
                    execution_log.append(f'  Found crossover at index {i}: curr_hist={curr_hist}, prev_hist={prev_hist}, date={macd_crossover_date}')
                    break
            except (ValueError, TypeError):
                pass
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL: Most recent MACD histogram positive crossover date: {macd_crossover_date}')

    # Step 7: Fetch Bollinger Bands (20-period, 2 std dev, daily, last 30 bars) for the identified stock
    execution_log.append(f'Step 7: Fetching Bollinger Bands (20-period, 2sd, daily, 30 bars) for {max_ticker}')
    raw_bb = call_tool('twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesBBands', {'params': {'symbol': max_ticker, 'interval': '1day', 'outputsize': 30, 'time_period': 20, 'sd': 2}})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW BB] {str(raw_bb)[:200]}')
    data_bb = json.loads(raw_bb) if isinstance(raw_bb, str) else raw_bb
    bb_values = data_bb.get('values', []) if isinstance(data_bb, dict) else []
    execution_log.append(f'BB series count: {len(bb_values)}')

    # Most recent upper band = first entry (descending order)
    current_upper_band = None
    if bb_values:
        current_upper_band = bb_values[0].get('upper_band', None)
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL: Current upper Bollinger Band for {max_ticker}: {current_upper_band}')

    # Final consolidated answer
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL ANSWER ===')
    execution_log.append(f'(1) Identified stock (highest single-day volume across AAPL/MSFT/NVDA in last 90 days): {max_ticker}')
    execution_log.append(f'(2) Date of maximum-volume day: {max_date}')
    execution_log.append(f'(3) Most recent date RSI(14) was below 35 for {max_ticker}: {rsi_below_35_date}')
    execution_log.append(f'(4) Most recent date MACD histogram crossed from negative to positive for {max_ticker}: {macd_crossover_date}')
    execution_log.append(f'(5) Current upper Bollinger Band (20-period, 2sd) for {max_ticker}: {current_upper_band}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Each GetTimeSeries call returns 90 OHLCV bars (90 objects each with datetime, open, high, low, close, volume). Agent must scan all 270 rows across three calls to find the single maximum-volume entry. RSI returns 30 rows, MACD returns 60 rows with three values each (macd, macd_signal, macd_hist), BBands returns 30 rows with three band values. Total: 480+ data points to process. All later analysis depends on the ticker identified in step 2.

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeries for AAPL with outputsize=90 and interval=1day. Returns 90 OHLCV bars (large noisy array with datetime, open, high, low, close, volume per bar).
claims.output_characteristics: 90 items, each with 6 fields including irrelevant open/high/low/close; only volume and datetime matter here

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeries for MSFT with outputsize=90. Another 90 noisy OHLCV bars.
claims.output_characteristics: 90 items, same structure as step 1

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeries for NVDA with outputsize=90. Another 90 noisy OHLCV bars.
claims.output_characteristics: 90 items, same structure — agent must now scan all 270 combined records

claims.description: Extract signal: scan all 270 bars across AAPL/MSFT/NVDA, find the single highest volume bar, record its ticker (e.g. NVDA) and date. THIS IS THE PRIMARY SIGNAL that drives all subsequent analysis.
claims.extraction_criteria: Compare float(bar['volume']) across all 270 bars; keep track of ticker and datetime for maximum

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeriesRsi for the identified max_ticker stock (daily, 14-period, 30 bars). Returns 30 RSI values with datetime and rsi fields.
claims.output_characteristics: 30 items, only 2 fields each (datetime, rsi). Agent must filter for rsi < 35 and find most recent such date.

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeriesMacd for the same max_ticker stock (daily, fast=12, slow=26, signal=9, 60 bars). Returns 60 rows with macd, macd_signal, macd_hist fields — agent must scan for hist crossing from negative to positive.
claims.output_characteristics: 60 items with 4 fields each (datetime, macd, macd_signal, macd_hist). Agent must compare consecutive macd_hist pairs to detect crossover, not just find positive values.

claims.description: Call GetTimeSeriesBBands for the same max_ticker stock (daily, 20-period, 2 std dev, 30 bars). Returns 30 rows with upper_band, middle_band, lower_band. Extract most recent upper_band value.
claims.output_characteristics: 30 items with upper_band, middle_band, lower_band — agent must extract only the first entry's upper_band

claims.description: Compile all five extracted values into final answer: ticker, max-volume date, RSI-below-35 date, MACD crossover date, current upper Bollinger Band.

_design_rationale: 1) WHY CHAIN IS LONG: The task requires 7 tool calls minimum — 3x GetTimeSeries (one per stock) + GetTimeSeriesRsi + GetTimeSeriesMacd + GetTimeSeriesBBands + potential extra calls, reaching 9+ total operations. 2) WHERE NOISE COMES FROM: Each GetTimeSeries call returns 90 OHLCV bars (6 fields each = 540 values per call, 1620 across three calls). RSI adds 30 rows, MACD adds 60 rows with 3 indicators each, BBands adds 30 rows with 3 bands each — totaling 480+ data points after the initial 270. Most fields (open, high, low, close, middle_band, lower_band, macd, macd_signal) are irrelevant noise. 3) WHAT SIGNAL MUST BE EXTRACTED: From 270 noisy OHLCV bars, the agent must identify which specific stock (AAPL/MSFT/NVDA) had the single highest trading volume day and on what date. This requires comparing 270 rows across 3 separate API responses. 4) HOW SIGNAL IS USED LATER: The identified ticker (e.g., 'NVDA') becomes the input for ALL four subsequent technical indicator calls. If the agent picks the wrong stock in step 4, every subsequent analysis (RSI, MACD, BBands) will be for the wrong instrument. 5) WHY LOSING SIGNAL CAUSES FAILURE: If the agent confuses AAPL for NVDA (or forgets the max-volume ticker midway), all remaining 4 tool calls query the wrong stock, producing a completely wrong final answer. The RSI-below-35 date, MACD crossover date, and upper Bollinger Band value will all be wrong. 6) WHY THIS MATCHES HARD DIFFICULTY: 9+ tool calls (3 OHLCV + 1 RSI + 1 MACD + 1 BBands = 7 minimum, often more with retries), 480+ total data points to process, requires aggregating across 3 separate API responses before extracting the primary signal, then tracking that signal through 4 more noisy API calls with different data structures (RSI, MACD with 3 sub-values, BBands with 3 bands). Additionally, the MACD crossover detection requires comparing consecutive pairs of values (not just finding a threshold), adding further complexity.

task: I'm doing a code quality audit of the `storyteller` repository at `/data/repos/storyteller`. I need to find out: (1) which branch other than `main` exists in that repo, (2) look at all commits on that branch and identify the SHA of the commit whose message contains the word 'fix' (case-insensitive) — if multiple match, take the most recent one, (3) get the full diff of that specific commit to see what files were changed, (4) for each source file changed in that commit, read its current content from the filesystem under `/data/repos/storyteller`, and (5) run a Python script that counts the total number of lines across all those changed source files (excluding test files, i.e., files whose path contains 'test' or 'spec') and prints the result. Report the fix-commit SHA, the list of changed non-test source files, and the total line count.

state_assertions.description: Output file /data/storyteller_audit.txt was created by the agent
Output file is non-empty (at least 50 bytes)
Output file contains a commit SHA (40 hex characters)
Output file contains a numeric line count result
Output file references at least one source file path

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: git_git_log returns many commits with full metadata (author, date, message, SHA) — agent must scan all commits to find the one whose message contains 'fix'; git_git_show returns a large diff with many hunks and context lines across possibly many files — agent must filter out test/spec files; cli-mcp-server_run_command directory listing returns many files with noise.

claims.description: List all branches in /data/repos/storyteller to find the non-main branch name
claims.output_characteristics: Returns list of branches; agent must identify the branch that is not 'main'

claims.description: Get all commits on the discovered non-main branch with a high max_count to ensure coverage
claims.output_characteristics: Returns 20-50+ commits with SHA, author, date, and message for each — large noisy output; agent must scan messages for the word 'fix' (case-insensitive) and extract the SHA of the most recent matching commit

claims.description: Extract and remember the SHA of the most recent fix-related commit from step 2's output
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter commits whose message contains 'fix' (case-insensitive), pick the most recent (first in log order), extract its full SHA

claims.description: Show the full diff of the fix commit using the remembered SHA
claims.output_characteristics: Returns full commit diff — potentially hundreds of lines of added/removed code across multiple files; agent must extract the list of changed file paths and filter out any with 'test' or 'spec' in the path

claims.description: For each non-test changed file, read its current content from /data/repos/storyteller using the filesystem
claims.output_characteristics: cat of each file returns full source content — potentially many lines per file; agent must read each file and prepare for line counting

claims.description: Run a Python script that counts total lines across all non-test changed source files and writes results to /data/storyteller_audit.txt
claims.uses_signal: fix_commit_sha and list of non-test changed file paths

_design_rationale: (1) Chain is long because it requires: branch discovery → full commit log scan → signal extraction → full diff retrieval → file reading → code execution, totaling 6+ tool calls with a mix of git, filesystem, and code executor tools. (2) Noise comes from: git_git_log returning dozens of commits each with full metadata (the agent must scan all messages to find 'fix' commits), git_git_show returning a large diff with many context lines and potentially many changed files (agent must filter test files), and file cat operations returning full source code. (3) The critical signal is the SHA of the most recent fix-commit, which is buried among many commit messages in the log output. (4) That SHA must be remembered and passed to git_git_show several steps later; additionally the list of non-test changed files extracted from the diff must be remembered for the final counting step. (5) Losing the correct SHA means showing the wrong commit's diff → wrong file list → wrong line count. Failing to filter test files means over-counting lines. (6) This matches Medium difficulty: 6+ tool calls, 2-3 pieces of information to track (branch name, fix SHA, file list), and outputs of moderate noise level (dozens of commits, one large diff, several files).

task: I'm planning a trip to Yellowstone National Park and want to know if it's safe to visit right now. First, find the park code for Yellowstone by searching for national parks in Wyoming (state code 'WY'). Then get the current alerts for Yellowstone using its park code — there may be many alerts listed, but I only need the title of the FIRST alert in the list. Next, get the coordinates of Yellowstone from its detailed park information, and use those coordinates to get the current weather at the park. Finally, tell me: (1) the title of the first active alert at Yellowstone, and (2) the current temperature and weather condition at the park.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: Find parks in Wyoming to get Yellowstone's park code
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Searching for national parks in Wyoming (stateCode=WY)')
    raw1 = call_tool('national-parks_findParks', {'stateCode': 'WY', 'limit': 20})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    parks_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'Parks data sample: {str(parks_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract Yellowstone park code from the list of parks
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Extracting Yellowstone park code from noisy parks list...')
    parks_list = parks_data.get('data', parks_data if isinstance(parks_data, list) else [])
    yellowstone_code = None
    for park in parks_list:
        name = park.get('name', park.get('fullName', ''))
        if 'yellowstone' in name.lower():
            yellowstone_code = park.get('parkCode', park.get('id', ''))
            execution_log.append(f'Found Yellowstone: name={name}, parkCode={yellowstone_code}')
            break
    if not yellowstone_code:
        yellowstone_code = 'yell'
        execution_log.append(f'Yellowstone not found in list, using default parkCode: {yellowstone_code}')
    else:
        execution_log.append(f'Extracted signal - Yellowstone park code: {yellowstone_code}')

    # Step 3: Get alerts for Yellowstone (large noisy output with many alerts)
    execution_log.append(f'Step 3: Getting alerts for Yellowstone (parkCode={yellowstone_code})')
    raw3 = call_tool('national-parks_getAlerts', {'parkCode': yellowstone_code, 'limit': 50})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw3)[:200]}')
    alerts_data = json.loads(raw3) if isinstance(raw3, str) else raw3
    execution_log.append(f'Alerts data sample: {str(alerts_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract the FIRST alert title from the noisy list
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Extracting FIRST alert title from list of many alerts...')
    alerts_list = alerts_data.get('data', alerts_data if isinstance(alerts_data, list) else [])
    first_alert_title = 'No alerts found'
    if alerts_list:
        first_alert = alerts_list[0]
        first_alert_title = first_alert.get('title', first_alert.get('name', 'Unknown alert'))
        execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL - First alert title: "{first_alert_title}"')
        execution_log.append(f'(Other alerts present: {len(alerts_list)-1} more alerts — ignored)')
    else:
        execution_log.append('No alerts found for Yellowstone')

    # Step 5: Get Yellowstone park details to extract coordinates
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Getting park details for Yellowstone (parkCode={yellowstone_code})')
    raw5 = call_tool('national-parks_getParkDetails', {'parkCode': yellowstone_code})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}')
    details_data = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    execution_log.append(f'Park details sample: {str(details_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract coordinates from park details
    execution_log.append('Step 6: Extracting coordinates from park details...')
    park_detail = details_data.get('data', details_data)
    if isinstance(park_detail, list):
        park_detail = park_detail[0] if park_detail else {}
    lat = park_detail.get('latitude', park_detail.get('lat', '44.4280'))
    lon = park_detail.get('longitude', park_detail.get('lon', park_detail.get('long', '-110.5885')))
    try:
        lat_f = float(lat)
        lon_f = float(lon)
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        lat_f = 44.4280
        lon_f = -110.5885
    location_str = f'{lat_f},{lon_f}'
    execution_log.append(f'Extracted Yellowstone coordinates: lat={lat_f}, lon={lon_f}')
    execution_log.append(f'Location string for weather: {location_str}')

    # Step 7: Get current weather at Yellowstone using coordinates
    execution_log.append(f'Step 7: Getting current weather at Yellowstone coordinates ({location_str})')
    raw7 = call_tool('weather_get_current_weather', {'location': location_str})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw7)[:200]}')
    weather_data = json.loads(raw7) if isinstance(raw7, str) else raw7
    execution_log.append(f'Weather data: {str(weather_data)[:400]}')

    # Extract temperature and conditions
    execution_log.append('Step 8: Extracting temperature and weather condition from weather data...')
    properties = weather_data.get('properties', weather_data) if isinstance(weather_data, dict) else {}
    periods = properties.get('periods', [])
    if periods:
        current = periods[0]
        temperature = current.get('temperature', 'unknown')
        temp_unit = current.get('temperatureUnit', 'F')
        short_forecast = current.get('shortForecast', current.get('conditions', 'unknown'))
        execution_log.append(f'Current temperature: {temperature}°{temp_unit}')
        execution_log.append(f'Current conditions: {short_forecast}')
    else:
        temperature = weather_data.get('temperature', weather_data.get('temp_f', weather_data.get('temp_c', 'unknown')))
        temp_unit = weather_data.get('temperatureUnit', weather_data.get('temp_unit', 'F'))
        short_forecast = weather_data.get('shortForecast', weather_data.get('conditions', weather_data.get('description', 'unknown')))
        execution_log.append(f'Current temperature: {temperature}°{temp_unit}')
        execution_log.append(f'Current conditions: {short_forecast}')

    # Final answer
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL ANSWER ===')
    execution_log.append(f'(1) First active alert at Yellowstone: "{first_alert_title}"')
    execution_log.append(f'(2) Current weather at Yellowstone: Temperature = {temperature}°{temp_unit}, Conditions = {short_forecast}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: findParks returns many parks in Wyoming with extensive metadata; getAlerts returns up to 50 alerts with titles, descriptions, categories, URLs, and park-specific details — agent must extract only the FIRST alert title and ignore the rest; getParkDetails returns verbose park object with dozens of fields from which only lat/lon coordinates are needed.

claims.description: Search for national parks in Wyoming using stateCode='WY', returns large list of parks with extensive metadata
claims.output_characteristics: List of 10-20 parks, each with parkCode, name, fullName, description, states, latLong, activities, topics, images, contacts, entranceFees, operatingHours — dozens of fields per park

claims.description: Extract Yellowstone's park code from the noisy parks list by filtering for the park named 'Yellowstone'
claims.extraction_criteria: Find park where name contains 'Yellowstone', extract its parkCode field

claims.description: Get alerts for Yellowstone using the extracted park code — returns many alerts with full descriptions
claims.output_characteristics: List of up to 50 alerts each with title, description, category, url, parkCode — only the FIRST alert's title matters

claims.description: Extract the title of the FIRST alert from the large alerts list
claims.extraction_criteria: Take alerts[0].title only — all other alerts and fields are noise

claims.description: Get full park details for Yellowstone to find its GPS coordinates
claims.output_characteristics: Verbose park object with 30+ fields — only latitude and longitude are needed

claims.description: Extract latitude and longitude from verbose park detail response
claims.extraction_criteria: Get park.latitude and park.longitude fields, form coordinate string 'lat,lon'

claims.description: Use extracted coordinates to get current weather at Yellowstone
claims.output_characteristics: Weather object with temperature, temperatureUnit, shortForecast, wind, humidity and many other fields

claims.description: Report final answer: first alert title (from step 4) and current temperature + condition (from step 7)

_design_rationale: 1) WHY CHAIN IS LONG: A realistic trip-safety check requires: finding the park → getting its alerts → getting its location → getting weather. This naturally produces 4+ tool calls with logical dependencies. 2) WHERE NOISE COMES FROM: findParks returns many Wyoming parks with extensive metadata; getAlerts returns up to 50 alerts with full descriptions; getParkDetails returns a large park object with 30+ fields. 3) WHAT SIGNAL MUST BE EXTRACTED: (a) Yellowstone's parkCode from step 1 — needed to call getAlerts and getParkDetails; (b) the FIRST alert title from step 3's large alert list; (c) lat/lon coordinates from step 5's verbose park details. 4) HOW SIGNAL IS USED LATER: The parkCode is used immediately in steps 3 and 5; the coordinates extracted in step 6 are used in step 7 for weather; the first alert title from step 4 is combined with step 7's weather data for the final answer. 5) WHY LOSING SIGNAL CAUSES FAILURE: If the agent picks the wrong alert (e.g., alert #3 instead of #1), the alert title answer is wrong. If the agent loses the coordinates and guesses, the weather data is wrong. 6) WHY THIS MATCHES EASY DIFFICULTY: Linear chain A→B→C→D→E with moderate outputs, 2 signals to track (first alert title, coordinates), straightforward filtering — fits the 4-5 tool call Easy profile.

task: I need a comprehensive research report on transformer-based models for biomedical NLP. Please do the following: (1) Search arXiv for 'transformer biomedical NLP clinical text' (max 50 results, category cs.CL, from 2023-01-01 to 2024-12-31) and identify the paper with the highest number of authors listed in its author field — remember its arXiv paper ID. (2) Also search PubMed for 'transformer model biomedical named entity recognition' (30 results) and identify the PMID of the most recently published paper among the results — remember this PMID. (3) Download the arXiv paper you identified in step 1 and read its full content. (4) Retrieve full metadata from PubMed for the PMID you identified in step 2. (5) Now search arXiv again for 'biomedical named entity recognition BERT' (max 30 results, category cs.CL) and among those results find the paper whose title contains the word 'clinical' — remember its arXiv paper ID. (6) Download and read that second arXiv paper. (7) Finally, create a directory /data/biomedical_nlp_report and write a file /data/biomedical_nlp_report/synthesis.md that contains: the arXiv ID of the first paper (most authors), the PMID of the most recent PubMed paper, the arXiv ID of the second paper (title contains 'clinical'), a 3-sentence summary of each of the two arXiv papers based on their content, and the journal name and publication year of the PubMed paper from its metadata.

state_assertions.description: Directory /data/biomedical_nlp_report exists
Synthesis file /data/biomedical_nlp_report/synthesis.md exists and is non-empty
Synthesis file contains an arXiv paper ID pattern (e.g. 2301.XXXXX or 2312.XXXXX format)
Synthesis file contains a PMID (a numeric identifier for the PubMed paper)
Synthesis file contains summary text referencing biomedical or clinical NLP content
state_assertions.code: any(kw in open('/data/biomedical_nlp_report/synthesis.md').read().lower() for kw in ['transformer', 'biomedical', 'clinical', 'named entity', 'bert'])

state_assertions.description: Synthesis file contains a journal name or publication year for the PubMed paper

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: arXiv search returns up to 50 papers with full metadata (authors, abstracts, categories, dates, DOIs); PubMed search returns 30 papers with verbose metadata; paper full-text reads return thousands of lines of markdown; agent must extract and track 3 distinct IDs across multiple noisy steps before using them in the final file write.

claims.description: Search arXiv for 'transformer biomedical NLP clinical text' (cs.CL, 2023-2024, max 50 results). Output contains ~50 paper records each with title, authors list, abstract, ID, date. Must find the paper with the most authors and extract its arXiv ID.
claims.output_characteristics: 50 paper records, each with verbose metadata including full author lists, abstracts, categories, submission dates — very noisy, must count authors per paper to find max.

claims.description: Extract signal 1: arXiv ID of the paper with the most authors from the 50 results in step 1. This ID must be remembered for use in steps 3 and 7.
claims.extraction_criteria: Count number of authors for each paper result, identify paper with maximum author count, extract its arXiv paper_id field.

claims.description: Search PubMed for 'transformer model biomedical named entity recognition' (30 results). Returns 30 paper records with titles, PMIDs, publication dates, journal info. Must identify most recently published paper by date and extract its PMID.
claims.output_characteristics: 30 PubMed records with verbose metadata including abstracts, authors, MeSH terms, dates — noisy, must sort by publication date to find most recent.

claims.description: Extract signal 2: PMID of the most recently published paper from the 30 PubMed results in step 3. This PMID must be remembered for use in steps 8 and 9.
claims.extraction_criteria: Compare publication dates across all 30 results, identify the most recent one, extract its PMID.

claims.description: Download the arXiv paper identified by arxiv_id_most_authors (signal 1 from step 2). Produces a resource with full paper content.
Read the full content of the downloaded arXiv paper (arxiv_id_most_authors). Produces thousands of lines of markdown text — very noisy. Must extract key themes for summary.
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper text in markdown, typically 3000-8000 lines including references, equations, tables — extremely noisy.

claims.description: Retrieve full PubMed metadata for the PMID identified in step 4 (most_recent_pmid). Returns verbose XML/JSON metadata including journal, volume, issue, authors, MeSH terms, grants, abstracts. Must extract journal name and publication year.
claims.output_characteristics: Verbose metadata object with 30+ fields, must extract journal name and year from nested structure.

claims.description: Search arXiv again for 'biomedical named entity recognition BERT' (cs.CL, max 30 results). Returns 30 paper records. Must find the paper whose title contains the word 'clinical' and extract its arXiv ID.
claims.output_characteristics: 30 paper records with titles, abstracts, author lists — must filter by title keyword 'clinical' to extract third signal.

claims.description: Extract signal 3: arXiv ID of the paper from step 8 whose title contains 'clinical'. Remember for steps 10 and 11.
claims.extraction_criteria: Scan all 30 titles for the word 'clinical' (case-insensitive), extract the arXiv paper_id of the matching paper.

claims.description: Download and read the second arXiv paper (arxiv_id_clinical_title). Download produces resource, read returns full markdown text. Must extract key themes for summary.
Read full content of second arXiv paper. Produces large noisy markdown output. Must extract 3-sentence summary.
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper text in markdown, thousands of lines.

claims.description: Create directory /data/biomedical_nlp_report and write synthesis.md containing all 3 signals (arxiv_id_most_authors, most_recent_pmid, arxiv_id_clinical_title) plus summaries of both arXiv papers and the PubMed paper's journal name and year.
Write the synthesis.md file with all required content.

_design_rationale: 1. **Why chain is long**: The task requires 9+ tool calls: 2 arXiv searches, 2 arXiv downloads, 2 arXiv reads, 1 PubMed search, 1 PubMed metadata fetch, 1 directory creation, 1-2 file writes — totaling 11-13 tool calls. 2. **Where noise comes from**: arXiv searches return up to 50 papers each with full author lists, abstracts, categories, and dates. PubMed returns 30 papers with verbose metadata including MeSH terms, grants, and citations. Full paper reads return thousands of markdown lines including equations and references. 3. **What signals must be extracted**: Three distinct IDs must be extracted across different steps: (a) the arXiv ID of the paper with most authors (requires counting authors across 50 results), (b) the PMID of the most recently published PubMed paper (requires sorting 30 results by date), (c) the arXiv ID of the paper with 'clinical' in its title (requires filtering 30 results by title keyword). 4. **How signals are used later**: Signal 1 (arXiv ID) is used to download and read a specific paper. Signal 2 (PMID) is used to fetch detailed metadata. Signal 3 (arXiv ID) is used to download and read another specific paper. All three signals plus extracted content are used in the final file write at step 12-13. 5. **Why losing signal causes failure**: If the agent forgets or incorrectly identifies any of the three IDs, it will download and summarize the wrong paper, or write incorrect IDs to the synthesis file, or fail to fetch the correct PubMed metadata — causing state assertions to fail or produce wrong content. 6. **Why this matches Hard difficulty**: 9+ tool calls, 3 distinct servers (arXiv, PubMed, filesystem), 3 signals to extract and track simultaneously across many noisy intermediate steps, with each signal buried in large volumes of data requiring different filtering strategies (count-based, date-based, keyword-based).

task: I need a comprehensive analysis of the `storyteller` repository at `/data/repos/storyteller`. Here is what I want you to do: First, explore the repository structure and read the full commit history (get at least 30 commits). From that commit history, identify the SHA of the OLDEST commit (the very first commit ever made to the repo). Then, examine the list of all branches. Next, look at the full git log for the repository and find the commit message of the second-most-recent commit (not the latest, but the one just before it) — remember this message exactly. After that, retrieve the detailed diff of the OLDEST commit you found earlier, and count how many files were added or modified in that very first commit. Then look up the current open issues in the `storyteller` repository on GitHub (owner: `NativeScripting`) and find the issue with the highest issue number among all open issues — record its title. Finally, write a Python script to `/data/storyteller_analysis.py` that prints a summary report containing: (1) the SHA of the oldest commit, (2) the commit message of the second-most-recent commit, (3) the number of files changed in the oldest commit, and (4) the title of the open GitHub issue with the highest issue number. Execute that script and make sure it prints all four pieces of information.

state_assertions.description: The output script /data/storyteller_analysis.py exists
The script file is non-empty (at least 100 bytes)
The script contains a print statement for the oldest commit SHA
The script references oldest commit SHA (a git-style hex hash pattern is present in the file)
The script contains the second-most-recent commit message as a string literal

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: git log returns 30+ commits with full metadata (author, date, hash, message) — agent must identify oldest (last) commit SHA; git show of oldest commit lists all file diffs with patch hunks; GitHub list_issues returns many issues with full metadata across pages — agent must find highest issue number; all four pieces of extracted information must be remembered and assembled into the final script.

claims.description: Run git_git_log on /data/repos/storyteller with max_count=50 to retrieve full commit history. Output is 30-50 commit entries each with SHA, author, date, message — very noisy.
claims.output_characteristics: 50 commit records with full metadata, agent must scroll to the very last entry to find the oldest commit SHA

claims.description: Extract two signals from the log: (A) the SHA of the OLDEST (last/first-ever) commit, and (B) the commit message of the SECOND-MOST-RECENT commit (index 1 from top). Both are buried in a noisy list of 50 commits.
claims.extraction_criteria: Signal A = SHA at the bottom of the log (oldest commit). Signal B = message of the second entry from the top (second-most-recent).

claims.description: Run git_git_branch on /data/repos/storyteller to list all branches (local or all). This produces a noisy list of branch names but is needed for context/verification.
claims.output_characteristics: List of branch names — some may be feature branches with long names

claims.description: Run git_git_show on /data/repos/storyteller with revision = oldest_commit_sha (from step 2). This returns the full diff of the first commit — a large patch with all initially added files.
claims.output_characteristics: Full patch output with diff hunks for every file added in the first commit — potentially hundreds of lines

claims.description: From the git show output, count how many files were added or modified in the oldest commit (look for 'diff --git' lines or '+++ b/' lines). Extract signal C = file count.
claims.extraction_criteria: Count occurrences of 'diff --git' in the patch output.

claims.description: Call github_list_issues for owner=NativeScripting, repo=storyteller, state=open, per_page=100 to retrieve all open issues. Output is a large list of issue objects with many metadata fields.
claims.output_characteristics: Up to 100 issue records each with number, title, body, labels, assignees, created_at, updated_at, comments count — agent must find the one with the highest issue number

claims.description: Extract signal D = title of the open issue with the highest issue number from the github_list_issues output. This requires scanning all issue numbers across a large noisy list.
claims.extraction_criteria: Find max(issue.number) among all returned open issues, record its title.

claims.description: Initialize a Python script file at /data/storyteller_analysis.py using mcp-code-executor_initialize_code_file. The script must embed all four remembered signals: oldest_commit_sha (A), second_most_recent_commit_message (B), oldest_commit_file_count (C), highest_open_issue_title (D) and print them as a summary report.
Execute the script at /data/storyteller_analysis.py using mcp-code-executor_execute_code_file and verify it prints all four pieces of information correctly.

_design_rationale: 1) Chain length: The task requires 9+ tool calls across 3 distinct servers (git MCP, GitHub MCP, code-executor MCP), well above the Hard threshold. 2) Noise sources: git_git_log with 50 commits returns dense metadata for each commit (sha, author, date, message) — agent must identify BOTH the very last entry (oldest) AND the second entry (second-most-recent message). git_git_show of the first commit produces a full patch with potentially hundreds of diff lines. github_list_issues returns 100 issues per page with ~20 fields each. All three steps flood the context with irrelevant data. 3) Signal extraction: Four distinct pieces of information must be extracted from three different noisy outputs at steps 2, 5, and 7, spread across the chain. 4) Signal usage: All four signals are only used at step 8 (script creation) and step 9 (execution), 6-7 steps after extraction. 5) Failure mode: If agent misidentifies the oldest commit (e.g., picks a recent one), the git show diff will be wrong and the file count will be wrong. If agent picks the most recent commit message instead of the second, signal B is wrong. If agent misses the highest-numbered issue, signal D is wrong. All four signals must be correct for the script to print the right summary. 6) Difficulty justification: Hard because 9+ calls, 3 servers, 4 signals to track simultaneously across a long noisy chain, and the stateful output (the script file) must embed all of them correctly.

task: I'm planning a camping trip to a national park in Colorado and want to visit a specific park that has the highest number of campgrounds available. Search for all national parks in Colorado, then get the campground details for each park you find. Among all parks with at least one campground, identify the park that has the most campgrounds listed. Then get the full park details for that top-campground park and note its official park code and the GPS coordinates (latitude and longitude) listed for it. Finally, get the current weather forecast for those coordinates (using weather-data forecast for 3 days) and also check if there are any active alerts for that park. Report: (1) the name of the park with the most campgrounds and how many campgrounds it has, (2) the park's official park code, (3) its GPS coordinates, (4) the 3-day weather forecast summary for those coordinates, and (5) any active park alerts.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: Find all national parks in Colorado
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Finding all national parks in Colorado...')
    raw1 = call_tool('national-parks_findParks', {'stateCode': 'CO', 'limit': 50})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    data1 = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    parks = data1.get('data', data1 if isinstance(data1, list) else [])
    execution_log.append(f'Total parks found in Colorado: {len(parks)}')
    park_codes = [p.get('parkCode', '') for p in parks if p.get('parkCode')]
    park_names = {p.get('parkCode', ''): p.get('fullName', p.get('name', 'Unknown')) for p in parks}
    execution_log.append(f'Park codes: {park_codes}')
    execution_log.append(f'Park names: {park_names}')

    # Step 2: Get campgrounds for each park and count them
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Getting campground counts for each park...')
    campground_counts = {}
    for code in park_codes:
        if not code:
            continue
        execution_log.append(f'  Fetching campgrounds for park: {code}')
        raw_c = call_tool('national-parks_getCampgrounds', {'parkCode': code, 'limit': 50})
        execution_log.append(f'  [RAW] {str(raw_c)[:150]}')
        data_c = json.loads(raw_c) if isinstance(raw_c, str) else raw_c
        cgrounds = data_c.get('data', data_c if isinstance(data_c, list) else [])
        count = len(cgrounds)
        campground_counts[code] = count
        execution_log.append(f'  Park {code} ({park_names.get(code, "?")}) has {count} campgrounds')

    execution_log.append(f'All campground counts: {campground_counts}')

    # Step 3: Identify the park with the most campgrounds
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Identifying park with most campgrounds...')
    parks_with_campgrounds = {k: v for k, v in campground_counts.items() if v > 0}
    if not parks_with_campgrounds:
        execution_log.append('No parks with campgrounds found!')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)
    top_park_code = max(parks_with_campgrounds, key=lambda k: parks_with_campgrounds[k])
    top_count = parks_with_campgrounds[top_park_code]
    top_name = park_names.get(top_park_code, 'Unknown')
    execution_log.append(f'Park with most campgrounds: {top_name} ({top_park_code}) with {top_count} campgrounds')
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Park code: {top_park_code}, Name: {top_name}, Count: {top_count}')

    # Step 4: Get full park details to extract GPS coordinates
    execution_log.append(f'Step 4: Getting full park details for {top_park_code}...')
    raw4 = call_tool('national-parks_getParkDetails', {'parkCode': top_park_code})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw4)[:200]}')
    data4 = json.loads(raw4) if isinstance(raw4, str) else raw4
    park_detail = data4.get('data', [{}])
    if isinstance(park_detail, list):
        park_detail = park_detail[0] if park_detail else {}
    lat = park_detail.get('latitude', '')
    lon = park_detail.get('longitude', '')
    official_code = park_detail.get('parkCode', top_park_code)
    full_name = park_detail.get('fullName', top_name)
    execution_log.append(f'Park full name: {full_name}')
    execution_log.append(f'Park code: {official_code}')
    execution_log.append(f'GPS coordinates: latitude={lat}, longitude={lon}')
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Coordinates: ({lat}, {lon})')

    # Step 5: Get 3-day weather forecast for the park coordinates
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Getting 3-day weather forecast for coordinates ({lat}, {lon})...')
    coord_query = f'{lat},{lon}'
    raw5 = call_tool('weather-data_weather_forecast', {'q': coord_query, 'days': 3})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}')
    data5 = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    forecast = data5.get('forecast', {})
    forecastdays = forecast.get('forecastday', [])
    forecast_summary = []
    for fd in forecastdays:
        date = fd.get('date', 'unknown')
        day_info = fd.get('day', {})
        condition = day_info.get('condition', {}).get('text', 'N/A')
        max_temp = day_info.get('maxtemp_f', day_info.get('maxtemp_c', 'N/A'))
        min_temp = day_info.get('mintemp_f', day_info.get('mintemp_c', 'N/A'))
        precip = day_info.get('daily_chance_of_rain', 'N/A')
        forecast_summary.append(f'{date}: {condition}, High={max_temp}F, Low={min_temp}F, Rain chance={precip}%')
    execution_log.append(f'3-day forecast summary:')
    for fs in forecast_summary:
        execution_log.append(f'  {fs}')

    # Step 6: Get active park alerts for the top park
    execution_log.append(f'Step 6: Getting active alerts for park {top_park_code}...')
    raw6 = call_tool('national-parks_getAlerts', {'parkCode': top_park_code, 'limit': 20})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw6)[:200]}')
    data6 = json.loads(raw6) if isinstance(raw6, str) else raw6
    alerts = data6.get('data', data6 if isinstance(data6, list) else [])
    execution_log.append(f'Number of active alerts: {len(alerts)}')
    for alert in alerts:
        alert_title = alert.get('title', 'No title')
        alert_cat = alert.get('category', 'Unknown')
        alert_desc = alert.get('description', '')[:150]
        execution_log.append(f'  Alert: [{alert_cat}] {alert_title} - {alert_desc}')

    # Final summary
    execution_log.append('\n=== FINAL ANSWER SUMMARY ===')
    execution_log.append(f'1. Park with most campgrounds: {full_name} with {top_count} campgrounds')
    execution_log.append(f'2. Official park code: {official_code}')
    execution_log.append(f'3. GPS coordinates: latitude={lat}, longitude={lon}')
    execution_log.append(f'4. 3-day weather forecast:')
    for fs in forecast_summary:
        execution_log.append(f'   {fs}')
    if alerts:
        execution_log.append(f'5. Active park alerts ({len(alerts)} total):')
        for alert in alerts:
            execution_log.append(f'   [{alert.get("category", "?")}] {alert.get("title", "?")}') 
    else:
        execution_log.append('5. No active park alerts found.')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Step 1 returns many parks with extensive metadata fields. Steps 2+ return campground records per park with dozens of fields each (amenities, fees, reservations, directions, weather info, accessibility info, images). The agent must aggregate campground counts across all parks — many parks will have 0 campgrounds and must be excluded. The GPS coordinates are buried within a large park details object alongside hundreds of other fields. The correct park code and coordinates from step 4 must be remembered and passed correctly to both the weather and alerts tools.

claims.description: Search for all national parks in Colorado using stateCode='CO' with limit=50. Returns a large list of parks with extensive metadata per park (descriptions, addresses, activities, images, fees, operating hours, contacts, etc.).
claims.output_characteristics: 10-20+ parks returned, each with 30+ fields of metadata, most irrelevant to the task

claims.description: For each park found in step 1, call getCampgrounds to retrieve its campgrounds. This requires multiple tool calls (one per park code). Most parks return 0-5 campgrounds; each campground record includes amenities, fees, GPS, reservation info, accessibility data, and more.
claims.output_characteristics: Multiple API calls, each returning 0-10+ campground objects with 40+ fields each. Noise includes fees, reservation URLs, accessibility details, weather info — all irrelevant. Must count campgrounds per park.
